issues.title: Multiple exceptions when working with db via sqlalchemy at load >200 req/sec
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code
This is how I create an application instance:
```python
import sentry_sdk
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.exceptions import RequestValidationError
from fastapi.responses import ORJSONResponse
from prometheus_fastapi_instrumentator import Instrumentator
from sentry_sdk.integrations.asgi import SentryAsgiMiddleware
from starlette.exceptions import HTTPException
from starlette.middleware.base import BaseHTTPMiddleware
from starlette.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware
from starlette.responses import Response
from starlette.status import HTTP_200_OK

from core.api.identity_management import routes as auth_routes
from core.api.v1_1 import v1_1_routes
from core.api.v2 import v2_routes
from core.app.config import config, EnvironmentEnum
from core.app.logging import init_logging
from core.utils.constants import responses
from core.utils.errors.handlers import ExceptionsHandler
from core.utils.middlewares.error_handling import error_handler, OUR_CUSTOM_ERROR_HANDLERS, error_handling
from core.utils.middlewares.log_requests import log_requests
from core.utils.middlewares.request_id import request_id_handling


def create_app() -> FastAPI:
    init_logging()

    app = FastAPI(
        title="My-API",
        description="The service provides an REST API for bla-bla-bla.",
        version="6.6.6",
        responses=responses,
        default_response_class=ORJSONResponse,
        swagger_ui_init_oauth={
            "usePkceWithAuthorizationCodeGrant": True,
            "clientId": config.APP_NAME.lower(),
        },
    )

    @app.get("/health", status_code=200, include_in_schema=False)
    def health_check() -> Response:
        return Response(status_code=HTTP_200_OK)

    app.include_router(auth_routes, tags=["identity_management"])
    app.include_router(v1_1_routes, tags=["v1.1"])
    app.include_router(v2_routes, tags=["v2"])

    app.exception_handlers[HTTPException] = error_handler(ExceptionsHandler(*OUR_CUSTOM_ERROR_HANDLERS))
    app.exception_handlers[RequestValidationError] = error_handler(ExceptionsHandler(*OUR_CUSTOM_ERROR_HANDLERS))

    sentry_sdk.init(environment=config.ENVIRONMENT, dsn=config.SENTRY_URL)
    app.add_middleware(SentryAsgiMiddleware)

    app.add_middleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware, dispatch=request_id_handling)
    app.add_middleware(
        BaseHTTPMiddleware,
        dispatch=error_handling(ExceptionsHandler(*COMMON_ERROR_HANDLERS)),
    )
    app.add_middleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware, dispatch=log_requests)
    app.add_middleware(
        CORSMiddleware,
        allow_origins=["foobar"],
        allow_credentials=True,
        allow_methods=["foobar"],
        allow_headers=["foobar"],
    )

    @app.on_event("startup")
    async def integrate_monitoring_plugin():
        Instrumentator(excluded_handlers=["/health", "/metrics"]).instrument(app).expose(app, include_in_schema=False)

    return app

```

Here's how I determine the connection to the database:
```python
...
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import create_async_engine, AsyncSession
from sqlalchemy.orm import sessionmaker

from src.app.config import config

engine = create_async_engine(
    config.POSTGRES_DSN,
    future=True,
    echo=True,
    pool_size=config.POSTGRES_MAX_CONNECTIONS,
    max_overflow=config.POSTGRES_CONNECTIONS_OVERFLOW,
    pool_pre_ping=True,
    pool_recycle=300,
    connect_args={"server_settings": {"application_name": f"{config.APP_NAME.lower()}[{config.ENVIRONMENT.lower()}]"}},
)
async_session = sessionmaker(bind=engine, class_=AsyncSession, expire_on_commit=False)


async def get_db() -> AsyncSession:
    async with async_session() as session, session.begin():
        yield session
```

here's my Users repository (i use repository pattern in my data layer) (Note that here I pass AsyncSession using fastapi's Depends feature):
```python
from fastapi import Depends
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession
from sqlalchemy.future import select

from .engine import get_db
from .models import User
from .dto_models import UserDTO, UserQueryFilters

class UsersRepo:
    """Repository class for work with user entities in PostgreSQL"""

    def __init__(self, session: AsyncSession):
        self.session: AsyncSession = session

    def convert_to_dto(self, obj: User, schema):
        return schema.from_orm(obj) if obj else None

    async def get_user(
        self,
        filters: UserQueryFilters,
    ) -> UserDTO | None:
        stmt = select(User)
        schema = UserDTO
        ...
        # adding more filters to query
        ...
        user: User = await self.session.scalar(stmt)

        return self.convert_to_dto(user, schema) if user else None


def get_users_repo(db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db)) -> UsersRepo:
    return UsersRepo(db)

```


here's the middlware dealing with authorization (Note that here I pass UsersRepo using fastapi's Depends feature):
```python
...

class Authenticate:
    async def __call__(
        self,
        encoded_access_token: str = Security(oauth2_scheme),
        idp_client: IDPService = Depends(get_idp_client),
        users_repo: UsersRepo = Depends(get_users_repo),
    ):
        self.idp_client = idp_client
        self.users_repo = users_repo

        decoded_access_token: t.Dict[str, t.Any] = self._validate_access_token(encoded_access_token)
        groups, permissions = await self._get_user_roles_permissions(encoded_access_token)

        user: UserDTO | None = await self._get_user_from_db(decoded_access_token["sub"])
        if not user:
            raise AccessDeniedError("User account was not found.")
        return UserIdentity(**user.dict(), groups=groups, permissions=permissions)

    @staticmethod
    def _validate_access_token(access_token: str):
        ...

    async def _get_user_roles_permissions(self, access_token: str):
        ...

    async def _get_user_from_db(self, user_uuid) -> UserDTO | None:
        """Get user from db by uuid"""
        user: UserDTO | None = await self.users_repo.get_user(UserQueryFilters(uuid=user_uuid))
        return user


authenticate = Authenticate()

```

here's how I use this middleware in the requests handler (Note that here I pass **authenticate** using fastapi's Depends feature):
```python
...
from middlewares.auth import UserIdentity, authenticate

routes: APIRouter = APIRouter()


@routes.get(
    "/devices",
    response_model=List[GetDevicesResp],
    summary="Get devices",
    description="Gives devices",
)
async def get_devices(
    ids: Set[NonNegativeInt] = Query(None, alias="id", description="devices IDs", example=[1, 12, 23]),
    name: str = Query(None, description="device name", example="Tablet"),
    use_case: GetDevicesUseCase = Depends(get_devices_usecase),
    user: UserIdentity = Depends(authenticate),
) -> List[GetDevicesResp]:
    req_obj: GetDevicesReq = GetDevicesReq(user=user, ids=ids, name=name)
    result: List[GetDevicesResp] = await use_case.execute(req_obj)
    return result
```

besides that, I also pass the db session into the use_case instance (note, in the example above, besides **authenticate** I also use Depends for **get_devices_usecase**):
here's **get_devices_usecase** in turn gets the db session, also using fastapi's Depends for the previously mentioned session generator **get_db**
```python
import typing as t
from logging import getLogger, Logger

from fastapi import Depends
from pydantic import NonNegativeInt, NoneStr
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession

from src.app.config import config
from src.data.postgresql.engine import get_db
from src.data.postgresql.repositories.devices import DevicesRepo
from src.data.postgresql.repositories.devices_dto import DevicesFilters, DevicesDTO
from src.use_cases.base import BaseUseCase
from src.utils.helpers.serialization_additions import OurBaseModel
from src.utils.middlewares.auth import UserIdentity

logger: Logger = getLogger(config.APP_NAME)


class GetDevicesReq(OurBaseModel):
    """Object of input data"""

    ids: t.Set[int] | None
    name: NoneStr
    user: UserIdentity


class GetDevicesResp(OurBaseModel):
    """Object of output data"""

    id: NonNegativeInt  # noqa A003
    name: str


class GetDevicesUseCase(BaseUseCase):
    def __init__(self, devices_repo: DevicesRepo):
        self.devices_repo: DevicesRepo = devices_repo

    async def execute(self, req: GetDevicesReq) -> t.List[GetDevicesResp]:
        """
        Implements the business logic of getting devices
        :param req: object with input data
        :return: devices
        """
        filters: DevicesFilters = DevicesFilters(ids=req.ids, name=req.name)
        devices: t.List[DevicesDTO] = await self.devices_repo.get_devices(filters)

        return [GetDevicesResp(**_.dict()) for _ in devices]


async def get_devices_usecase(db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db)) -> GetDevicesUseCase:
    """Input Boundary for GetDevicesUseCase"""
    return GetDevicesUseCase(devices_repo=DevicesRepo(db))
```

here's my Devices repository (Note that here I pass AsyncSession using fastapi's Depends feature):
```python
import logging
import typing as t

from fastapi import Depends
from sqlalchemy import or_, and_, desc
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession
from sqlalchemy.future import select
from sqlalchemy.orm import contains_eager
from sqlalchemy.sql.functions import count

from core.app.config import config
from core.data.postgresql.engine import get_db
from core.data.postgresql.models.devices import DeviceFormFactor, DeviceBrand, DeviceModel
from core.data.postgresql.repositories.devices_dto import (
    DevicesDTO,
    DevicesFilters,
    DevicesBrandsFilters,
    BaseDeviceBrandDTO,
    DeviceBrandDTO,
    DevicesModelsFilters,
    DevicesModelsDTO,
)

logger = logging.getLogger(config.APP_NAME)


class DevicesRepo:
    """Repository class for work with Devices"""

    def __init__(self, session: AsyncSession):
        self.session: AsyncSession = session

    def convert_to_dto(self, obj, schema):
        """
        Converts device entity to DTO
        :param obj: device entity
        :param schema: schema for convert to DTO
        :return: DTO of device entity
        """
        return schema.from_orm(obj)

    async def get_devices(self, filters: DevicesFilters) -> t.List[DevicesDTO]:
        """
        Returns the Devices
        :param filters: filters for query devices
        :return: list of devices entities
        """
        stmt = select(DeviceFormFactor).where(DeviceFormFactor.deleted_at.is_(None)).order_by(DeviceFormFactor.id)

        if ids := filters.ids:
            stmt = stmt.where(DeviceFormFactor.id.in_(ids))
        if name := filters.name:
            stmt = stmt.where(DeviceFormFactor.name.ilike(f"{name}%"))

        results = (await self.session.scalars(stmt)).all()

        return [self.convert_to_dto(_, DevicesDTO) for _ in results]

```

In this [Issue](https://github.com/MagicStack/asyncpg/issues/978), we discussed the above exceptions with the maintainers of [asyncpg](https://github.com/MagicStack/asyncpg) and [SQLAlchemy](https://github.com/sqlalchemy/sqlalchemy), and they gave their expert opinion that the problem is on the FastAPI side. 
According to my personal observations, this problem probably correlates with the load on the service - exceptions begin to occur at a load starting at 1000 requests per second.

**asyncpg**: 0.27.0
**PostgreSQL**: 11.4
**Do you use a PostgreSQL SaaS? If so, which?** : Nope
**Can you reproduce the issue with a local PostgreSQL install?**: Nope. It's very difficult because errors occur only under certain conditions under high load.
**Python**: 3.10.8
**Platform**: Linux
**Do you use pgbouncer?**: Yes, 1.10.0
**Did you install asyncpg with pip?**: Nope, via poetry
**If you built asyncpg locally, which version of Cython did you use?**: -
**Can the issue be reproduced under both asyncio and [uvloop](https://github.com/magicstack/uvloop)?**: I don't know. 
My service is based on **FastAPI** and runs under the hood on **AnyIO**. The app runs through **Uvicorn**, which uses **uvloop** under the hood.


### Operating System

Linux

### Operating System Details

Manjaro 22.0.0

### FastAPI Version

0.88.0

### Python Version

3.10.8

### Additional Context

The exceptions I listed above, due to peculiarities of the internal FastAPI implementation, as [specified](https://github.com/MagicStack/asyncpg/issues/978#issuecomment-1332395665) by @zzzeek, may be the cause of leak database connections.
In the graphs below, there is a consistent trend of more connections to postgresql. If we don't deploy the application for a long time, for example, after 3-4 weeks we were getting up to 160-200 connections for the same number of requests to the service.

The service is currently running in two containers, each containing 8 app workers running through [Gunicorn](https://docs.gunicorn.org/en/latest/index.html).

PostgreSQL connection statistics (the sharp dips here are the app deployments):
![connections_count](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/33978094/205426308-53a26d75-acaa-4aec-b5b6-46b1ad297728.png)

Connections status information from pg_stat_activity:
![pg_stat_activity](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/33978094/204846267-d3784f90-0c56-4206-b0e8-3211843ff88b.png)

Statistics of requests being processed:
![req_per_sec](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/33978094/205426326-08132d36-853b-4392-8a87-e65b989f1230.png)
![common_stat](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/33978094/205426330-1a974ba2-efb1-4d52-aadc-34a9a3e979bb.png)

comments.author: jgould22
comments.body:
So I have (as your linked issue alludes) encountered the two phase error when accidentally passing the async session over a task/thread boundary.

Having not looked at any of the FastAPI internals I wonder if your 
```
def get_users_repo(db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db)) -> UsersRepo:
```
Is causing the dependency framework to run that function in a thread pool which might cause problems (the docs seem to mention it would do that for a sub dep defined as `def` here https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/async/#sub-dependencies) 

Maybe you can test it by combining the `get_db` and `get_users_repo` into a single async function.

comments.author: zzzeek
comments.body:
> So I have (as your linked issue alludes) encountered the two phase error when accidentally passing the async session over a task/thread boundary.

that "two phase" error occurs as a common side effect of concurrent access to a Session.  it has nothing to do with two phase transactions, just that two threads/tasks/etc are modifying the session's state concurrently and it can't find one of its internal member variables.

in 2.0 we specifically addressed this exact error and all the others by adding a state transition assertion layer, described at https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/changelog/whatsnew_20.html#session-raises-proactively-when-illegal-concurrent-or-reentrant-access-is-detected .  it still will raise errors but the errors would hopefully be very clear why there's a problem.

comments.author: jgould22
comments.body:
Yeah, that page was what actually lead me to the solution of my original problem though I was on an older version of SQLA. I was passing the session to a task and using async gather. Even though, from what I could tell only that task had the session it was enough to trigger the error. Removing  the task from async gather solved the problem for me. 

All that said I am not too familiar with the FastAPI internals and how it chooses to run those dependencies (or cache them).

comments.author: erikwrede
comments.body:
Also getting the 
`InterfaceError: cannot use Connection.transaction() in a manually started transaction` error on certain deployments after switching a project from `aiosqlite:latest` to `asyncpg:latest`. I'm on `starlette==0.20.4`/`strawberry-graphql` with a single `uvicorn` worker on `gunicorn`.  
I'm using `SQLAlchemy==2.0.0b2`, but I'm not using `Depends` to create my sessions, just creating a session using `session = async_sessionmaker()` at the beginning of my request and closing it at the end using `session.close()`.

I cannot reproduce the error on my local machine although the dependencies, db config and invoked commands are exactly the same. Any ideas? I'm really stuck at a dead end and might have to revert the entire service back to sync sessions. The exact same DB doesn't yield any errors using the async driver when I query it using my local environment. The error on the deployment is sometimes randomly thrown right after startup even when it's the first request and no other requests have come in for multiple minutes.

comments.author: adriangb
comments.body:
> I am not too familiar with the FastAPI internals and how it chooses to run those dependencies

I can at least chime in on this:
- Async dependencies are all run in a single async task
  - If you use BaseHTTPMiddleware with context manager async tasks then `__aenter__` and `__aexit__` will run in different async tasks but within the same thread and event loop.
- Sync dependencies _always_ run in their own thread and for context manager dependencies`__exit__` runs in a different thread than `__enter__`
  - The same caveat with BaseHTTPMiddleware applies: if you use it not only with `__exit__` and `__enter__` run in different  threads, they'll actually run in different async tasks as well.

comments.author: alexted
comments.body: The fellow testers did a load test (they were querying multiple endpoints ~1500 times per second) and the exceptions were still being logged. I don't know how correct this test is, but the result is this.

comments.author: adriangb
comments.body: Fair enough! Since we don’t really know what issue you were encountering it’s completely possible that this PR does not resolve things for you at all.

issues.title: OAuth2 scopes error in documentation for endpoints with multiple scopes
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from typing import List, Union

from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI, HTTPException, Security, status
from fastapi.security import (
    OAuth2PasswordBearer,
    OAuth2PasswordRequestForm,
    SecurityScopes,
)
from jose import JWTError, jwt
from passlib.context import CryptContext
from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError

# to get a string like this run:
# openssl rand -hex 32
SECRET_KEY = "09d25e094faa6ca2556c818166b7a9563b93f7099f6f0f4caa6cf63b88e8d3e7"
ALGORITHM = "HS256"
ACCESS_TOKEN_EXPIRE_MINUTES = 30


fake_users_db = {
    "johndoe": {
        "username": "johndoe",
        "full_name": "John Doe",
        "email": "johndoe@example.com",
        "hashed_password": "$2b$12$Glui89L967h/y4cklxmhUu6SoFMSe7VbvpDtGZnf1NPQcw.7Krzve",  # secret
        "disabled": False,
    },
    "alice": {
        "username": "alice",
        "full_name": "Alice Chains",
        "email": "alicechains@example.com",
        "hashed_password": "$2b$12$E6I0U4U7v/EXMj/5iJvHE.NCk/u3K2ur5T6RgauCuItO5dBWdfT2q",  # secret
        "disabled": True,
    },
}


class Token(BaseModel):
    access_token: str
    token_type: str


class TokenData(BaseModel):
    username: Union[str, None] = None
    scopes: List[str] = []


class User(BaseModel):
    username: str
    email: Union[str, None] = None
    full_name: Union[str, None] = None
    disabled: Union[bool, None] = None


class UserInDB(User):
    hashed_password: str


pwd_context = CryptContext(schemes=["bcrypt"], deprecated="auto")

oauth2_scheme = OAuth2PasswordBearer(
    tokenUrl="token",
    scopes={"me": "Read information about the current user.",
            "items": "Read items.",
            "my-items": "Read items that belong to the current user."},
)

app = FastAPI()


def verify_password(plain_password, hashed_password):
    return pwd_context.verify(plain_password, hashed_password)


def get_password_hash(password):
    return pwd_context.hash(password)


def get_user(db, username: str):
    if username in db:
        user_dict = db[username]
        return UserInDB(**user_dict)


def authenticate_user(fake_db, username: str, password: str):
    user = get_user(fake_db, username)
    if not user:
        return False
    if not verify_password(password, user.hashed_password):
        return False
    return user


def create_access_token(data: dict, expires_delta: Union[timedelta, None] = None):
    to_encode = data.copy()
    if expires_delta:
        expire = datetime.utcnow() + expires_delta
    else:
        expire = datetime.utcnow() + timedelta(minutes=15)
    to_encode.update({"exp": expire})
    encoded_jwt = jwt.encode(to_encode, SECRET_KEY, algorithm=ALGORITHM)
    return encoded_jwt


async def get_current_user(
    security_scopes: SecurityScopes, token: str = Depends(oauth2_scheme)
):
    if security_scopes.scopes:
        authenticate_value = f'Bearer scope="{security_scopes.scope_str}"'
    else:
        authenticate_value = "Bearer"
    credentials_exception = HTTPException(
        status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,
        detail="Could not validate credentials",
        headers={"WWW-Authenticate": authenticate_value},
    )
    try:
        payload = jwt.decode(token, SECRET_KEY, algorithms=[ALGORITHM])
        username: str = payload.get("sub")
        if username is None:
            raise credentials_exception
        token_scopes = payload.get("scopes", [])
        token_data = TokenData(scopes=token_scopes, username=username)
    except (JWTError, ValidationError):
        raise credentials_exception
    user = get_user(fake_users_db, username=token_data.username)
    if user is None:
        raise credentials_exception
    for scope in security_scopes.scopes:
        if scope not in token_data.scopes:
            raise HTTPException(
                status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,
                detail="Not enough permissions",
                headers={"WWW-Authenticate": authenticate_value},
            )
    return user


async def get_current_active_user(
    current_user: User = Security(get_current_user, scopes=["me"])
):
    if current_user.disabled:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="Inactive user")
    return current_user


@app.post("/token", response_model=Token)
async def login_for_access_token(form_data: OAuth2PasswordRequestForm = Depends()):
    user = authenticate_user(fake_users_db, form_data.username, form_data.password)
    if not user:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="Incorrect username or password")
    access_token_expires = timedelta(minutes=ACCESS_TOKEN_EXPIRE_MINUTES)
    access_token = create_access_token(
        data={"sub": user.username, "scopes": form_data.scopes},
        expires_delta=access_token_expires,
    )
    return {"access_token": access_token, "token_type": "bearer"}


@app.get("/users/me/", response_model=User)
async def read_users_me(current_user: User = Depends(get_current_active_user)):
    return current_user


@app.get("/users/me/items/")
async def read_own_items(
    current_user: User = Security(get_current_active_user, scopes=["items", "my-items"])
):
    return [{"item_id": "Foo", "owner": current_user.username}]


@app.get("/status/")
async def read_system_status(current_user: User = Depends(get_current_user)):
    return {"status": "ok"}
```


### Description

This code come from https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/security/oauth2-scopes/ I've just added a scope `my-items` to the endpoint `/users/me/items/` to show the problem.

* Run with `uvicorn test:app` (name of the python file test.py)
* Go to http://127.0.0.1:8000/docs and login with `johndoe` / `secret` with scope `my-items`

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/25102234/205262434-b2f46fe8-b1fc-4fc4-98f6-fbce996ae125.png)

* Make a query on `/users/me/items/` which should answer since it accepts the scope `items` and `my-items`

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/25102234/205262808-0902393d-203f-4117-a3c5-fe834d1a6616.png)

We get "Not enough permission" back.

Indeed, if we look at the code :

```python
    for scope in security_scopes.scopes:
        if scope not in token_data.scopes:
            raise HTTPException(
                status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,
                detail="Not enough permissions",
                headers={"WWW-Authenticate": authenticate_value},
            )
```

It stops at the first iteration.

It would be better to put this in order to iterate on the whole list :

```python3 
    if security_scopes.scopes:
        check_scope = any(
            scope in token_data.scopes for scope in security_scopes.scopes
        )
    else:
        # If no security scopes are defined, return True
        check_scope = True

    if not check_scope:
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=status.HTTP_403_FORBIDDEN,
            detail=strings.NOT_ENOUGH_PERMISSIONS,
            headers={"WWW-Authenticate": authenticate_value},
        )
```


### Operating System

macOS

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.88.0

### Python Version

3.11

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: Duspy99
chichi13
comments.body: I forgot to update the code for an empty scope, it's done

issues.title: Using scopes on `Security()` changes dependency inheritance
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [x] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
async def get_authenticated_user(security_scopes: SecurityScopes,
                                 session: Session = Depends(get_session),
                                 token: str = Depends(oauth2_scheme),
                                 ):

    print(f"get_authenticated_user: {id(session)}")
    return

@router.post('/activate', status_code=204)
async def user_activate(session: Session = Depends(get_session),
                        user: UserAccountInDatabase = Security(
                            get_authenticated_user,
                            scopes=["activate"])):
    print(f"user_activate: {id(session)}")
    return
```


### Description

As you can see I have `scopes=["activate"]` defined in this `Security()` example.

The result will print:
```
get_authenticated_user: 140600933628496
user_activate: 140600933312848
```

As you can see the IDs of both sessions are different, this is unexpected to me as I was under the impression that arguments propagate through dependencies.

The weird thing is that when I remove `scopes=["activate"]` from `Security()` I do get the expected result:
```
get_authenticated_user: 139952151843088
user_activate: 139952151843088
```

I figured this out when I tried to commit a change in the `UserAccountInDatabase` object and sqlalchemy complained that the object does not belong to the same `Session`.

I'm wondering if this has anything to do with the instantiation of a SecurityScopes at some point by Security(). I tried manually adding the security scopes like this:
```python
activation_security_scope = SecurityScopes(["activate"])
@router.post('/activate', status_code=204)
async def user_activate(session: Session = Depends(get_session),
                        security_scopes: SecurityScopes = activation_security_scope,
                        user: UserAccountInDatabase = Depends(
                            get_authenticated_user)):
    print(f"user_activate: {id(session)}")
    print(security_scopes.scope_str)
```

But this actually will just show that `security_scopes` is empty, it almost seems like it's being overwritten somewhere (maybe by the `Depends(get_authenticated_user)`. 

As I'm new to FastAPI there's a big chance I'm misunderstanding something.

### Operating System

Linux

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.87.0

### Python Version

3.11.0

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: sehraramiz
comments.body:
[Dependant](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/63a5ffcf577abc6015d021f65857b3826d8db74e/fastapi/dependencies/models.py#L15) object has a cache_key attribute which the dependency resolver of fastapi uses it to cache dependencies and use the already resolved dependencies (get_session in this case).
according to [this line](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/63a5ffcf577abc6015d021f65857b3826d8db74e/fastapi/dependencies/models.py#L58) of Dependant class the cache_key is generated using the **dependency function reference** and **security scopes**.
when you use get_authenticated_user as a security dependency every param in get_authenticated_user gets the security scopes of the parent api (user_activate).
so when resloving dependencies from user_activate with scopes, the param ```session: Session = Depends(get_session)``` gets saved in dependency_cache with a key like ```(<function get_session at 0x7fb87827c9d0>, ('activate'))```
when resloving dependencies from user_activate with no scopes, the param ```session: Session = Depends(get_session)``` gets saved in dependency_cache with a key like ```(<function get_session at 0x7fb87827c9d0>, ())```

i think this behavior might be a security feature.

it might help you to read [this part of the doc](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/dependencies/sub-dependencies/?h=dependenc#using-the-same-dependency-multiple-times) on dependency cache
and [this part](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/security/oauth2-scopes/?h=dependency#dependency-tree-and-scopes) about dependency tree and scopes.

issues.title: Depends() not correctly generating openapi docs for list

comments.author: mlbase
comments.body:
@Adrian-at-CrimsonAuzre 
why are you using depends in pydantic model?
Depends function imply you will use cache memory for specific function or callable object. 
If you just want for typing and swagger-ui, remove it

comments.author: Adrian-at-CrimsonAzure
comments.body:
> @Adrian-at-CrimsonAuzre why are you using depends in pydantic model? 

Because using a class with `Depends()` is a [documented](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/dependencies/classes-as-dependencies/) way of adding query parameters? The use of Pydantic classes specifically is also [commonly](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/67580885/fastapi-query-parameter-using-pydantic-model) [recommended](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/62468402/query-parameters-from-pydantic-model) [on](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/67814772/how-to-write-schema-for-query-parameters-in-fastapi-as-variable) [Stack Overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/71177433/custom-pydantic-classes-as-fastapi-body-parameters). Abstraction of shared query parameters and the magic of Pydantic validators is nice. Plus it happens with also documented [method-based usage](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/dependencies/) of `Depends()`, so I'm reporting the bug.

comments.author: hellocoldworld
comments.body: Also running into this.

comments.author: EricKnowsCodeFu
comments.body:
Bump please, also encountering this.

For an additional example, I would expect these two methods to produce the same interface, but it results in a GET endpoint that expects a body.

```
from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI, Query
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import List, Optional

app = FastAPI()


class CommonQueryParams(BaseModel):
    stuff: Optional[List[str]] = Query(default=[])


@app.get('/using-model')
async def using_model(
        query: CommonQueryParams = Depends(CommonQueryParams)
):
    pass


@app.get('/using-func')
async def using_func(
        stuff: Optional[List[str]] = Query(default=[])
):
    pass
```

The parameters of `using-func` is what I would expect `using-model` to have as well.

issues.title: SQLAlchemy + FastAPI Routers
issues.body:
### Description

I am following the FastAPI integration guide with MySQL and it is working fine. The issue happens when I want to use the routers and want to divide the routes in multiple files. As of now, I have the route (which interacts with DB) in the main file but if I move this route to some other file say `routes.py` and import get_db function from the main file, it gives an error. 

Is there a way to use the DB function in all files just by importing?

``` 
# main.py

... imports....

def get_db:
    ......

@app.post("/users/", response_model=schemas.User)
def create_user(user: schemas.UserCreate, db: Session = Depends(get_db)):
    db_user = crud.get_user_by_email(db, email=user.email)
    if db_user:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="Email already registered")
    return crud.create_user(db=db, user=user)
```

```
# routes.py
from .main import get_db

@app.post("/users/", response_model=schemas.User)
def create_user(user: schemas.UserCreate, db: Session = Depends(get_db)):
    db_user = crud.get_user_by_email(db, email=user.email)
    if db_user:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="Email already registered")
    return crud.create_user(db=db, user=user)
```

Getting the following error:` ImportError: cannot import name 'get_db'`


### Operating System

macOS

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

Latest

### Python Version

3.10

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: mateoradman
comments.body:
I suggest you create a separate file or a folder where you can store your dependencies such as `get_db` function that you mentioned above. This allows you to import it like:
```python
from .dependencies import get_db
```
Please take a look at @tiangolo's example https://github.com/tiangolo/full-stack-fastapi-postgresql/tree/master/%7B%7Bcookiecutter.project_slug%7D%7D/backend/app/app/api. More specifically, the file deps.py contains dependencies that are widely used for all endpoints.

comments.author: rafsaf
comments.body:
It's more Python related than FastAPI related, you have a known and very common problem of circular imports in your code (most probably, because you wrote `... imports....` in main.py so I'm not 100% sure).

You can do as stated above (reasonable to move it to third file) or few other things.
There is great resource about this on RealPython:
https://realpython.com/python-import/#handle-cyclical-imports

You can read whole article as well.


As you are probably aware, there are thousands of open issues in FastAPI, if the comments answer your question, please close the issue, if they don't, don't hesitate to ask. Thanks 👍

comments.author: nhdinh
comments.body:
There is a thing in mind: if the module is generally imported for other modules to work with, then it should be in a place where every other module can see it and can import it.
In your case, the get_db should be moved into a separate module, and then, other modules which contain routers can import it to use. For example
```
from .db import get_db
```

comments.author: TheJumpyWizard
comments.body: @theinfosecguy I would do as everyone else so far has suggestd to resolve dependency issues. I would move the get_db function into a separate file/directory. Separation of responsibilities is important especially as your code base grows, it will be easier to maintain. Typically I would just handle the responses in the API functions and delegate the db operations and data transformations to handlers that you call from the API

comments.author: theinfosecguy
comments.body: Thanks for the help! The issue is now resolved (by moving function to a new file).

issues.title: Middleware to fingerprint incoming TCP/IP requests
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from tcp_fingerprint import TCPFingerprintMiddleware

fingerprint_dict = {}

app = FastAPI()

app.add_middleware(TCPFingerprintMiddleware,log_dict=fingerprint_dict)
```


### Description

Hello, I would like to implement a TCP fingerprinting middleware where incoming requests to the endpoints can be stored in a dictionary variable or a database connection (ex. SQL, Redis). The information would be used for profiling the requests made to the endpoint. I have found another project that achieves similar goals to what I am looking for, but it utilizes `libpcap` on Linux and isn't very friendly for integrating with FastAPI. Is there a way this can be implemented in FastAPI?

### Wanted Solution

A simple middleware that collects TCP fingerprints on the incoming API connections.

### Wanted Code

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from tcp_fingerprint import TCPFingerprintMiddleware

fingerprint_dict = {}

app = FastAPI()

app.add_middleware(TCPFingerprintMiddleware,log_dict=fingerprint_dict)
```


### Alternatives

_No response_

### Operating System

Linux

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.81.0

### Python Version

3.8.13

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
I'm not sure what's a TCP fingerprint. But if it depends on reading binary TCP data, you would probably need something that does it even before Uvicorn takes the request.

FastAPI receives ASGI data, already processed dicts with the information from the request. Uvicorn sends this data, reading HTTP (or WebSockets).

comments.author: jgould22
comments.body:
This would likely be difficult to do within the framework.

A better approach may be to write a tcp fingerprinting proxy, fingerprint your requests and save them where ever and then pass them on to your application.

comments.author: iudeen
comments.body:
Beauty of FastAPI / Starlette is self serviced Middlewares. That is, you could write your own middleware. 

Here are few things that would help us understand: 

1. Is starlette request or ASGI scope, receive and send objects have the necessary information you'd need to do TCP fingerprinting.

2. If it's not there above, then it must be done at Uvicorn (or ASGI) level as Sebastian suggests.

3. If it's available at ASGI level, you can write your own ASGI middleware. It can do the job.

Also, you mentioned it's available in other frameworks, can you give example links, I'll look into it in my free time.

comments.author: JohnnyRacer
comments.body: @tiangolo  @jgould22 I believe you are correct, what I wanted to do is a bit lower level than what ASGI or FastAPI should be handling. Since for the packet fingerprints to be generated, it really should be processed by something like `wireshark` or another packet capture/analysis software to monitor the incoming request packets. I should have clarified that the main goal of obtaining such fingerprints is mainly for the detection of spoofed browser user-agents and operating systems as well as headless browsers. Since differing browsers send their requests in slightly different ways, by analyzing the packets sent it is possible to establish a more accurate profile of the clients accessing the API than simply trusting the header information (which can be easily spoofed).

comments.author: JohnnyRacer
comments.body: @iudeen Sorry for not linking the existing project that I found above, it is [here named zardaxt](https://github.com/NikolaiT/zardaxt) if you are interested in taking a look at it. Although I've had issues with permissions with `libpcap` trying to run the code from the repo and I found it not very usable, it is also only compatible with Linux.

comments.author: iudeen
comments.body:
Thanks for sharing! I took a quick look into it, seems really interesting.  Having said that, What you are looking for is something to be done at lowest level, even before Uvicorn. I don't think a middleware will make sense. 

May be you can close this issue, if you agree with this being not in scope of FastAPI. 

>_I'll come back and add an update if I find something._

comments.author: JohnnyRacer
comments.body: @iudeen Thanks a lot, I appreciated you taking the time to look into it and please keep me posted on anything interesting! I will close this issue since I definitely agree with you that this is outside the scope of FastAPI.

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Thanks for the discussion and thanks for coming back and closing the issue! 🤓

Just dump of random thoughts, without checking much if it even makes sense or not...

Maybe you could have Traefik, handling the HTTPS stuff, and behind it Uvicorn and FastAPI.

Traefik now has a way to write plugins, I haven't used it but I know about it. Maybe you could use that to implement the logic necessary to do the TCP fingerprinting.

comments.body: Assuming the original need was handled, this will be automatically closed now. But feel free to add more comments or create new issues or PRs.

issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from datetime import datetime

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from fastapi.encoders import jsonable_encoder


class A(BaseModel):
    class Config:
        json_encoders = {datetime: lambda t: round(t.timestamp())}

    date: datetime = Field(default_factory=datetime.now)


class B(BaseModel):
    a: A = Field(default_factory=A)


a = A()
b = B()

# a.dict() and b.dict() both serialize A the same way
print(a.dict())
print(b.dict())

# jsonable_encoder does not serialize both the same way.
print(jsonable_encoder(a))
print(jsonable_encoder(b))
```


### Description

I expect `class A` to be serialized the same way by jsonable_encoder regardless of whether it is a nested property of a jsonified class or not.

### Operating System

macOS

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.87.0

### Python Version

3.10.6

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: kfreezen
comments.body: I'm taking a stab at a PR to fix this. Will update if/when I give up.

comments.author: kfreezen
comments.body: Working on fix in #5690

comments.author: azogue
comments.body:
Not sure if this is a bug or a feature 😅 (from `pydantic`!)

AFAIK, the `pydantic` `Config` affects to the class object where it's defined, is not related to the **fields**

And to compare the `jsonable_encoder`, the way to do it is with the `.json()` method of pydantic models, as the customisation is defined for it. 

```python
assert json.dumps(jsonable_encoder(a)) == a.json()
assert json.dumps(jsonable_encoder(b)) == b.json()
```
That is correct, so, ¿where is the issue?

To use the export customisation in another object, the `Config` should be defined in the other object too:

```python
class C(BaseModel):
    a: A = Field(default_factory=A)

    class Config:
        json_encoders = {datetime: lambda t: round(t.timestamp())}

c = C()
assert c.json() == json.dumps(jsonable_encoder(c))
assert isinstance(jsonable_encoder(c)["a"]["date"], int)
```

> a.dict() and b.dict() both serialize A the same way

`.dict()` is not affected with the customisation, as it's for `json_encoders`. It always generates a `datetime` value

> jsonable_encoder does not serialize both the same way.

Nor should do. But serialises _like_ `.json()`, so works as expected 👍

issues.title: Adding callable parameters to "Depends" & Adding a new "validator" parameter to "Query"
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.

- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
def validate_date(date: str):
    try:
        return datetime.strptime(date, "%Y-%m-%d").date
    except ValueError:
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=400,
            detail="Incorrect date format, should be YYYY-MM-DD",
        )   

@app.get("/endpoint")
async def handler(
    required_date: str = Depends(validate_date),
    optional_date: str | None = None
):
    return "hello"
```


### Description

I wanted to add a validator for the parameters "required_date" and "optional_date" but I must create two functions to handle that because one of them is optional.

### Wanted Solution

I would like to see an option in Depends() that let me pass parameters to that callable dependency.

### Wanted Code

```python
def validate_date(date: str, is_required: bool = True):
    if date is None:
        if is_required:
            raise HTTPException(400, f"{date=} is required.")
        return None
    try:
        return datetime.strptime(date, "%Y-%m-%d").date
    except ValueError:
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=400,
            detail="Incorrect date format, should be YYYY-MM-DD",
        )   

@app.get("/endpoint")
async def handler(
    required_date: str = Depends(validate_date),
    optional_date: str | None = Depends(validate_date, parameters={"is_required": False})
):
    return "hello"
```


### Alternatives

Handle input validators in some other cooler way.

### Operating System

Windows

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.85.2

### Python Version

Python 3.11.0

### Additional Context

Another suggestion.
Add "validator" parameter to Query() where it takes a callable to validate that value with the previous suggestion applied (callable parameters).

Sorry in advance if there is an existing approach to what I am trying to do.

comments.author: iudeen
comments.body:
You can use a Pydantic model to validate your Query params. Try that!

Callable to Depends is a good one!

comments.author: alfawal
comments.body:
I did use the pydantic models and validators at the end, but don't we need to apply the suggestion?
I am not sure if its suitable.

comments.author: TheJumpyWizard
comments.body: @alfawal I personally agree with @iudeen. I think using a Pydantic model to validate the Query params is a sufficient implementation to address your concern. Just to have a bit better of an understanding on your perspective, could you elaborate on why you don't think it's suitable?

comments.author: extreme4all
comments.body:
In the code you provided, the `EndpointRequest `class is a pydantic model that is used to define the data type, default value, and validation rules for the query parameters in the `handler `function.

The `EndpointRequest `class defines two fields, `required_date `and `optional_date`. This means that the `required_date `and `optional_date `fields will be set to the values of the `required_date `and `optional_date `query parameters, respectively, and pydantic will automatically validate the values of these parameters using the validation rules defined in the `EndpointRequest `class.

The `validate_required_date `method is a custom validator that is used to validate the values of the `required_date `and `optional_date `fields. This method attempts to convert the date string to a datetime.date object using the strptime method, and if the date string is not in the correct format it raises an HTTPException with a status code of 400 and a detailed error message that includes the invalid date value.

The `handler `function uses the `Depends `decorator to specify that it depends on an `EndpointRequest `object, which is automatically constructed by pydantic using the query parameters and validated using the `validate_required_date `method. This `EndpointRequest `object is then passed as an argument to the `handler `function, which returns it in the response.

```py
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, validator
from datetime import datetime
from fastapi import HTTPException, Query, Depends, FastAPI

app = FastAPI()

class EndpointRequest(BaseModel):
    required_date: str = Query(...) # required
    optional_date: str | None = Query() # optional

    @validator("required_date", "optional_date")
    def validate_required_date(cls, value, field):
        if value is None:
            return value
        try:
            value = datetime.strptime(value, "%Y-%m-%d").date()
        except ValueError:
            raise HTTPException(
                status_code=400,
                detail=f"Incorrect date format, '{value}' should be format YYYY-MM-DD",
            )  
        return value
		
@app.get("/endpoint")
async def handler(request: EndpointRequest = Depends()):
    print(request)
    return request
```
Source: 
https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/318#issuecomment-507043221
https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/dependencies/classes-as-dependencies/

comments.author: alfawal
comments.body: Thanks a lot, I did it using the pydantic classes as all suggested, although that I would love to see something like a callable param in the Query.

issues.title: Feature: The implementation of a class to manage cron tasks
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.

- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, CronTasks

from time import sleep

app = FastAPI()

@app.on_event("startup")
async def startup_event():
    await some_function()

async def some_function(cron_tasks: CronTasks):
    cron_tasks.add_task(function = other_function, parameters = 'Some parameters', repeat_time = 216000) # Time in seconds; 24h = 216000

async def other_function(parameters):
    print(parameters)
    sleep(3)
```


### Description

Call a cron tasks class in my controllers or events to set up repetitive actions.

### Wanted Solution

When I launch my project with uvicorn I would like to have some cron tasks, like background tasks but repeated over time instead of in the background.

### Wanted Code

```python
I haven't thought about the solution yet, but I could do it if it is interesting for the community.
```

Linux

3.10

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Thanks for the suggestion, but this would be out of the scope of FastAPI. Also, if you deploy several workers/containers they would end up duplicating the work, so it's probably not the best idea to do that on the same FastAPI app.

It would probably work better as a Celery component, or something in Kubernetes.

comments.author: OpetherMB
comments.body:
i would suggest that you use Celery, i think it's not a great idea for the FastAPI app to include such an overheat.
you can use workers that schedule a task and run after X time. i personnally use rq package it's lightweight lib that works with redis and you can duplicate your workers to run the task. there is also an async version of rq called arq by all means go and check them out. 
let me know if i can help

issues.title: Path(alias=) feature doesn't work if the alias has a hyphen in it
issues.body: ### First Check - [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue. - [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it. - [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search. - [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information. - [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer. - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic). - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui). - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc). ### Commit to Help - [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆 ### Example Code ```python This route, based on Closed Issue https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/131#issuecomment-1177187775, works, and shows how one can alias a path parameter. @router.get("/api/users/{id}/profile/") def user_profile(user_id: int = Path(None, alias="id")): return { "id": user_id, "username": "johndoe" } ``` This route, using `"user-id"` instead of `"id"` as the alias, does not work. ``` @router.get("/api/users1/{user-id}/profile/") def user_profile(user_id: int = Path(None, alias="user-id")): return { "id": user_id, "username": "johndoe" } ``` ``` ### Description Both `Query()` and `Path()` have an `alias` feature. `Query()` aliases can contain hyphens, but `Path()` aliases cannot. This appears to be a bug, as the [doc](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/path-params-numeric-validations/#declare-metadata) says about Path metadata > You can declare all the same parameters as for Query. ### Operating System Linux ### Operating System Details Amazon Linux 2 ### FastAPI Version 0.86.0 ### Python Version 3.9.13 ### Additional Context Swagger UI showing working route: Swagger UI showing hyphenated alias Not Found:

comments.body:
Just read that it is required. https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/3546#issuecomment-882055834

But the implementation in starlette does not seem to use it at all.

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body: It's used by Uvicorn IIRC.

comments.author: iudeen
comments.body:
@solarjoe, adding more to what Sebastian said above! Uvicorn, the ASGI server uses Websockets to support your implementation of Websocket routes. 

More info:
Alternatively, you can also use `wsproto` with Uvicorn. It can be set using `-ws` flag. Read more on that in Uvicorn's [documentation](https://www.uvicorn.org/settings/#implementation).


And finally, if this answers your query, it'd be great if you can close the issue! 😄

issues.title: How to use to use dynamic response_model when using SQLModel classes defined at runtime
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from typing import List, Optional
from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI, HTTPException, Query
from sqlmodel import Field, Relationship, Session, SQLModel, create_engine, select
from sqlalchemy.orm import registry

all_bases = {}

class BaseRepository:
    def __init__(self, db_filename):
        self.engine = create_engine(
            f"sqlite:///{db_filename}",
            echo=True
        )
        self.mapper_registry = registry()
        class SQLModel_custom(SQLModel, registry=self.mapper_registry):
            pass
        self.sqlmodel_subclass = SQLModel_custom
        self.sqlmodel_subclass.metadata = self.mapper_registry.metadata


def generate_models(base_repository: BaseRepository):
    class TeamBase(base_repository.sqlmodel_subclass):
        name: str = Field(index=True)
        headquarters: str

    class Team(TeamBase, table=True):
        id: Optional[int] = Field(default=None, primary_key=True)
        heroes: List["Hero"] = Relationship(back_populates="team")

    class TeamCreate(TeamBase):
        pass

    class TeamRead(TeamBase):
        id: int

    class HeroBase(base_repository.sqlmodel_subclass):
        name: str = Field(index=True)
        secret_name: str
        age: Optional[int] = Field(default=None, index=True)
        team_id: Optional[int] = Field(default=None, foreign_key="team.id")

    class Hero(HeroBase, table=True):
        id: Optional[int] = Field(default=None, primary_key=True)
        team: Optional["Team"] = Relationship(back_populates="heroes")

    class HeroRead(HeroBase):
        id: int

    class HeroCreate(HeroBase):
        pass

    class HeroReadWithTeam(HeroRead):
        team: Optional["TeamRead"] = None

    class TeamReadWithHeroes(TeamRead):
        heroes: List["HeroRead"] = []
        
    base_repository.TeamBase = TeamBase
    base_repository.Team = Team
    base_repository.TeamCreate = TeamCreate
    base_repository.TeamRead = TeamRead
    base_repository.HeroBase = HeroBase
    base_repository.Hero = Hero
    base_repository.HeroRead = HeroRead
    base_repository.HeroCreate = HeroCreate
    base_repository.HeroReadWithTeam = HeroReadWithTeam
    base_repository.TeamReadWithHeroes = TeamReadWithHeroes

def get_base_repository(basename):
    return all_bases[basename]

def create_db_and_tables():
    for base in all_bases.values():
        generate_models(base)
        base.sqlmodel_subclass.metadata.create_all(base.engine)

def get_session(basename):
    with Session(all_bases[basename].engine) as session:
        yield session
        
app = FastAPI()

all_bases['AAA'] = BaseRepository('./aaa.db')
all_bases['BBB'] = BaseRepository('./bbb.db')
# and so on ... (base names and path are not static)
        

@app.on_event("startup")
def on_startup():
    create_db_and_tables()

@app.get("/{basename}/heroes/") # I wish I could put something like 'List[base_repository.HeroRead]' here
def read_heroes(
    *,
    base_repository = Depends(get_base_repository)
    session: Session = Depends(get_session),
):
    heroes = session.exec(select(base_repository.Hero)).all()
    return heroes
    
    
@app.get("/{basename}/heroes/{hero_id}") # I wish I could put something like 'base_repository.HeroRead' here
def read_hero(
    *,
    base_repository = Depends(get_base_repository),
    session: Session = Depends(get_session),
    hero_id: int
):
    hero = session.get(Hero, hero_id)
    if not hero:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Hero not found")
    return hero
```


### Description

Hello !

(Note : I'm using SQLModel but I think my issue is more generally related to FastAPI and the way it works with response models).

I'm working on an API that is linked to several databases and in which SQLModel classes are dynamically generated at runtime for each database with their own registry/metadata/schema.
It works well (although I had a hard time understanding how to overcome the "sqlalchemy.exc.InvalidRequestError: Multiple classes found for path "xxx" in the registry of this declarative base. Please use a fully module-qualified path.". See this issue : https://github.com/tiangolo/sqlmodel/issues/264).

My MRE is based on the code from this tutorial page : https://sqlmodel.tiangolo.com/tutorial/fastapi/relationships/
I added code so that the models are dynamically generated, just like in my real usecase.
The problem is that, from what I understand, the integration between FastAPI and SQLModel relies on 'response_model' parameter is used to automatically "cast" the data output from the select() into the desired returned type. (In my example that would be cast 'List[Hero]' into 'List[HeroRead]' or 'Hero' into 'HeroReadWithTeam'). 

However, in my case, data models are defined at runtime and don't exist yet when the routes are defined. It is not possible to use Union types either. But maybe is there an alternative way to define this response_model within the route body ?

Otherwise, if the response_model can not be dynamically defined, an alternative solution could be reimplementing such a type-casting feature, which seems quite difficult though and not the smartest way of solving this issue ; maybe is it possible to directly call FastAPI underlying mechanism ?


### Operating System

Linux

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.86.0

### Python Version

3.9.5

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: gsouveton
tiangolo
comments.body: Thanks for coming back to close the issue! ☕

comments.author: gsouveton
comments.body:
Using 'from_orm' is indeed the right way of proceeding, but it introduces a new error :

`pydantic.errors.ConfigError: field "team" not yet prepared so type is still a ForwardRef, you might need to call HeroReadWithTeam.update_forward_refs().`

However, it is not possible to use 'update_forward_refs()' since it will look for a 'TeamRead' model that does not exist :

```
  File "pydantic/main.py", line 780, in pydantic.main.BaseModel.update_forward_refs
  File "pydantic/typing.py", line 480, in pydantic.typing.update_model_forward_refs
  File "pydantic/typing.py", line 450, in pydantic.typing.update_field_forward_refs
  File "pydantic/typing.py", line 63, in pydantic.typing.evaluate_forwardref
  File "/usr/lib/python3.9/typing.py", line 518, in _evaluate
    eval(self.__forward_code__, globalns, localns),
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
NameError: name 'TeamRead' is not defined
```

which seems logical since there's no such 'TeamRead' globally defined.
The solution is to use 'update_forward_refs()' right after all classes have been defined in 'generate_models' function, and provide it a mapping to the locally defined classes like so :

```
    def generate_models(base_repository: BaseRepository):
    
        class TeamBase(base_repository.sqlmodel_subclass):
            name: str = Field(index=True)
            headquarters: str

        class Team(TeamBase, table=True):
            id: Optional[int] = Field(default=None, primary_key=True)
            heroes: List["Hero"] = Relationship(back_populates="team")

        class TeamCreate(TeamBase):
            pass

        class TeamRead(TeamBase):
            id: int

        class HeroBase(base_repository.sqlmodel_subclass):
            name: str = Field(index=True)
            secret_name: str
            age: Optional[int] = Field(default=None, index=True)
            team_id: Optional[int] = Field(default=None, foreign_key="team.id")

        class Hero(HeroBase, table=True):
            id: Optional[int] = Field(default=None, primary_key=True)
            team: Optional["Team"] = Relationship(back_populates="heroes")

        class HeroRead(HeroBase):
            id: int

        class HeroCreate(HeroBase):
            pass

        class HeroReadWithTeam(HeroRead):
            team: Optional["TeamRead"] = None

        class TeamReadWithHeroes(TeamRead):
            heroes: List["HeroRead"] = []

        base_repository.TeamBase = TeamBase
        base_repository.Team = Team
        base_repository.TeamCreate = TeamCreate
        base_repository.TeamRead = TeamRead
        base_repository.HeroBase = HeroBase
        base_repository.Hero = Hero
        base_repository.HeroRead = HeroRead
        base_repository.HeroCreate = HeroCreate
        base_repository.HeroReadWithTeam = HeroReadWithTeam
        base_repository.TeamReadWithHeroes = TeamReadWithHeroes
        
        entities = {}
        entities['TeamBase'] = TeamBase
        entities['Team'] = Team
        # ...
         
        base_repository.TeamBase.update_forward_refs(**entities)
        base_repository.Team.update_forward_refs(**entities)
        # ...
```

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body: And does calling `update_forward_refs()` work?

comments.author: gsouveton
comments.body:
`update_forward_refs()` with no parameter does not work since it will look for globally defined names and classes defined in the repository local namespace aren't.
But by providing the mapping as parameter (either explicitly : `update_forward_refs(TeamBase=base_repository.TeamBase, Team=base_repository.Team, ...)` or with a dict mapping like I did in my example above) it works fine!

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body: Awesome! Thanks for reporting back. 🤓

issues.title: Adding a middleware causes background tasks cancellation
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
import asyncio
import time
from fastapi import BackgroundTasks, FastAPI

app = FastAPI()


@app.middleware("http")
async def pass_all_middleware(request, call_next):
    return await call_next(request)


async def test_bg():
    try:
        print("BG TASK STARTED")
        await asyncio.sleep(5)
        print("BG TASK ENDED")
    except BaseException as be:
        print("BG TASK NOT ENDED CORRECTLY", type(be))


@app.get("/hello")
async def root(background_tasks: BackgroundTasks):
    background_tasks.add_task(test_bg)
    return {"message": "hello"}
```


### Description

Executing the sample code causes an unexpected (and apparently unrelated) asyncio `CancelledError` on long running background tasks.

Sample output running on uvicorn and calling GET on `/hello` endpoint:

```text
INFO:     Started server process [95599]
INFO:     Waiting for application startup.
INFO:     Application startup complete.
INFO:     127.0.0.1:57617 - "GET /hello HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
BG TASK STARTED
BG TASK NOT ENDED CORRECTLY <class 'asyncio.exceptions.CancelledError'>
```

The cause seems to be the introduction of a middleware; when removing the middleware fragment everything get fine:

```text
INFO:     Started server process [96777]
INFO:     Waiting for application startup.
INFO:     Application startup complete.
INFO:     127.0.0.1:57674 - "GET /hello HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
BG TASK STARTED
BG TASK ENDED
```

### Operating System

macOS Ventura

### Operating System Details

Darwin ARM64 M1

### FastAPI Version

0.86.0

### Python Version

Python 3.10.8

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: odiseo0
comments.body:
I believe this is an issue with `BaseHTTPMiddleware` from Starlette

See [BaseHTTPMiddleware](https://www.starlette.io/middleware/#:~:text=to%20500.-,BaseHTTPMiddleware,-An%20abstract%20class) and this [issue](https://github.com/encode/starlette/issues/1438)

Possible solution:
Use a pure ASGI middleware

comments.author: csuriano23
comments.body: Thank you. It seems that the issue has been fixed in starlette 0.21, so I'm looking forward to have the release with this https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/5471

comments.author: csuriano23
comments.body: I think this can be closed after latest FastAPI release. Thank you

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Thanks for the help here @odiseo0 ! 👏  🙇 

Thanks for reporting back and closing the issue @csuriano23  👍

```python
class JSONError(JSONResponse):
    def __init__(self, content: Any, status_code: int = 503, headers: Optional[Dict[str, str]] = None, media_type: Optional[str] = None, background: Optional[BackgroundTask] = None) -> None:
        super().__init__(content, status_code, headers, media_type, background)

async def getlog(procuuid):
    proc = await create_subprocess_shell("tail -f /opt/wildfly/standalone/log/server.log", stdout=subprocess.PIPE, stderr=subprocess.PIPE)
    PROCCONTAINER.update({procuuid: proc})

    async for i in proc.stdout:
        if procuuid not in PROCCONTAINER:
            proc.terminate()
            return
        yield i.decode()

@router.post("/service/{getorkill}/{procuuid}", description="Stream WildFly Logs")
async def POST_getlog(getorkill: str, procuuid: str):
    if getorkill == "getlog":
        return StreamingResponse(getlog(procuuid), media_type="text/plain")
    elif getorkill == "killlog":
        proc = PROCCONTAINER[procuuid]
        proc.send_signal(2)
        del PROCCONTAINER[procuuid]
    else:
        return JSONError({"error": "Unknown action."})
```

Sure, i can rewrite code to call the original endpoint but i would like to avoid that as i would have to grant users full access to that port and it may cause security concerns as this will later be called from browser javascript.

It's not, i have masked true value since it contains authorization header.
I figured it out (eventually).

Problem is:
- When calling endpoint from http://{serverip}:3322/api/service/getlog/3e170885-c0e4-469a-bc73-c598d99e9de1 works fine since it directly reaches fastapi and so i get a valid StreamingResponse
- When calling /getwflogs that's supposed to fetch response from above endpoint it fails, and i receive whole response immidiately or upon httpx timeout - because that endpoint is behind nginx proxy. Configuring `proxy_buffering  off;` on nginx fixes this issue and i get a valid StreamingResponse. Neat :)

issues.title: Add Support for Pydantic models in multipart/form data requests.
issues.body:
### Example Code

```python
from typing import List, Type, Union, Any, Dict, Tuple, TypedDict

from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI
from fastapi import Form, UploadFile, File
from fastapi.openapi.utils import get_openapi
from pydantic import BaseModel
from pydantic import ValidationError
from starlette.responses import JSONResponse
from starlette.status import HTTP_422_UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY

Loc = Tuple[Union[int, str], ...]


class _ErrorDictRequired(TypedDict):
    loc: Loc
    msg: str
    type: str


class ErrorDict(_ErrorDictRequired, total=False):
    ctx: Dict[str, Any]


app = FastAPI()


class MultipleValidationErrors(Exception):
    def __init__(self, errors: List[ErrorDict]):
        super().__init__()
        self.errors = errors


class TestSchema(BaseModel):
    foo: str
    bar: int


def validate_json(model: Type[BaseModel]):
    def wrapper(body: str = Form(...)):
        try:
            return model.parse_raw(body)
        except ValidationError as exc:
            errors = exc.errors()
            for error in errors:
                error["loc"] = tuple(["body"] + list(error["loc"]))
            raise MultipleValidationErrors(errors)

    return wrapper


@app.post("/test")
def endpoint(file: UploadFile = File(...), body: TestSchema = Depends(validate_json(TestSchema))):
    print(file)
    print(body)
    return body


@app.exception_handler(MultipleValidationErrors)
def exception_handler(_request, exc: MultipleValidationErrors):
    return JSONResponse(status_code=HTTP_422_UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY, content={"detail": exc.errors})


def custom_openapi():
    if app.openapi_schema:
        return app.openapi_schema
    openapi_schema = get_openapi(title="Test App", version="v1", routes=app.routes)

    # Provide Schema for JSON body inside multipart/form type
    openapi_schema["components"]["schemas"]["Body_endpoint_test_post"]["properties"]["body"] = {
        "title": "Body",
        "type": "object",
        "$ref": "#/components/schemas/TestSchema",
    }
    openapi_schema["components"]["schemas"]["TestSchema"] = TestSchema.schema()

    app.openapi_schema = openapi_schema
    return app.openapi_schema


app.openapi = custom_openapi
```


### Description

The documentation at https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/request-forms-and-files states:

> You can declare multiple File and Form parameters in a path operation, but you can't also declare Body fields that you expect to receive as JSON, as the request will have the body encoded using multipart/form-data instead of application/json.

> This is not a limitation of FastAPI, it's part of the HTTP protocol.

This is true, but there is a workaround if you want to send data along with a file in a multipart/form request. You can send a string of JSON data along with the request, and both swagger-ui and redoc support this sort of openapi definition. For example, in the example code above, this is what I see in the documentation pages.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/32141163/199875889-2c564052-2421-4068-97f8-c86c0f590bc3.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/32141163/199875954-2c08d764-8442-4714-b0d8-9ef2a4fba1b4.png)

In the example, I had to manually change the openapi schema file in order to get this to work, but it is certainly supported at this time.

### Wanted Solution

I was hoping that FastAPI could natively support this approach to sending a JSON string that could be automatically documented and validated, as opposed to needing to go through these workarounds that I implemented in my example code.

As you can see in my example code below, this would offer a concise solution for anyone who want to accomplish this in their endpoints.

### Wanted Code

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi import File
from fastapi import FormObject  # New way to declare that you want an openapi object in the form
from fastapi import UploadFile
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()


class TestSchema(BaseModel):
    foo: str
    bar: int

@app.post("/test")
def endpoint(file: UploadFile = File(...), body: TestSchema = FormObject(...)):
    print(file)
    print(body)
    return body
```


### Alternatives

_No response_

### Operating System

Linux, Windows

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.74.1

### Python Version

Python 3.9.12

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.body:

So far it can only capture the string that I mentioned in the parameter. `async def index(command: str = Form()):` this will print command as it is. 

Now my question is how do I capture all of the incoming data into the class instance? 


```
# This class instance never works and it throws `INFO:     54.157.105.70:0 - "POST / HTTP/1.1" 422 Unprocessable Entity`
class Command(BaseModel):

# b'token=thisistoken&team_id=TEAMID213&team_domain=bwork&channel_id=IUHFF32HF&channel_name
```
