comments.author: iudeen
comments.body:
You can do this using both `dataclasses`
```python
from dataclasses import dataclass
from fastapi import FastAPI, Form, Depends, UploadFile
from starlette.responses import HTMLResponse

app = FastAPI()



@dataclass
class SimpleModel:
    no: int = Form(...)
    nm: str = Form(...)
    f: UploadFile = Form(...)


@app.post("/form")
def form_post(form_data: SimpleModel = Depends()):
    return form_data
```

or using Pydantic `BaseModel`

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Form, Depends, UploadFile
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()


class SimpleModel(BaseModel):
    no: int = Form(...)
    nm: str = Form(...)
    f: UploadFile = Form(...)


@app.post("/form")
async def form_post(form_data: SimpleModel = Depends()):
    fp = await form_data.f.read()
    print(fp)
```

issues.title: WSGIMiddleware is deprecated and will be removed in a future release
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.middleware.wsgi import WSGIMiddleware
from flask import Flask, escape, request

flask_app = Flask(__name__)


@flask_app.route("/")
def flask_main():
    name = request.args.get("name", "World")
    return f"Hello, {escape(name)} from Flask!"


app = FastAPI()


@app.get("/v2")
def read_main():
    return {"message": "Hello World"}


app.mount("/v1", WSGIMiddleware(flask_app))
```


### Description

* `fastapi.middleware.wsgi` is inherited from Starlette
* Starlette will remove `starlette.middleware.wsgi` in the future ([See here](https://github.com/encode/starlette/blob/master/starlette/middleware/wsgi.py#L11))
* `fastapi.middleware.wsgi` also has the deprecation and removal warning due to its import of  `WSGIMiddleware` ([See here](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/master/fastapi/middleware/wsgi.py#L1))
* It is unclear if FastAPI will continue to support `WSGIMiddleware` from `fastapi.middleware.wsgi` or whether it too will be removed in the future

How to solve
* Please clarify if `fastapi.middleware.wsgi` and / or `WSGIMiddleware` will continue to be supported or if it will be removed when Starlette removes it (which it will if no further action is taken)
* Update docs to reflect an upcoming removal (if it will be removed)
* Suppress deprecation warning from `starlette.middleware.wsgi` (if `wsgi.WSGIMiddleware` will not be removed from FastAPI)

* [x] I commit to helping implement these changes.

### Operating System

macOS

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.85.2

### Python Version

Python 3.11.0

### Additional Context

Thank you everyone for your hard work!

issues.title: null values are discarded from OpenAPI examples
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

Taken from https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/additional-responses/

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI

import json

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/", responses={200: {"content": {"application/json": {"example": {"absent": None, "present": "value"}}}}})
def read_root():
    return {"absent": None, "present": "value"}

print(json.dumps(app.openapi()))
```


### Description

- Add an example to the OpenAPI spec (using `example` or `examples`)
- Set the value to `None`
- The example is absent

The code above outputs:

```
{"openapi": "3.0.2", "info": {"title": "FastAPI", "version": "0.1.0"}, "paths": {"/": {"get": {"summary": "Read Root", "operationId": "read_root__get", "responses": {"200": {"description": "Successful Response", "content": {"application/json": {"schema": {}, "example": {"present": "value"}}}}}}}}}
```

As you can see, the `absent` example is absent.

### Operating System

Linux

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.73.0

### Python Version

3.8.9

### Additional Context

`openapi.utils.get_openapi` has this line:

    return jsonable_encoder(OpenAPI(**output), by_alias=True, exclude_none=True)  # type: ignore

`exclude_none` could cause the issue.

When setting it to True, OpenAPI generation fails with the following error:

    n is null

issues.title: Spec-first development with `openapi-generator`?
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code
I have the following OpenAPI spec
```yaml
openapi: 3.0.0
info:
  description: |
    Connector Builder Server API

  version: "1.0.0"
  title: Connector Builder Server API
  contact:
    email: myemail@gmail.com
  license:
    name: MIT
    url: "https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT"

paths:
  /hello_world:
    get:
      description: "Sample endpoint"
      responses:
        "200":
          description: Successful operation
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                type: object
                properties:
                  hello:
                    type: string
```

When I run `openapi-generator -i <path_to_spec> -g python-fastapi`, a file `src/openapi/apis/default_api.py` (among other things) is autogenerated containing the following code:

```python
router = APIRouter()

@router.get(
    "/hello_world",
    responses={
        200: {"model": InlineResponse200, "description": "Successful operation"},
    },
    tags=["default"],
)
async def hello_world_get(
) -> InlineResponse200:
    """Sample endpoint"""
    ...
```


### My Question
How is a developer using this generator expected to implement and maintain these endpoints on an ongoing basis as the spec evolves over time? In other words, is there any way to: 
1. override the behavior of these methods from outside this file (so my implementation logic doesn't get overwritten when I regenerate the file) , and
2. detect if an API method in the spec was not implemented by the developer, and raise an exception during CI or tests

If this was say, Java, the endpoints would be encapsulated under an interface like: 


```java
// DefaultAPI.java
// This interface is autogenerated 
interface DefaultAPI() {
  InlineResponse200 helloWorld();
}
```

Then in a separate file I'd implement the API 
```java
// DefaultApiImplementation.java
class DefaultApiImplementation implements DefaultAPI {
  InlineResponse200 helloWorld() {
    // ... 
  }
}
```

If a new method is added to the spec or an existing method is updated, I would run the generator and it would update `DefaultAPI.java` with any new parameters, return types, API endpoints, etc... And because the implementation (which I create by hand) implements that interface, the build will fail if one of the signatures doesn't correctly match or if an endpoint is not implemented. 

Is the FastAPI OpenAPI Generator supposed to be used in this way? If so, how could I achieve my goals 1 & 2 above with the current output created by the generator? 



### Operating System

macOS

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.85.1

### Python Version

3.9.11

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: iudeen
comments.body: It's perhaps a question to be asked on the generator side.

comments.author: sherifnada
comments.body: @iudeen fair enough, I asked the question there as well. I asked here since it wasn't clear to me who owns the OpenAPI Generator templates for FastAPI in that repo, so I thought I'd ask the community here since the ownership is more clearly defined.

comments.author: sherifnada
comments.body: Added an implementation suggestion in this issue https://github.com/OpenAPITools/openapi-generator/issues/13863 would love any feedback or opinions

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
FastAPI is actually not intended for you to work on the schema first. The design ideas and intention are more that you would write the code in Python, and that FastAPI makes it so easy to just use standard type annotations and minimal code that it would be easier to maintain that Python code than the spec.

In that case, anything that uses the spec (e.g. docs, generated clients) would use the spec generated by FastAPI.

The code generators would make more sense when you already have a spec and you need to migrate to FastAPI. But all that probably makes less sense if you want to keep updating the schema before the code.

issues.title: Duplicate tags ending up in openapi.json, causing ReDoc issue [fixed, with PR]
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from fastapi import APIRouter, FastAPI, Request
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse

app = FastAPI()
router = APIRouter(tags=["items", "items"])

@router.get("/items/")
def read_items(request: Request):
    return JSONResponse({"hello": "world"})

app.include_router(router)

# or just simply
@app.get('/test/', tags=["test", "test"])
def read_test():
    return "test"
```


### Description

* Open the browser and call endpoint '/redoc'
* Observe duplicate entries for /items and /test:

![Screenshot 2022-10-24 at 11 23 31](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/58346970/197496996-4aba111c-8438-4d6e-b0c5-e27b1977f7b5.png)


This is caused by treating tags as a List of strings or enum inside APIRouter.
The root cause is duplicate tags ending up in openapi.json:

![Screenshot 2022-10-24 at 11 22 35](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/58346970/197497176-b4201979-7b44-4459-aeec-287a855fd913.png)

Using Class Based Views in fastapi-utils trigger this bug (external package).

Added a fix for the issue in PR #5533 (with test)

### Operating System

macOS

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.85.1

### Python Version

Python 3.10.8

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body:
Just answering here as well (copy paste from PR comment):

If you provide the same tag twice, it ends up twice in the openapi.json. That doesn't seem like that is wrong behaviour at all, nor isn't it against the spec (it is [against the spec](https://swagger.io/specification/#openapi-object) to list the same tag twice in the openapi document tags key). That said, SwaggerUI plays nicely along (as it should) and only redocs seems to have an issue with it. I would argue this need to be solved in Redoc, not in FastAPI.

comments.author: aradipe
comments.body:
@JarroVGIT Thanks for taking a look and sorry for the late reply!
I've checked the openapi specs and indeed, only the global tags are restricted to be unique. To be perfectly honest I can't think of a usecase where you would want to specify duplicate tags for an API operation ("Operation Object" in spec), but that is irrelevant here.
I will probably report the tag duplication with CBV at [fastapi-utils](https://github.com/dmontagu/fastapi-utils/) as I don't think it is intentional and also an issue with ReDoc.
In the meantime I'll double-check whether FastApi allows for global (OpenAPIObject) tag duplication as that would indeed violate the spec.

comments.author: aradipe
comments.body:
> In the meantime I'll double-check whether FastApi allows for global (OpenAPIObject) tag duplication as that would indeed violate the spec.
Yes, it does, I've opened issue #5552 and I'm closing this and PR #5533.
Thanks!

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Thanks for the help here @JarroVGIT ! ☕

And thanks @aradipe for coming back to close the issue. 🍰

issues.title: Testing using pydantic models

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
If I wanted to reuse the models for tests I would do it the same way as you. I wouldn't want something that in some way "skips" passing the JSON data, because that's the main part, the core of the web API. I would like my tests to still handle JSON data and then ensure that it's correct.

Also, for tests, I prefer the code as simple and dumb as possible. In many cases that even means duplicating some code. But if the complexity in the logic to test the app grows a bit, I would feel that would become "testable" code. And I don't want to write tests for the testing code. I like it to be as simple and straightforward as possible. Unless there's a particular reason why I could need something a bit more complex.

from fastapi import FastAPI
from config.ConfigLoader import ConfigLoader # Here ConfigLoader is singleton class

app = FastAPI()

APP_VERSION = "v1"

app.include_router(SegmentRoute.router)

@app.on_event("startup")
async def startup_event():

# some other file
ConfigLoader().get_instance() # this code is been called first before fastAPI's startup event
```


### Description

Start the app with `uvicorn main:app --reload`

I want to create a single object of my singleton class from FastAPI's startup event. But the similar code i wrote in some other `py` file which should return created object (object which should be created from startup event).

But the problem is, code to get instance from other file is been called before startup event function of FastAPI. So my code is failing saying that class is singleton. (I have added exception if someone try to create 2nd object)

Any fix for such situation?

comments.author: JarroVGIT

It seems it is created with different arguments in your code, what is the envisioned behaviour of that design choice?

comments.author: Dustyposa

issues.title: Receiving NULL when using fastapi with flutterflow
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
#this section will be for providing an API for the application

#system imports
import os
import sys
import psycopg2
import pandas as pd
import datetime as dt
import uvicorn 
from typing import Union
import fastapi 
from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends, HTTPException
from fastapi.security import OAuth2PasswordBearer
from fastapi.encoders import jsonable_encoder
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
import json
from starlette import status
from pydantic import BaseModel
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

#the os path to the folder to the ocular-git master folder
dir_path = os.getcwd()
#the sub path to the parent folder that contains the modules to import
sub_path = '\\ocular\\database\\connection_info\\'
sys.path.append(dir_path + sub_path)
path = sys.path.append(dir_path + sub_path)

#module/package imports. example " from 'module_name' import 'function_name' " within the parent folder

from config import config

#display this programs file name and that it has been started
print(os.path.basename(__file__) , ' has started')

#logic variables
logic_name = 'API Execution Logic'

#function to perform a read query on the database
def read_postgresql_conn(name,query):
    conn = None
    try:
        params = config()
        conn = psycopg2.connect(**params)
        cur = conn.cursor()
        print("|logic| " + logic_name + " |executed query| " + query)   
        df = pd.read_sql(query, conn)   
        return df
        cur.close()
    except (Exception, psycopg2.DatabaseError) as error:
        print(error)
    finally:
        if conn is not None:
            conn.close()

#function to perform a write query on the database
def write_postgresql_conn(name,query):
    conn = None
    try:
        # read connection parameters
        params = config()
        conn = psycopg2.connect(**params)
        cur = conn.cursor()
        print("|logic| " + logic_name + " |executed query| " + query)   
        df = cur.execute(query, conn)
        conn.commit()
        cur.close()
    except (Exception, psycopg2.DatabaseError) as error:
        print(error)
    finally:
        if conn is not None:
            conn.close()

#SECURITY AUTHENTICATION LOGIC------------------------------------------------------------

# Use token based authentication
oauth2_scheme = OAuth2PasswordBearer(tokenUrl="token")

# Ensure the request is authenticated
def auth_request(token: str = Depends(oauth2_scheme)) -> bool:
    authenticated = token == os.getenv("API_KEY", "dummy-api-key")
    return authenticated

#END OF SECURITY AUTHENTICATION LOGIC------------------------------------------------------------

app = FastAPI()

#THIS IS THE SIMPLEST API THAT I CREATED FOR TROUBLESHOOTING. IT ALSO WORKS WITH POSTMAN BUT NOT FLUTTERFLOW

@app.get("/hello", status_code=status.HTTP_200_OK, dependencies=[Depends(auth_request)])
async def hello():
    return {"message": "Hello World"}
    

#THE FOLLOW APIS ARE THE ONES THAT WORK WITH POSTMAN BUT NOT FLUTTERFLOW - THESE ARE RELATED TO MY APPLICATION

@app.get("/newestcard")
async def newestcard(authenticated: bool = Depends(auth_request)):
    # Check for authentication like so
    if not authenticated:
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,
            detail="Not authenticated")

    # query to retrieve the newest card
    retrieve_newest_card_query = 'SELECT * FROM public."API_Card_Data_View" ORDER BY "timestamp" DESC LIMIT 1'

    #call the function to query the database for the latest card to analyze
    new_card_results = read_postgresql_conn(logic_name,retrieve_newest_card_query)

    #return the results of the query as a dictionary
    return new_card_results.to_dict(orient='records')

#retrieve cards from the database based on a range of row numbers------------------------------------------------------------
@app.post("/cardrange")
async def cardrange(offset: int, limit: int, authenticated: bool = Depends(auth_request)):
    # Check for authentication like so
    if not authenticated:
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,
            detail="Not authenticated")

    # query to retrieve the newest card. Limit is the amount of cards and offset is the starting point 0 based. App must
    # track how many cards are currently queried and where it last left off
    retrieve_card_range_query = 'SELECT * FROM public."API_Card_Data_View" ORDER BY "timestamp" DESC LIMIT ' + str(limit) + ' OFFSET ' + str(offset)

    #call the function to query the database for the latest card to analyze
    card_range_results = read_postgresql_conn(logic_name,retrieve_card_range_query)

    #return the results of the query as a dictionary
    return card_range_results.to_dict(orient='records')



#retrieve the user setpoints from the database------------------------------------------------------------

@app.get("/readsetpoints")
async def setpoints(authenticated: bool = Depends(auth_request)):
    # Check for authentication like so
    if not authenticated:
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,
            detail="Not authenticated")

    # query to retrieve the newest card
    retrieve_setpoints_query = 'SELECT * FROM public."user_control_and_setpoints"'

    #call the function to query the database for the latest card to analyze
    setpoints_results = read_postgresql_conn(logic_name,retrieve_setpoints_query)

    #return the results with a dictionary
    return setpoints_results.to_dict('records')

#update the user setpoints in the database using post user input data with two parameters--this is used for manual shutdown and reset/ack
@app.post("/updatesetpoints")
async def setpoints(value: float, column: str, authenticated: bool = Depends(auth_request)):
    # Check for authentication like so
    if not authenticated:
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,
            detail="Not authenticated")

    # query to update the setpoints
    update_setpoints_query = 'UPDATE public."user_control_and_setpoints" SET ' + column + ' = ' + str(value)

    #call the function to query the database for the latest card to analyze
    setpoints_results = write_postgresql_conn(logic_name,update_setpoints_query)

    #return the results with a dictionary
    return 'setpoint updated!'



#launch unvicorn to run the fastapi application on localhost with reload enabled
if __name__ == "__main__":
    uvicorn.run(app, host="localhost", port=80)
```


### Description

I am seeing an odd issue that i can't seem to track down. When i use postman I receive status code 200 and can retrieve data with api bearer token logic functioning correctly. However, when i attempt to use the flutterflow to retrieve the same data i receive a status code 200 and NULL data results. FYI, to get my localhost Fastapi outside of my network i have been using ngrok and i have verified that everything functions correctly with postman while using the ngrok external https address. I also have noticed that removing the bearer token from flutterflow still results in a status code 200. I watched the incoming traffic to uvicorn and noticed that the server is not seeing any requests when i test the flutterflow logic. i'm unsure if there is an issue on the flutterflow side or if ngrok is doing something strange with the traffic, but i'm leaning towards flutterflow since postman works perfectly with ngrok. I realize the questions here are going to be "You mention flutterflow 10 times....have you talked to them?". And yes i have a request into them as well, but i would like to get a second opinion on my logic to rule out any issues on my side while i wait to hear back from them. 

### Operating System

Windows

### Operating System Details

Python version:  3.10.7 (tags/v3.10.7:6cc6b13, Sep  5 2022, 14:08:36) [MSC v.1933 64 bit (AMD64)]
Pandas version:  1.4.3  
FastAPI version:  0.84.0
Uvicorn version:  0.18.3

Windows 10 with VSC

### FastAPI Version

FastAPI version:  0.84.0

### Python Version

Python version:  3.10.7

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: TDoggin
comments.body: well to anyone stumbling across this thread with the same issue. I was able to resolve it...or it resolved itself. The only thing that has happened between now and posting this comment was i registered for the pro plan with flutterflow and paid for ngrok pro. I then used a custom domain with ngrok instead of the one provided by ngrok when using the free version. So my custom domain was "www.mydomain.api.ngrok.io" rather than the cryptic "www.ngrok.23-25-23-52-6a-3g.io". Once i did this it suddenly started working. I honestly do not know why but i have a theory that flutterflows servers only validate domains that are alpha characters and that ngroks complex temporary URL names mess up the flutterflow server. Wish i had a better explaination......i hope this helps someone out there.

comments.body: Thanks for closing the issue 👍

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI()

# …doesn’t matter
```


### Description

Is there a way to use an existing OpenAPI-definition? I saw that the OpenAPI-generator provides a Beta-Option to generate FastAPI-stubs, but that seems to be a little backwards, given the close relation between FastAPI and OpenAPI.

Using an additional API-definition is more time consuming from a usage point of view, however it adds the following benefits:

- Contract driven development
- Easier type safety
- Not needing a running OpenAPI-app to re-generate the Frontend-API

comments.author: chbndrhnns
comments.body: Are you looking for something like [connexion](https://connexion.readthedocs.io/)?

comments.author: tim-hilt
comments.body:
Looks like it, yes. I don’t want to rely solely on the Python Code but on an external definition.

Connexion is not ready for FastAPI, right? https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/2942

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
The spirit in FastAPI is different, the intention is that the code would be the starting point, using type annotations, to get autocompletion, inline errors, linters, tooling to check type correctness and bugs, etc.

If you don't need/want those features, you can approach it the other way and start from building the spec by hand and then using that from code. But in that case you would be looking for something else, not FastAPI.

If you already have a spec and want to migrate to FastAPI (probably switching the process and making FastAPI the starting point) you could use https://koxudaxi.github.io/fastapi-code-generator/

issues.title: FastAPI return internal server error when response model validation fails
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from fastapi import Body, Depends, FastAPI, HTTPException, Path, Query, Request
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, ValidationError
from pprint import pprint as pp

app = FastAPI()


class MetadataOut(BaseModel):
    """
    Model for Metadata
    """

    region: str = Field(
        ...,
        title="region",
        description="region - one of dc00|dc01|dc02",
        regex=r"^(dc00|dc01|dc02)$",
    )


@app.get("/api/v1/metadata/fail", response_model=MetadataOut)
def metadatafail():
    return {"region": "whoops"}


@app.get("/api/v1/metadata/failwitherror", response_model=MetadataOut)
def metadatafailwitherror():
    try:
        return MetadataOut(region="whoops")
    except ValidationError as e:
        pp(e)
        raise HTTPException(status_code=422, detail=e.errors())


@app.get("/api/v1/metadata/pass", response_model=MetadataOut)
def metadatapass():
    return {"region": "dc01"}


if __name__ == "__main__":
    uvicorn.run(
        app,
        host="0.0.0.0",
        port=8080,
    )
```


### Description

I am asking this as a question because I am hoping that I just need to adjust my code a bit.

I am expecting when the response validation fails fastapi would return a 422 error and the pydantic descriptive error just like input works.

The only way I have gotten a half way decent return is to do what you see in the /api/v1/metadata/failwitherror endpoint.

0.74.1

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body:
This behaviour is intentionally and explained here in the docs:

https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/handling-errors/#requestvalidationerror-vs-validationerror

TLDR: if your response cannot be casted as the pydantic model you've declared, that is considered a logic flaw and hence a 500 is thrown.

comments.author: JoeyG1973
comments.body: ok cool thank you

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body:
To be complete: a 422 is basically saying that the entity as provided by the client is unprocessable; so it's the clients fault. Rendering a response and incurring an error while doing that, is by definition a server error, and thus it will produce a HTTP 500. 

The way you've caught and handled the exception is an option (like in `metadatafailwitherror`) but it does violate the HTTP response codes (or actually, misuses them). That is up to you of course! 

If your response model is dependant on an input, you might want to cast it your self in a try/except block, rather than having FastAPI doing it by means of `response_model=`, and then return the object itself. 

If this answers your question, please close the issue.

comments.author: JoeyG1973
comments.body: @JarroVGIT unfortunately without the response_model= the swagger interface doesn't self-document the return.

issues.title: `RequestValidationError` is raised when testing, instead of returing `422` response
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
import uuid

from fastapi import FastAPI, APIRouter, Header, HTTPException
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
from pydantic import BaseModel


# Models and CRUD
class Item(BaseModel):
    id: int
    content: str


def crud_get_items():
    # This function lies in a separate module
    return [
        Item(id=1, content="First item"),
        Item(id=2, content="Second item"),
    ]


# App
app = APIRouter()


@app.get("/")
async def get_items(user_id: str = Header()):
    data = crud_get_items()
    if not len(data):  # This is redundant in the example, but exist in "normal" codebase
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="No items found")

    return data


# Tests
client = TestClient(app)


def test_get_items():
    headers = {"user-id": str(uuid.uuid4())}
    res = client.get("/", headers=headers)
    assert res.status_code == 200


def test_get_items_without_userid():
    res = client.get("/")
    assert res.status_code == 422
```


### Description

When testing my app I noticed, that sending request to router without header parameter set, I get `RequestValidationError` instead of `422` status code. **This doesn't happen, when using `FastAPI` instead of `APIRouter`**.

One could say, that an easy fix to this is to wrap the second test code in `pytest.raises()`, but this will only execute the `client.get` while skipping all asserts the test has.
```py
from fastapi.exceptions import RequestValidationError

def test_get_items_without_userid():
    with pytest.raises(RequestValidationError):
        res = client.get("/")          # This line will execute
        assert res.status_code == 422  # This line won't (and any below) 
```

### Operating System

Windows

### Operating System Details

Windows 10, 21H2

### FastAPI Version

0.85.0

### Python Version

Python 3.10.4

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: iudeen
comments.body:
Why are you not initializing the app?

Like `app = FastAPI()`?

comments.author: jkazimierczak
comments.body:
In production code I'm using it. The API itself is mounted under `/api`, as I'm serving static files from "/".

So to avoid the need to write `/api/items/<anything that comes after that>` I thought that maybe the TestClient has some way to set the base (`/api/items`) for each request. I tried passing `base_url` and `root_path` like so:
```py
# One at the time, of course
client = TestClient(app, base_url="http://testserver/api")
client = TestClient(app, root_path="/api")
```
but this didn't change anything. So to avoid repetitive writing, testing the router itself seemed like a good solution.

If routers aren't meant to be tested directly, how one can shorten request base path (in my case `/api/items`)?

comments.author: jkazimierczak
comments.body:
Now when I wrote this, I thought of solution - initialize app per test file, like so:
```py
# all imports here

app = FastAPI()
app.include_router(router)  # router I want to test
client = TestClient(app)

# tests below
```

Then it works correctly. But at this point I want to know if `APIRouter` is untestable on it's own by design?

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body:
The `TestClient` is created to test `Starlette` applications, and by extension you can use it to test `FastAPI` applications. It is not designed to test Starlette's `Router` or FastAPI's `APIRouter` directly. As you have experienced, it can yield unexpected results. Most notably: The difference between sending a request to a `FastAPI` application and sending a request to the `APIRouter`  directly (using it as an ASGI application on its own), is that no middleware is executed when doing the latter.

And by default (and design), there are always at least 2 middleware present in your FastAPI application (which will not be present in your `APIRouter` instance: one that catches and handles exceptions like `HTTPException` and one (the very last one) that handles any uncaught exception and returns a `HTTP 500 Internal Server Error` to ensure your server keeps running. 

So, when using `APIRouter` directly in your `TestClient`, any raised `HTTPException`s will not result in a proper response, rather than actually raised (and unhandled) exception.

So, to answer your question:
> But at this point I want to know if APIRouter is untestable on it's own by design?

The behaviour that you are experiencing is by design, and when testing an `APIRouter` one should keep in mind that `FastAPI + APIRouter` has different behaviour than just the `APIRouter` as an ASGI application on its own.

comments.author: jkazimierczak
comments.body: Thank you for very through explanation of the differences between `FastAPI` and `APIRouter`. This is very helpful and insightful. It makes sense now why I was was getting exceptions and not the responses with correct status codes.

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Thanks for the help here @JarroVGIT ! 👏  You rock!  🙇 

Thanks for reporting back and closing the issue @jkazimierczak  👍

issues.title: Allow creating advanced custom route classes
issues.body:
(Duplicate of #5390)

### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
# N/A
```


### Description

I'd like to be able to implement a multimedia route that can optimally handle not only an entire e.g. msgpacked payload, but individual fields in the payload.

For example, if some models include `bytes` fields, for optimal performance, you want those fields to be (de)serialized only once, i.e. msgpack/base64 to `bytes` or `bytes` to msgpack/base64, instead of, e.g., msgpack to base64 to `bytes`.

### Wanted Solution

Building on top of the contributions of @dmontagu, @sandys, and @jcsaaddupuy in #521, in particular [this comment](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/521#issuecomment-646989249), I'd like for `get_route_handler()` to be able to pass a `custom_encoder` to `get_request_handler()`, and for `get_request_handler()` to be able to pass `custom_encoder` to `serialize_response()`.

By being able to pass a custom msgpack encoder to `get_request_handler()`, you could suppress base64-encoding of custom e.g. `Base64Bytes` fields by overriding their encoders with `lambda x: x` identity passthroughs, while utilizing the existing `jsonable_encoder()` and `custom_encoder` mechanisms, without resorting to any hackery such as dynamically patching models' `.__config__.json_encoders`.

### Wanted Code

```python
# Copy-paste from tests/test_advanced_custom_route_class.py
# in https://github.com/natskvi/fastapi/tree/multimedia-route-support
# Requires a few more tests before it is PR-ready.

from collections import defaultdict
import msgpack
from typing import Any, Callable, Dict, Final
import pytest
import base64
import fastapi
from fastapi import APIRouter, FastAPI, status
from fastapi.routing import APIRoute, get_request_handler
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
from pydantic import BaseModel
from starlette.requests import Request
from starlette.responses import Response
from starlette.routing import Route

app = FastAPI()


ACCEPT: Final = "accept"
CONTENT_TYPE: Final = "content-type"
JSON: Final = "application/json"
MSGPACK: Final = "application/x-msgpack"


class Base64Bytes(bytes):
    """Defines a Pydantic custom field type for deserializing base64
    string to bytes."""

    @classmethod
    def __get_validators__(cls):
        yield cls.validate

    @classmethod
    def validate(cls, value) -> "Base64Bytes":
        if isinstance(value, str):
            value = base64.b64decode(value)
        return cls(value)


def base64_encode_as_str(data: bytes) -> str:
    """base64-encode binary data as ASCII string"""
    return base64.b64encode(data).decode('ascii')


custom_json_encoder = {
    Base64Bytes: base64_encode_as_str,  # Overrides Pydantic default `lambda o: o.decode()`
}


_catchall_encoder_saved = fastapi.encoders.catchall_encoder
# fastapi.encoders.catchall_encoder callable is applied by default to obj
# (if obj is not None) after the logic that handles primitives, dicts, lists,
# tuples, Pydantic models, etc. (applying models'  __config__.json_encoders,
# custom encoders, pydantic.json.ENCODERS_BY_TYPE).
fastapi.encoders.catchall_encoder = str


# Override default encoders for raw bytes fields to skip base64-encoding
# when using msgpack to serialize response payloads.
msgpack_encoder_override = defaultdict(
    lambda: str,
    {
        Base64Bytes: lambda x: x,  # Overrides custom_json_encoder's `base64_encode_as_str`
    },
)


class MsgpackRequest(Request):
    """Msgpack request class, used only if the request headers include
    'Content-Type: application/x-msgpack'."""

    media_type = MSGPACK

    async def body(self) -> bytes:
        if not hasattr(self, "_body"):
            body: Final = await super().body()
            if body:
                # fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/custom-request-and-route/#handling-custom-request-body-encodings
                self._body = msgpack.unpackb(body)
        return self._body


class MsgpackResponse(Response):
    """Msgpack response class, used only if the request headers include
    'Accept: application/x-msgpack'."""

    media_type = MSGPACK

    def render(self, content: Any) -> bytes:
        return msgpack.packb(content)


class MultimediaRouteMixin(APIRoute):
    """A route "mixin" that handles 'application/x-msgpack' in addition to
    'application/json'. Its generic design simplifies adding support for more
    mime types. (Hence, a more accurate name for this class would be
    `MultiMimeTypeRouteMixin`.)
    Credit:
    https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/521#issuecomment-646989249"""

    REQUEST_CLASSES: Final = {
        MsgpackRequest.media_type: MsgpackRequest,
    }

    RESPONSE_CLASSES: Final = {
        MsgpackResponse.media_type: MsgpackResponse,
    }

    def _get_multimedia_request_handler(self, mime_type):
        # Use custom response class or fall back on default self.response_class.
        response_class = self.RESPONSE_CLASSES.get(mime_type, self.response_class)
        # Use msgpack override encoder for msgpack responses.
        custom_encoder = msgpack_encoder_override if mime_type == MSGPACK else None

        return get_request_handler(
            dependant=self.dependant,
            body_field=self.body_field,
            status_code=self.status_code,
            response_class=response_class,  # Based on mime_type (see above)
            response_field=self.secure_cloned_response_field,
            response_model_include=self.response_model_include,
            response_model_exclude=self.response_model_exclude,
            response_model_by_alias=self.response_model_by_alias,
            response_model_exclude_unset=self.response_model_exclude_unset,
            response_model_exclude_defaults=self.response_model_exclude_defaults,
            response_model_exclude_none=self.response_model_exclude_none,
            dependency_overrides_provider=self.dependency_overrides_provider,
            custom_encoder=custom_encoder,  # Based on mime_type (see above)
        )

    def get_route_handler(self) -> Callable:
        """Overrides APIRoute.get_route_handler()"""

        async def multimedia_route_handler(request: Request) -> Response:

            content_type_headers = request.headers.get("Content-Type")
            try:
                request_cls = self.REQUEST_CLASSES[content_type_headers]
                request = request_cls(request.scope, request.receive)
            except KeyError:
                pass  # No handler for content_type, process request as-is.

            accept_headers = request.headers.get("Accept")
            route_handler = self._get_multimedia_request_handler(accept_headers)
            return await route_handler(request)

        return multimedia_route_handler


class ModelConfig:
    json_encoders = custom_json_encoder
    validate_assignment = True


class ModelWithBytes(BaseModel):
    text: str
    raw: Base64Bytes

    class Config(ModelConfig):
        pass


TEXT: Final ="text"
BYTES: Final = b"\x16\r\xd4)\x93>(\x04\x83\xcc\xc5\xd6"


test_dict = {
    "text": TEXT,
    "raw": BYTES,
}

base64_encoded_test_dict = {
    "text": TEXT,
    "raw": base64_encode_as_str(BYTES),
}


router = APIRouter(route_class=MultimediaRouteMixin)


@router.post("")
def post_test_model(body: ModelWithBytes) -> ModelWithBytes:
    assert body == ModelWithBytes(text=TEXT, raw=BYTES)
    assert ModelConfig.json_encoders[Base64Bytes] is base64_encode_as_str
    # Verify jsonable_encoder did not mutate body
    # TODO: assert Base64Bytes not in body.__config__.json_encoders
    return body


ENDPOINT_PATH: Final = "/test_multimedia"

app.include_router(router=router, prefix=ENDPOINT_PATH)

client = TestClient(app)


def test_multimedia_route():
    """Test multimedia "middleware" (route mixin) for a simple payload
    that includes raw bytes for two combinations of  'Accept' and
    'Content-Type' HTTP headers."""

    # Test JSON-to-JSON POST
    for headers in (
        {},
        {ACCEPT: JSON},
        {CONTENT_TYPE: JSON},
        {ACCEPT: JSON, CONTENT_TYPE: JSON},
    ):
        resp = client.post(
            ENDPOINT_PATH,
            json=base64_encoded_test_dict,
            headers=headers,
        )
        assert resp.ok
        result = resp.json()
        assert resp.status_code == status.HTTP_200_OK
        assert result == base64_encoded_test_dict

    # Test JSON-to-msgpack POST
    for headers in (
        {ACCEPT: MSGPACK},
        {ACCEPT: MSGPACK, CONTENT_TYPE: JSON},
    ):
        resp = client.post(
            ENDPOINT_PATH,
            headers=headers,
            json=base64_encoded_test_dict,
        )
        assert resp.ok
        assert resp.status_code == status.HTTP_200_OK
        result = msgpack.unpackb(resp.content)
        assert result == test_dict

    # Test msgpack-to-JSON POST
    for headers in (
        {CONTENT_TYPE: MSGPACK},
        {ACCEPT: JSON, CONTENT_TYPE: MSGPACK},
    ):
        resp = client.post(
            ENDPOINT_PATH,
            headers=headers,
            data=msgpack.packb(test_dict),
        )
        assert resp.ok
        result = resp.json()
        assert resp.status_code == status.HTTP_200_OK
        assert result == base64_encoded_test_dict

    # Test msgpack-to-msgpack POST
    for headers in (
        {ACCEPT: MSGPACK, CONTENT_TYPE: MSGPACK},
    ):
        resp = client.post(
            ENDPOINT_PATH,
            headers=headers,
            data=msgpack.packb(test_dict),
        )
        assert resp.ok
        assert resp.status_code == status.HTTP_200_OK
        result = msgpack.unpackb(resp.content)
        assert result == test_dict

    # Test for error when sending JSON but expecting msgpack
    resp = client.post(
        ENDPOINT_PATH,
        headers={CONTENT_TYPE: MSGPACK},
        json=base64_encoded_test_dict,
    )
    assert not resp.ok
    assert resp.status_code == status.HTTP_400_BAD_REQUEST

    # Test for error when sending msgpack but expecting JSON
    resp = client.post(
        ENDPOINT_PATH,
        headers={CONTENT_TYPE: JSON},
        data=msgpack.packb(test_dict),
    )
    assert not resp.ok
    assert resp.status_code == status.HTTP_400_BAD_REQUEST


# Restore pre-test state
fastapi.encoders.catchall_encoder = _catchall_encoder_saved
```


### Alternatives

_No response_

### Operating System

Linux

### Operating System Details

Ubuntu 20.04.5 LTS

### FastAPI Version

0.83.0

### Python Version

3.8.12

### Additional Context

* closes #5389
* closes #5390
* #521
* #2117
* #2901

issues.title: "request.json()" hangs indefinitely in middleware
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, Response
from starlette.middleware.base import BaseHTTPMiddleware, RequestResponseEndpoint


class Middleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware):

    async def dispatch(self, request: Request, call_next: RequestResponseEndpoint) -> Response:
        await request.json()
        response = await call_next(request)
        return response


app = FastAPI()
app.add_middleware(Middleware)


@app.post("/test")
async def test(test: dict) -> dict:
    return {"data": "test"}
```


### Description

- Run script with: `uvicorn main:app --reload`
- Run CURL with POST request: `curl -X 'POST'   'http://localhost:8000/test'   -H 'accept: application/json'   -H 'Content-Type: application/json'   -d '{"test": "test"}'`

The application keeps hanging indefinitely and don't send a response to the request. I tested the equivalent code using `app = Starlette()` and it answers correctly.

### Operating System

Linux

### Operating System Details

Ubuntu 22.04

### FastAPI Version

0.79.0

### Python Version

Python 3.10.4

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: virgesmith
comments.body: I have exactly the same problem on fastapi 0.81.0, python 3.10.6 ubuntu 22.04

comments.author: csrgxtu
comments.body:
you should remove line 8 code:
```python
await request.json()
```
it is not this code pending your execution, it's following code pending your execution:

```python
@app.post("/test")  # here pending your code
async def test(test: dict) -> dict:
    return {"data": "test"}
```
when fastapi run your test func, it tries to fill the params from request.body, but you already read the body out from stream, thus later read will pending. ref following fastapi code:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5053620/190113608-a09d2a69-3a43-43be-91f4-0f277fad2379.png)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5053620/190114235-009a47ca-4f21-492c-bb92-3a119a2e6bf3.png)

comments.author: virgesmith
comments.body: In my case I actually want to read the json in the middleware (for HMAC validation) before forwarding to the endpoint, is this possible?

comments.author: leo-aa88
comments.body: @csrgxtu I need to read the body in JSON format in several middleware in order to process the data for various reasons (logging, security, sanitizing, etc.) so I wonder what the correct implementation would be in order to avoid halting the program?

comments.author: iudeen
comments.body:
In case you want to do this, you need to iterate `re.stream()`. 

I would rather suggest writing a pure ASGI middleware instead.

comments.author: csrgxtu
comments.body:
@araujo88 you can ref this issue: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/394

in summary it is a bug or design issue from starlette, and there is work around:

```python
from typing import Mapping

from starlette.requests import Request

from fastapi import FastAPI, APIRouter, Depends

app = FastAPI()
api_router = APIRouter()

@api_router.post("/")
def read_root(arg: Mapping[str, str]):
    return {"Hello": "World"}

async def auth_middleware(request: Request):
    # you can implement your auth here
    print(await request.json())

# the trick here is including auth_middleware in the dependencies:
app.include_router(api_router, dependencies=[Depends(auth_middleware)])
```
thus this middleware logic will work on every requests, we use it same way in our production env, works fine.

note: `Dependencies` will inject your auth logic into each request, for more detail ref: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/dependencies/

comments.author: leo-aa88
comments.body: Is it possible to stream a request multiple times in FastAPI using `self.stream()`? I tried various approches, like copying the `Request` object, but none of them worked.

comments.author: xingdongzhe
comments.body:
@araujo88 It can't be using `self.stream()`, for per request, only one time can use `self.stream()`.  
Or you will hang forever because of `await self.event_message.wait()`  
[You can see here: uvicorn/protocols/http/h11_impl.py#L543](https://github.com/encode/uvicorn/blob/fedf67e68d3f81c846bf3740c8ae21fa133000a4/uvicorn/protocols/http/h11_impl.py#L543)  
This is because that when `self.stream()` done, there are no action to notify `self.event_message`
This `asyncio.Event` will be notified of four actions:
- when Connection Lost
- when body_data received
- when body_data readed done
- when write reponse data done

And you can try this:
```
async for chunk in request.stream():
    print(chunk)
await request.json()
```

comments.author: leo-aa88
comments.body: @XingDongZhe I'm trying to process the request body through different middleware for different purposes (such as logging, sanitizing, etc.) however once the `request.stream()` has been called once by the first middleware, the next middleware keeps awaiting for the request due to the way `request.stream()` is implemented. What would be the correct approach for this? I've seen workarounds such as implementing a new middleware class instead of using `BaseHTTPMiddleware` but that seems too far-off to me.

comments.author: iudeen
comments.body: If you want to keep using BaseHTTPMiddleware, I would suggest you create a new Request object at the end of your middleware to avoid such indefinite waits.

comments.body: Hi all.

comments.author: mbroton
comments.body:
Hi! As described in documentation:
[Request Forms and Files](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/request-forms-and-files)

> You can declare multiple File and Form parameters in a path operation, but you can't also declare Body fields that you expect to receive as JSON, as the request will have the body encoded using multipart/form-data instead of application/json.
> 
> This is not a limitation of FastAPI, it's part of the HTTP protocol.

The workaround for this would be to send JSON as a string and parse it on your own. But I'm not sure if it's recommended.

Hi, I did see this in the docs but I was not certain of if that applied as in the same bit of docs it describes using Form() for JSON? Or maybe I am misunderstanding?

comments.author: iudeen
comments.body:
The only limitation here is you will not be able to have nested JSONs within form.

```python
from enum import Enum
from typing import Type
import inspect

from fastapi import (
    FastAPI,
    File,
    UploadFile,
    Form, Depends,
)
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

app = FastAPI()

def as_form(cls: Type[BaseModel]):
    new_parameters = []

    for field_name, model_field in cls.__fields__.items():
        model_field: ModelField  # type: ignore

        new_parameters.append(
             inspect.Parameter(
                 model_field.alias,
                 inspect.Parameter.POSITIONAL_ONLY,
                 default=Form(...) if not model_field.required else Form(model_field.default),
                 annotation=model_field.outer_type_,
             )
         )

    async def as_form_func(**data):
        return cls(**data)

    sig = inspect.signature(as_form_func)
    sig = sig.replace(parameters=new_parameters)
    as_form_func.__signature__ = sig  # type: ignore
    setattr(cls, 'as_form', as_form_func)
    return cls

# Models
class ModelProcessor(str, Enum):
    CPU = "cpu"
    GPU = "gpu"
    TPU = "tpu"
    DEFAULT = CPU


class ModelThresholds(BaseModel):
    confidence: float = Field(0.5, ge=0.0, le=1.0, descritpiton="Confidence Threshold")
    nms: float = Field(
        0.4, ge=0.0, le=1.0, description="Non-Maximum Suppression Threshold"
    )

class ModelOptions(BaseModel):
    processor: ModelProcessor = Form(None, description="Processor to use for model")
    height: int = Form(
        416, ge=1, description="Height of the input image (resized for model)"
    )
    width: int = Form(
        416, ge=1, description="Width of the input image (resized for model)"
    )
    square: bool = Form(False, description="Zero pad the image to be a square")
    fp_16: bool = Form(False, description="model uses Floating Point 16 Backend (EXPERIMENTAL!)")

    # thresholds: ModelThresholds = Form(...)



@app.post("/detect/{model_uuid}", summary="Run detection on an image")
async def object_detection(
        model_uuid: str,
        model_options: ModelOptions = Depends(),
        image: UploadFile = File(..., description="Image to run the ML model on"),
):
    print(f"Running detection on image '{image.filename}'")
    return {"filename": image.filename}


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import uvicorn

    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=5001)
```

In the above code, I just commented out `thresholds` and changed how you have formed the `BaseModel`

comments.author: mbroton
comments.body:
Examples in documentation have `str` type of `Form`. You've used `ModelOptions`.

You can use @iudeen's solution, but you can't nest your models.
Or send JSON as a string if you have to nest:
```py3
@app.post("/detect/{model_uuid}", summary="Run detection on an image")
async def object_detection(
        model_uuid: str,
        json_as_str: Json = Form(),  # from pydantic import Json
        image: UploadFile = File(..., description="Image to run the ML model on"),
):
    print(f"Running detection on image '{image.filename}'")
    return {"filename": image.filename}
```
which will result in this:

Something like that.

I haven't tested it, but I guess you can do something like:
```py3
@app.post("/detect/{model_uuid}", summary="Run detection on an image")

        model_uuid: str,

comments.author: iudeen
comments.body:
@baudneo one last attempt to get as close as you want this to be. This handles everything.

```python
import json
from enum import Enum
from typing import Type, Dict, Optional
import inspect

from fastapi import (
    FastAPI,
    File,
    UploadFile,
    Form, Depends,
)

from dataclasses import dataclass, field

from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()


# Models
class ModelProcessor(str, Enum):
    CPU = "cpu"
    GPU = "gpu"
    TPU = "tpu"
    DEFAULT = CPU

class ModelThresholds(BaseModel):
    confidence: float = Form(0.5, ge=0.0, le=1.0, descritpiton="Confidence Threshold")
    nms: float = Form(
        0.4, ge=0.0, le=1.0, description="Non-Maximum Suppression Threshold"
    )

@dataclass
class ModelOptions:
    processor: ModelProcessor = Form(None, description="Processor to use for model")
    height: int = Form(
        416, ge=1, description="Height of the input image (resized for model)"
    )
    width: int = Form(
        416, ge=1, description="Width of the input image (resized for model)"
    )
    square: bool = Form(False, description="Zero pad the image to be a square")
    fp_16: bool = Form(False, description="model uses Floating Point 16 Backend (EXPERIMENTAL!)")

    thresholds: Optional[str] = Form(ModelThresholds().json())


@app.post("/detect/{model_uuid}", summary="Run detection on an image")
async def object_detection(
        model_uuid: str,
        model_options: ModelOptions = Depends(),
        image: UploadFile = File(..., description="Image to run the ML model on"),
):
    threshold = ModelThresholds(**json.loads(model_options.thresholds))
    print(threshold.dict())
    print(f"Running detection on image '{image.filename}'")
    return {"filename": image.filename}


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import uvicorn

    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=5001)
```

issues.title: pytest routing needs explicit prefix on requests, when prefix is set in APIRouter. Works fine when prefix is added in FastAPI.include_router()
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
from fastapi import FastAPI, APIRouter


app = FastAPI(
    title='PYTEST PREFIX'
)

router_with_prefix = APIRouter(prefix='/prefix_in_router')
router_without_prefix = APIRouter()


@router_with_prefix.get('/test')
async def router_with_prefix_path_op():
    return 'PREFIX IN APIROUTER', 200


@router_without_prefix.get('/test')
async def router_without_prefix_path_op():
    return 'PREFIX IN INCLUDE ROUTER', 200

app.include_router(
    router_with_prefix
)

app.include_router(
    router_without_prefix,
    prefix='/prefix_in_include'
)


def test_prefix_in_include_router():
    client = TestClient(router_without_prefix)
    response = client.get("/test")
    assert 'PREFIX IN INCLUDE ROUTER' in response.text
    assert response.status_code == 200


def test_prefix_in_router():
    client = TestClient(router_with_prefix)
    response = client.get("/test")
    assert 'PREFIX IN APIROUTER' in response.text
    assert response.status_code == 200
```


### Description

#When running pytest on `FastAPI` app instance with routers, the expected behaviour was to instantiate a `TestClient` with the router relative path, and have it working independently if the prefix has been set in `APIRouter()` or in `FastAPI.include_router()`. This behaviour seems to be connected to how `APIRouter` appends routes in `routing.py`. While instantiating the `APIRouter` withouth `prefix`, `route_class(path)` receives the "relative" path, and only appends the `prefix` on the route after `include_router` is called, with the `prefix`, whereas instantiating a APIRouter with prefix, it has no "relative path", and therefore, the full path must be called inside the request url.

Tested locally removing the `self.prefix` from `APIRouter.add_api_router` `path` argument and the tests passed. This caused a problem with the docs, because now the full path is not referenced anymore. To fix this, added the `router.prefix` to `self.add_api_route` `path` argument, so the full path is still incorporated to the api routes. This fixed the docs, and the tests behave as expected.

#2639 & #2904 handled about similar issues.

Please consider taking a look at this.
Did not test anything else except the `TestClient.get()` and the `/docs` calls mentioned.

### Operating System

Windows

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.81.0

### Python Version

3.8.5

### Additional Context

![image_2022-09-03_171250786](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/58403260/188286391-06cc5b62-f7d8-4392-b873-35e390a73dde.png)

![image_2022-09-03_171058206](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/58403260/188286353-f5f87932-2e03-4d51-bb46-4273a548623a.png)

# https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/sql-databases/#alternative-db-session-with-middleware
# paths in repo:
# docs_src/sql_databases/sql_app/alt_main.py
# docs_src/sql_databases/sql_app_py39/alt_main.py
# docs_src/sql_databases/sql_app_py310/alt_main.py

@app.middleware("http")
async def db_session_middleware(request: Request, call_next):
    response = Response("Internal server error", status_code=500)
    try:
        request.state.db = SessionLocal()
        response = await call_next(request)
    finally:
        request.state.db.close()
    return response
```


### Description

In the docs is an example of how to create a DB session via middleware. In this example, an error 500 response is generated at the beginning. However, this response is never used, because if an exception occurs, this response object is never returned.

One possibility would be to move the return statement into the finally block. However, the problem is that the text in the response text is not displayed in swagger because the content type still has to be defined.

Generally speaking, it is not necessary to return an error 500, as far as I understand. Because this is already handled in `Starlette` by the `ServerErrorMiddleware`.

So my suggestion would be to remove the error 500 from this piece of code, as it does nothing at the moment and everything works as it should:

```py
@app.middleware("http")
async def db_session_middleware(request: Request, call_next):
    try:
        request.state.db = SessionLocal()
        return await call_next(request)
    finally:
        request.state.db.close()
```

## Resources

+ https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/sql-databases/#alternative-db-session-with-middleware
+ docs_src/sql_databases/sql_app/alt_main.py
+ docs_src/sql_databases/sql_app_py39/alt_main.py
+ docs_src/sql_databases/sql_app_py310/alt_main.py

### Operating System

Linux

### Operating System Details

OS independent

### FastAPI Version

0.81.0

### Python Version

3.10.6

### Additional Context

Just a small bug in the example code in the doc. If it is desired I can open a PR to fix this bug.

comments.author: r0b2g1t
comments.body: I have created a pull-request to fix that issue: [#5872](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/5872)

issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from typing import Union

from fastapi import FastAPI, APIRouter
from fastapi.responses import FileResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel


class Item(BaseModel):
    id: str
    value: str


responses = {
    404: {"description": "Item not found"},
    302: {"description": "The item was moved"},
    403: {"description": "Not enough privileges"},
    "5XX": {"description": "Server errors", "model": Item},
}


app = FastAPI(responses=responses)
router = APIRouter()

@router.get(
    "/items/{item_id}",
    response_model=Item,
)
async def read_item(item_id: str, img: Union[bool, None] = None):
    if img:
        return FileResponse("image.png", media_type="image/png")
    else:
        return {"id": "foo", "value": "there goes my hero"}

app.include_router(router)
```


### Description

In approx 0.76.0 a regression was observed related to the OpenAPI responses no longer accepting patterns of the form 4XX 5XX etc.
```
>>> app.include_router(router)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/tmp/venv/lib/python3.8/site-packages/fastapi/applications.py", line 420, in include_router
    self.router.include_router(
  File "/tmp/venv/lib/python3.8/site-packages/fastapi/routing.py", line 738, in include_router
    self.add_api_route(
  File "/tmp/venv/lib/python3.8/site-packages/fastapi/routing.py", line 565, in add_api_route
    route = route_class(
  File "/tmp/venv/lib/python3.8/site-packages/fastapi/routing.py", line 422, in __init__
    assert is_body_allowed_for_status_code(
  File "/tmp/venv/lib/python3.8/site-packages/fastapi/utils.py", line 24, in is_body_allowed_for_status_code
    current_status_code = int(status_code)
ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: '5XX'
```
Please refer to this PR https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/5145 which introduced the new code

### Operating System

Linux

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.81.0

### Python Version

3.8.13

### Additional Context

I can't actually commit to a PR/monitoring this repo/such. I will likely sponsor the project soon as I love it, but I simply don't have that kind of time

I read about Middleware and seems simple enough.

using above middleware docs I have to do things manually but way I have been able to use Security in signature and do those check for me I wanted to hook that into /admin endpoint.  Actually if no JWT token he should be send to login page that is also not happening my entry page is /.

```
@app.get("/admin", response_class=Response)
def setup(request: Request,

app = FastAPI()

```

from models import User

from fastapi import Depends, HTTPException, status
from fastapi.security import OAuth2PasswordBearer
import jwt_token

oauth2_scheme = OAuth2PasswordBearer(tokenUrl="login")

def get_current_user(token: str = Depends(oauth2_scheme)):
    credentials_exception = HTTPException(
        status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,
        detail="Could not validate credentials",
        headers={"WWW-Authenticate": "Bearer"},
    )
    return jwt_token.verify_token(token,credentials_exception)
```

jwt_token.py
```python
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from fastapi import Depends
from jose import JWTError, jwt
import schemas

SECRET_KEY = "e3926957a0582e9a4a3d17034c157008"
ALGORITHM = "HS256"
ACCESS_TOKEN_EXPIRE_MINUTES = 1440

def create_access_token(data: dict):
    to_encode = data.copy()
    expire = datetime.utcnow() + timedelta(minutes=ACCESS_TOKEN_EXPIRE_MINUTES)
    to_encode.update({"exp": expire})
    encoded_jwt = jwt.encode(to_encode, SECRET_KEY, algorithm=ALGORITHM)
    return encoded_jwt

def verify_token(token:str,credentials_exception):
    try:
        payload = jwt.decode(token, SECRET_KEY, algorithms=[ALGORITHM])
        email: str = payload.get("sub")
        if email is None:
            raise credentials_exception
        token_data = schemas.TokenData(email=email)
    except JWTError:
        raise credentials_exception
```

issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from unicodedata import category
from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI
import schemas,models
from database import SessionLocal, engine
from sqlalchemy.orm import Session
from typing import List, Optional
from fastapi.middleware import Middleware
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware

middleware = [
    Middleware(
        CORSMiddleware,
        allow_origins=['*'],
        allow_credentials=True,
        allow_methods=['*'],
        allow_headers=['*']
    )
]

app = FastAPI(middleware=middleware)

models.Base.metadata.create_all(engine)

def get_db():
    db = SessionLocal()
    try:
        yield db
    finally:
        db.close()

@app.post('/blog',tags=['blogs'])
def create(request : schemas.Blogs, db : Session = Depends(get_db),):
    new_mail = models.Blogs(title=request.title,category = request.category, body=request.body, translate=request.translate, author = request.author, translator = request.translator, published = request.published, read = request.read, photo = request.photo)
    db.add(new_mail)
    db.commit()
    db.refresh(new_mail)
    return new_mail

@app.get('/blog', tags=['blogs'])
def all(db: Session = Depends(get_db)):
    blogs = db.query(models.Blogs).all()
    return blogs

@app.get('/blog/',tags=['blogs'])
def all( category: Optional[str] = None,db: Session = Depends(get_db)):
    blogs = db.query(models.Blogs).all()
    if not category:
        return blogs
    results = db.query(models.Blogs).filter(models.Blogs.category == category).all()
    return results

@app.get('/blog/{title}',tags=['blogs'])
def show(title,db: Session = Depends(get_db)):
    blogs = db.query(models.Blogs).filter(models.Blogs.title == title).first()
    return blogs

@app.delete('/blog/{title}',tags=['blogs'])
def delete(title,db: Session = Depends(get_db)):
    db.query(models.Blogs).filter(models.Blogs.title == title).delete(synchronize_session='evaluate')
    db.commit()
    return "deleted"

@app.put('/blog/{title}',tags=['blogs'])
def update(title,request : schemas.Blogs,db: Session = Depends(get_db)):
    db.query(models.Blogs).filter(models.Blogs.title == title).update(request.dict())
    db.commit()
    return "updated"

@app.post('/email',tags=['emails'])
def create(request : schemas.Emails, db : Session = Depends(get_db)):
    new_email = models.Emails(email=request.email)
    db.add(new_email)
    db.commit()
    db.refresh(new_email)
    return new_email

@app.get('/email',tags=['emails'])
def all(db: Session = Depends(get_db)):
    blogs = db.query(models.Emails).all()
    return blogs
```


### Description

When I make a request from Frontend with axios and whenever I made a request, it is showing the CORS error. And I already have setup the CORS middleware in fastapi.

``
    const createMail = (email) => {
        axios.post(emails, email)
            .then(response => {
                const addedEmail = response.data;
                console.log(`POST: user is added`, addedEmail);
            })
            .catch(error => {console.log(error)});
    };``

3.10

_No response_

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body: You can't have both `allow_origins=['*']` and `allow_credentials=True` as per the [docs](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/cors/#use-corsmiddleware).

comments.author: iudeen
comments.body: When both client and server is in same env (host) I don't think it's a CORS request.

comments.author: TomEverson
JarroVGIT
comments.body: Yeah this is a common misconception about the CORS middleware: it doesn't work on HTTP 500 response codes. There are several discussions on it in this repo and also in Starlette (where this was actually decided on). So, look in your server logs for the reason of the 500 Internal Server Error and ignore the CORS.
