issues.title: Unexpected behaviour while using Query with Depends
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends, Query
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Union

ALIAS_NAME = "param-with-alias"

class ParamsWithoutAlias(BaseModel):

	param_without_alias: Union[str, None] = Query(None)

class ParamsWithAlias(BaseModel):

	param_with_alias: Union[str, None] = Query(None, alias=ALIAS_NAME)

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/api/get")
async def get_route(
        params: ParamsWithoutAlias = Depends(),
	alias_params: ParamsWithAlias = Depends(),
):

	return {}
```


### Description

While using query params as dependencies, I came across a very interesting problem. The example code above should generate the following OpenApi schema:

![expected_behaviour](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/36893803/183673315-2431434e-ceb3-456f-9c57-fef70ce65eee.png)

Unfortunately, the ParamsWithAlias class generates the **extra_data** parameter:

![real_behaviour](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/36893803/183674159-ca2d5cf7-5ed4-464a-b21c-ad4c20301720.png)

Strangely enough, if I change the **ALIAS_NAME** variable to let's say `ALIAS_NAME="paramwithalias"` or `ALIAS_NAME="param_with_alias"` the error doesn't occur:

![paramwithalias](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/36893803/183674960-aff20295-bc54-41b4-b36f-061b8c9586f2.png)

I also tried using the pydantic **alias_generator** Config method or `pydantic.Field` objects insted of `fastapi.Query`, but it didn't solve the issue either.

### Operating System

Linux

### Operating System Details

Fastapi is being run with uvicorn in docker container with base image **python:3.10-slim-buster**.

### FastAPI Version

0.79.0

### Python Version

Python 3.10.6

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body:
This is behaviour that comes from Pydantic, not FastAPI. Whenever you give a 'non-pythonic' alias (like with dashes), the signature that `inspect.signature()` will get from that `BaseModel` subclass will be grouped in the kwargs `extra_data`. A reproducible bit if you want to raise this in the pydantic repo is this:

```python
import inspect
from typing import Union

from fastapi import Query
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field


class A(BaseModel):
    param_with_alias: Union[str, None] = Field(None, alias="hello-you")
    some_other_param_wihouth_alias: str = Field(None)


signature = inspect.signature(A)

print(signature)
```

There is nothing FastAPI can do about this.

comments.author: matmalinowski
comments.body: Thanks a lot for your comment. It is indeed a pydantic issue. I solved it by using dataclasses instead.

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body: Could you post your solution? Might be helpful for future readers that run into the same problem

comments.author: matmalinowski

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
import typing
from starlette.types import ASGIApp
from fastapi import Request, Response
from starlette.middleware.base import BaseHTTPMiddleware, RequestResponseEndpoint, DispatchFunction


class AllowIps(BaseHTTPMiddleware):
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp, ips: typing.Optional[list] = None, dispatch: typing.Optional[DispatchFunction] = None) -> None:
        super().__init__(app, dispatch)
        self.ips = ips

    async def dispatch(self, request: Request, call_next: RequestResponseEndpoint) -> Response:
        response = await call_next(request)
        if request.client.host not in self.ips:
            print("xxxxxxxxxxx")
            return {"msg": "ip not allowed"}
        return response
```


### Description

* I have a requirement that the unsatisfied ip does not allow access to my application.
* The custom middleware seems to have to return a call_next object, how can I do this.

Linux

issues.title: Validation of response model when using discriminated union
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from enum import Enum
from typing import Annotated, Union, Literal
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, root_validator


class PetType(str, Enum):
	CAT = "cat"
	DOG = "dog"

class Cat(BaseModel):
	kind: Literal[PetType.CAT]
	has_paws: bool
	color: str

	@root_validator
	def validate_cat(cls, values):
		print("Validating Cat")
		color = values.get('color')
		if color.lower() != "yellow":
			raise ValueError("Cat must be yellow")
		return values

class Dog(BaseModel):
	kind: Literal[PetType.DOG]
	has_paws: bool

	@root_validator
	def validate_cat(cls, values):
		print("Validating Dog")
		return values

Pet = Annotated[Union[Cat, Dog], Field(discriminator='kind')]

class Animal(BaseModel):
	pet: Pet

app = FastAPI()


@app.post("/", response_model=Animal)
def handle_pet(animal: Animal):
	print(f"Pet has been discriminated: {type(animal.pet)}")
	print(f"Animal can be re-created from dict: {Animal(**animal.dict())}")
	return animal
```


### Description

The `POST /` endpoint can be used normally with a pet `Cat` payload:
```
{
	"pet": {
		"kind": "cat",
		"has_paws": true,
		"color": "Yellow"
	}
}
```

However, using a pet `Dog` payload:
```
{
	"pet": {
		"kind": "dog",
		"has_paws": true
	}
}
```
an error is raised, because the `root_validator` function of the `Cat` class is called. 
This happens when the endpoint function returns. When validating the input payload, only the `Dog` root validator is called.

I know that this issue will likely also be linked to `pydantic` and discriminated unions; but why is FastAPI trying to validate the response against the `Cat` model? As noted, re-creating the `Animal` object from the object `.dict()` does not fail.

### Operating System

macOS

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.79.0

### Python Version

3.9.12

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: iudeen
comments.body:
```python
# routing.py ln128
value, errors_ = field.validate(response_content, {}, loc=("response",))
        else:
            value, errors_ = await run_in_threadpool(  # type: ignore[misc]
                field.validate, response_content, {}, loc=("response",)
            )
```

It fails at this point! I am not sure if the issue is with Pydantic or FastAPI. It seems to call the `validate` method on `ModelField` class, which is not handling the discrimination properly; and all this are happeing on Pydantic side.

Discussions related:
https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/issues/3758

From above reference, we can get a better understanding. 

Since response model are created with `Optional` it is no considering the discriminator. 

To replicate, I tried this: 

```python
class Animal(BaseModel, ):
    pet: Optional[Annotated[Union[Cat, Dog], Field(discriminator='kind')]]



Animal.validate({
	"pet": {
		"kind": "dog",
		"has_paws": True
	}
})
```

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from json import loads
from uvicorn import run
from psycopg2 import connect, Error
from redis import Redis, RedisError
from urllib.request import urlopen
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.staticfiles import StaticFiles
from fastapi.responses import HTMLResponse

app = FastAPI()

app.mount("/static", StaticFiles(directory="./static"), name="static")

url = "http://worldtimeapi.org/api/ip"

try:
	r = Redis(
		host='localhost',
		port=6379)
except (Exception, RedisError) as error:
	print("Error while connecting to redis", error)

try:
	connection = connect(user="filip", password="1234", host="172.17.0.3", port=5432, database="db")
	cursor = connection.cursor()
except (Exception, Error) as error:
	print("Error while connecting to PostgreSQL", error)



def time_api():
	response = urlopen(url).read()
	data = loads(response)
	return data["datetime"]

@app.get("/time")
def time_operations():
	time = r.get('TIME')
	if time == None:
		time = time_api()

		r.set("TIME", time)
		r.expire("TIME", 30)

		cursor.execute("SELECT version();")
		insert_query = """ INSERT INTO time (current) VALUES ('{}')""".format(time)
		cursor.execute(insert_query)
		connection.commit()
	return time

@app.get("/", response_class=HTMLResponse)
def root():
	return """
          random html
	"""

if __name__ == "__main__":
	run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8080)
```

The important details are that im using redis, postgre, nginx and myapp so those 4 containers.

issues.title: Create a middleware to sanitize the request body
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from starlette.middleware.base import BaseHTTPMiddleware
from starlette.types import Message
from fastapi import Request
import bleach
import json


class SanitizeMiddleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware):

    def __init__(self, app):
        super().__init__(app)

    @staticmethod
    def __sanitize_array(array_values):
        for index, value in enumerate(array_values):
            if isinstance(value, dict):
                array_values[index] = {key: bleach.clean(
                    value) for key, value in value.items()}
            else:
                array_values[index] = bleach.clean(value)
        return array_values

    async def set_body(self, request: Request):
        receive_ = await request._receive()
        async def receive() -> Message:
            return receive_

        request._receive = receive

    async def dispatch(self, request: Request, call_next):
        await self.set_body(request)

        if request.method == 'POST' or request.method == 'PUT':
            json_body = await request.json()
            sanitize_body = {key: self.__sanitize_array(value) if isinstance(
                value, list) else bleach.clean(value) for key, value in json_body.items()}
            request._body = json.dumps(
                sanitize_body, indent=2).encode('utf-8')
            await self.set_body(request)

        response = await call_next(request)
        return response
```


### Description

Hello guys, I'm facing an issue while trying to sanitize the request body from POST and PUT methods. The idea is to sanitize the request.body() before it even reachs the view. For instance, I would like to pass _bleach_ on it to avoid security issues that might appear under the body that is sent.
I've built a middleware that inherit from BaseHTTPMiddleware, to sanetize the body, but I've notived that I'm not changing the original _request_ element:

### Operating System

Linux

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.73.0

### Python Version

3.8.10

### Additional Context

_No response_

> 
>     def __init__(self, app):
>         super().__init__(app)
>

>         async def receive() -> Message:

>

>             sanitize_body = {key: self.__sanitize_array(value) if isinstance(
>                 value, list) else bleach.clean(value) for key, value in json_body.items()}
>             request._body = json.dumps(
>                 sanitize_body, indent=2).encode('utf-8')
>             request = await self.set_body(request)
> 
>         response = await call_next(request)
>         return response
> ```


I tested it here and it continues with the same previous request

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body:
Hmm I did not expect that.. 🤔 I am tinkering around with this and I notice that there is a custom request being send to the next ASGI app, but the body is unsanitized. If I include a custom key in the `request.scope`, then that key (and value) is available in my endpoint. 

Somewhere, Starlette (or FastAPI but I am thinking Starlette) is unsanitizing the body. Which is very weird!

comments.author: MarcelFox
comments.body: I think that maybe the request element isn't the same at all, I've noticed that the _id_ for both are different, not sure if that tells much but I'm assuming that they might be addressing to different elements in the memory, but is pure speculation at this point.

comments.author: xingdongzhe
comments.body:
@MarcelFox You are right for this
> I've noticed that the id for both are different

In `BaseHTTPMiddleware`, It will be new a request instance pass to next middleware or route endpoint
At this point, we only need the same request instance, so @casluxd you can do this two ways:
- pass request to scope: `scope["request"]=sanitized_reqeust`
- use contextvars: `sanitized_request_context=contextvars.Contexvars("request"), sanitized_request_context.set(sanitized_reqeust)`  

So you can get middlerware request in your route endpoint

from pydantic import BaseModel

class BaseResponse(BaseModel):

class Detect(BaseResponse):

router = APIRouter()

Since the error got cut off half way, I can not know which part of the model is referring to `type`. I've removed `Optional` from model, and make it as simple as possible. ### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
import uvicorn
from fastapi import FastAPI
from starlette.middleware.base import BaseHTTPMiddleware


app = FastAPI()


@app.get("/info")
def info():
    # raises Exception as expected, the traceback is seen in console
    raise Exception


private_api = FastAPI()


@private_api.get("/info")
def info():
    # exception is handled silently, no traceback is seen in console
    raise Exception


app.mount("/private", private_api)


class Middleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware):

    async def dispatch(self, request, call_next):
        return await call_next(request)


app.add_middleware(Middleware)  # when this is removed, the exceptions are raised for all routes


if __name__ == '__main__':
    uvicorn.run(app, port=8000)
```


### Description

When exceptions are raised on a subapp, the exceptions are not propagated all the way to see in console
In the example code 
**/info** raises an exception and the full stacktrace is seen in console
**/private/info** does not raise the exception and only `INFO:     127.0.0.1:56308 - "GET /info HTTP/1.1" 500 Internal Server Error` is shown in console

issues.title: Unavailability to set `default` response for OpenAPI doc at `0.79.0`
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel


class Item(BaseModel):
    id: str
    value: str


class Message(BaseModel):
    message: str


app = FastAPI()


@app.get("/items/{item_id}", response_model=Item, responses={"default": {"model": Message}})
async def read_item(item_id: str):
    if item_id == "foo":
        return {"id": "foo", "value": "there goes my hero"}
    else:
        return JSONResponse(status_code=404, content={"message": "Item not found"})
```


### Description

Prior to `0.79.0` I was able to specify the `default` response for the path. According to the OpenAPI Spec, it should be somehow possible: https://swagger.io/specification/#path-item-object

At `0.79.0` I am receiving this exception:
```python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 19, in <module>
  File "/Users/matnowak/.pyenv/versions/3.9.11/envs/radkit3.9/lib/python3.9/site-packages/fastapi/routing.py", line 622, in decorator
    self.add_api_route(
  File "/Users/matnowak/.pyenv/versions/3.9.11/envs/radkit3.9/lib/python3.9/site-packages/fastapi/routing.py", line 561, in add_api_route
    route = route_class(
  File "/Users/matnowak/.pyenv/versions/3.9.11/envs/radkit3.9/lib/python3.9/site-packages/fastapi/routing.py", line 418, in __init__
    assert is_body_allowed_for_status_code(
  File "/Users/matnowak/.pyenv/versions/3.9.11/envs/radkit3.9/lib/python3.9/site-packages/fastapi/utils.py", line 24, in is_body_allowed_for_status_code
    current_status_code = int(status_code)
ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'default'

invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'default'
```

How to set the default response in OpenAPI?

### Operating System

Linux, Windows, macOS

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.79.0

### Python Version

3.9.11

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: kalmastenitin
comments.body: default response_code is 200, you need to specify status code instead of default keyword

comments.author: matiuszka
comments.body:
@kalmastenitin As you can see in the spec example: https://swagger.io/specification/#path-item-object besides `200` there is the option to specify the `default`.

If I will rephrase the question here, it will be: how to recreate the same structure as presented in OpenAPI examples from `fastapi` point of view?

comments.author: tapple
comments.body: "default" response is a specifier for any status code that doesn't have an entry. It usually is for specifying the schema of error responses

comments.author: MarkusSintonen
comments.body:
Hi! We are also seeing this issue. We are initializing FastAPI with a default response for unexpected error responses for OpenAPI spec:
```
FastAPI(..., responses={"default": {"model": UnexpectedErrorResponse}})
```
After updating to `0.79.0` this breaks with similar exception as above.

See OpenAPI specification for "default" responses: https://swagger.io/docs/specification/describing-responses/#default previously this was possible to define it in FastAPI but it is now broken.

This PR seems the one that broke the OpenAPI default-response definitions: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/5145 cc @tiangolo

comments.author: matiuszka
comments.body: @MarkusSintonen Thanks for the answer. I am glad that someone else is also seeing this as a potential bug. I will try to propose a fix for this.

comments.author: gruberlu
comments.body:
Hi!

We are also facing this issue. We are using `default` as status code for error responses too.

Also things like `5XX` (see [Swagger Docs](https://swagger.io/docs/specification/describing-responses/#status-codes)) should be valid values.

Changing the function `is_body_allowed_for_status_code` could fix this issue. Maybe something like this:
```python
def is_body_allowed_for_status_code(status_code: Union[int, str, None]) -> bool:
    if status_code is None:
        return True
    if isinstance(status_code, str) and not status_code.isdigit():
        # return 'True' as we cannot know if this response is allowed to have a body
        return True
    current_status_code = int(status_code)
    return not (current_status_code < 200 or current_status_code in {204, 304})
```

comments.author: JarroVGIT
Kyle-sandeman-mrdfood
comments.body: @JarroVGIT the OpenAPI spec also allows specifiers of 4XX etc, which your code would still raise the same ValueError on. It's not very nice but at least the default use cases are client/server errors and as such we won't see 2XX 3XX often/at all

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body: My pull request does account for that, it checks various values including the xXX values

issues.body:
### First Check

### Commit to Help

### Example Code

```python
@app.middleware("http")
async def add_request_user_to_body(req: Request, call_next):
    '''
    description: 
    return {*}
    '''
    username=req.headers['username']
    if req.method.lower()!='get':
        req._body['username']=username
    response = await call_next(req)
    return response
```


### Description

1. i use jwt for auth, client will carry its jwt in headers everytime.
2. i can get username from jwt and use it as data owner.
3. for convenience, i want to add username to body which is from jwt. then i can use body.dict() to init my db model directly.
4. so, i want to achieve it in middleware instead of on each route.

then how to modify request to add custom data？

- [ ] is it correct to modify request.state like `request.state.username=username` ? if ok , then how to get in routeHandler which is like below?
```python
@router.post('/test')
async def handler(data:MyModel):
    pass
```

0.73.0

3.9

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body:
> is it correct to modify request.state like `request.state.username=username` ?

You can change `request.state` as you please and that is totally fine, for example like `request.state.username = 'username1'`.

To get this information in your path operation function (it is not called a route handler), you can do this:

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException, Request, Depends

async def username_dependency(r: Request):
    #put your JWT logic here, I will just assume you retrieved your username
    r.state.is_authenticated = True
    r.state.username = "user1"
    return

app = FastAPI(dependencies=[Depends(username_dependency)])

@app.get("/")
async def root(r: Request):
    if r.state.is_authenticated:
        return r.state.username
    return HTTPException(401, "You are not authenticated!")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import uvicorn
    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8000)
```

The above example will run as-is so you can copy paste it and try it out. It works with a global dependency. I would recommend that rather than using middleware, because global dependencies are better testable and the intent is much easier to read (in my opinion). 

Hope this helps!

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body:
Sorry one last comment, as you explicitly asked for a way to do this in middleware. Below is a fully working example on **both** approaches (mw and dependency):

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException, Request, Depends

async def username_dependency(r: Request):
    #put your JWT logic here, I will just assume you retrieved your username
    r.state.is_authenticated_from_dependency = True
    r.state.username_from_dependency = "user_from_dependency"
    return

app = FastAPI(dependencies=[Depends(username_dependency)])

@app.middleware("http")
async def add_request_user_to_body(r: Request, call_next):
    #put your JWT logic here, I will just assume you retrieved your username
    r.state.is_authenticated_from_mw= True
    r.state.username_from_mw = "user_from_middleware"
    response = await call_next(r)
    return response

@app.get("/from_dependency")
async def root(r: Request):
    if r.state.is_authenticated_from_dependency:
        return r.state.username_from_dependency
    return HTTPException(401, "You are not authenticated!")

@app.get("/from_middleware")
async def root(r: Request):
    if r.state.is_authenticated_from_mw:
        return r.state.username_from_mw
    return HTTPException(401, "You are not authenticated!")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import uvicorn
    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8000)
```

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body:
You can combine stuff together to get to such a solution like the below. It does the authentication stuff in middleware, and access it in the `__init__` of a dependable class. 

```python
from typing import Any
from fastapi import Body, FastAPI, HTTPException, Request, Depends

class RequestModel:
    def __init__(self, r: Request, id: int = Body(), value: Any = Body()) -> None:
        self.id = id
        self.value = value
        if r.state.is_authenticated:
            self.username = r.state.username
        else:
            raise HTTPException(401, "Not Authorized")

app = FastAPI()

@app.middleware("http")
async def add_request_user_to_body(r: Request, call_next):
    #put your JWT logic here, I will just assume you retrieved your username
    r.state.is_authenticated = True
    r.state.username = "user_from_middleware"
    response = await call_next(r)
    return response

@app.post("/test")
async def root(data: RequestModel = Depends() ):
    return {"Hello": data.username, "YourID": data.id}


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import uvicorn
    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8000)
```

Calling the endpoint:
```bash
curl -X 'POST' \
  'http://0.0.0.0:8000/test' \
  -H 'accept: application/json' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
  "id": 3,
  "value": "someValue"
}'
```

Results in:
```json
{
  "Hello": "user_from_middleware",
  "YourID": 3
}
```

comments.body: Why would you want to change the request body itself, while you can specifically put logic into the State object of a request? I'd say it is a worse practice to manually change the user input, rather than separating the input and derived information from that input.

> Why would you want to change the request body itself, while you can specifically put logic into the State object of a request? I'd say it is a worse practice to manually change the user input, rather than separating the input and derived information from that input.

From the point of view of design, what you said is indeed right. One thing I'm adamant about is that I want to simplify the code, which means making a lot of use of reflection and automation for convention based programming. In my current project, the data format of interaction between client and server is determined, and this way of modifying request only applies to some routes, such as RESTFUL API, so modifying request is the easiest for me.

comments.author: nikoladsp
comments.body:
Hi all,
in order not to open another similar question, I will be free to ask here (hope not a problem).

I would like to use refresh JWT token in my app. So all endpoints will use access token (short lifespan) in Bearer field. Only exceptions are login endpoint (no token needed at all) and endpoint where I want to generate access token itself - in which case refresh token is sent in Bearer field. 

There are different signing keys in these two cases: when coding/decoding refresh token, I am using application key (env variable, something does not change during runtime) and for coding/decoding access token I am using refresh token which is generated _**per user**_ (each user has its own)

My questions are as follows:
1. how can I (if possible) set dependency for majority of endpoints in FastAPI constructor, so I don't have to drag the same code everywhere and put exception/override on couple of specific ones?
2. my dependency should obtain SqlAlchemy session and also a user object pre-loaded so they can be passed to different services called from endpoints. What would be the recommended way? If I want to use Swagger, shall I inherit my dependency class from `HTTPBearer` and return `credentials.credentials`? Does this class must have

    ```python
    async def __call__(self, request: Request):
    ```
    This part is bit unclear to me. Can/shall I use the above approach or if there is a better/cleaner in my case?

Thank you kindly.

Best regards

comments.author: iudeen
comments.body:
@nikoladsp I think its needed since you'd be inheriting a base class. 

```python
    def __call__(self, request: Request, response: Response) -> str:
        cookies = request.cookies
        login_token = cookies.get("login-token")
        if not login_token:
            login_token = request.headers.get("login-token")
        if not login_token:
            raise HTTPException(status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED)

       # --------- some magic sauce --------------
       return user_id

```

Some explanation (purely based on my understanding):

When you define a dependency using a Class, the `__call__` is needed for it to be invoked. It's because as per the [documentation](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/advanced-dependencies/#use-the-instance-as-a-dependency) the dependency expects a Callable and is called, which essentially is provided by the call dunder method.

`Depends(MyMagicClass())` is equivalent to `MyMagicClass()()`

issues.title: OpenAPI schema: Missing description with Query parameters
issues.body: ### First Check - [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue. - [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it. - [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search. - [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information. - [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer. - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic). - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui). - [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc). ### Commit to Help - [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆 ### Example Code ```python from fastapi import FastAPI, APIRouter, Depends import uvicorn from typing import List from fastapi import Query from pydantic import BaseModel, Field import os class A(BaseModel): foo: str = Field( description="This is Foo", ) class B(A): bar: str = Field( description="This is Bar with alias", alias="bar2", ) class C(B): def __init__(self, xyz: str = Query(description="This is Xyz")): self.xyz = xyz app = FastAPI(docs_url=None, redoc_url="/redocs") rtr_api = APIRouter(prefix="/api") @rtr_api.get( "/test1", description="Query me", summary="Querio-matic 1", response_model=List[B], ) async def test(bdata: B = Depends()): return [bdata] @rtr_api.get( "/test2", description="Query me 2", summary="Querio-matic 2", response_model=List[B], ) async def test2(bdata: B): return [bdata] @rtr_api.get( "/test3", description="Query me 3", summary="Querio-matic 3", response_model=List[C], ) async def test3(bdata: C = Depends()): return [bdata] app.include_router(rtr_api) if __name__ == "__main__": uvicorn.run( app, port=int(os.environ.get("PORT", "8000")), host=os.environ.get("HOST", "localhost"), debug=True, ) ``` ### Description * I would like to use classes with Fields with description for query parameters. Example: ```python class MyClass(BaseModel): bar: str = Field(description="This is Bar", alias="bar2") async def fct(data: MyClass = Depends()): ``` * The parsing works, aliases work. But the OpenAPI Schema does not show the description of the field. To test * Using the source code provided, go on http://localhost:8000/redocs * Notice foo and bar2 are only described when used without `Depends()`. This is for a Body schema (using with refs in the OpenAPI JSON). * The only way to have the description field is to use a `Query` structure as an argument *in* the init. The following also does not show the description. ``` class MyClass(BaseModel): bar: str = Query(description="This is Bar", alias="bar2") ``` While this usage is not documented, I would be hoping to use Classes inheritance to define query parameters and reuse descriptions. This does not seem possible without adding it manually to the schema. Yet I was still unsure if it's an expected behavior since `alias` is still taken into account. The reason for using Fields would be to also re-use those schemas when returning a response. Perhaps I am missing a Python feature that would make this possible when setting the `__init__` ? If I use `def __init__(self, xyz: B = Query()):`, I get `AssertionError: Param: xyz can only be a request body, using Body()` Thank you for your help ### Operating System macOS ### Operating System Details _No response_ ### FastAPI Version 0.79.0 ### Python Version Python 3.9.12 ### Additional Context OpenAPI JSON file: [openapi-fastapi-github-issue.txt](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/files/9117708/openapi-fastapi-github-issue.txt) You can see the difference between `jq '.paths["/api/test1"].get.parameters` ```json [ { "required": true, "schema": { "title": "Foo", "type": "string" }, "name": "foo", "in": "query" }, { "required": true, "schema": { "title": "Bar2", "type": "string" }, "name": "bar2", "in": "query" } ] ``` `jq '.paths["/api/test3"].get.parameters` ```json [ { "description": "This is Xyz", "required": true, "schema": { "title": "Xyz", "type": "string", "description": "This is Xyz" }, "name": "xyz", "in": "query" } ] ```

issues.title: How to Manage Multiple Database Sessions when working with an API including Async Tasks
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
class RDSConnector(metaclass=Singleton):

    Base = declarative_base()

    async def intialize_rds_connection(self, db_config):
        # read connection
        read_pool_size_count = db_config.get("read_rds").get("pool_size")
        read_max_overflow_count = db_config.get("read_rds").get("max_overflow")
        db_url = 'mysql+pymysql'+'://'+read_user+':'+read_pwd+'@'+host+':'+str(port)+'/'+db_name
        engine = create_engine(db_url, pool_recycle=10, pool_pre_ping=True, pool_size=read_pool_size_count, max_overflow=read_max_overflow_count,pool_timeout=20)
        self.sessionLocal = sessionmaker(autocommit=False, autoflush=False, bind=engine)

        # write connection
        write_pool_size_count = db_config.get("write_rds").get("pool_size")
        write_max_overflow_count = db_config.get("write_rds").get("max_overflow")
        wdb_url = 'mysql+pymysql'+'://'+write_user+':'+write_pwd+'@'+whost+':'+str(wport)+'/'+wdb_name
        write_engine = create_engine(wdb_url, pool_recycle=10, pool_pre_ping=True, pool_size=write_pool_size_count, max_overflow=write_max_overflow_count,pool_timeout=20)
        self.writeSessionLocal = sessionmaker(autocommit=False, autoflush=False, bind=write_engine)



    def get_db_read(self):
        db = self.sessionLocal()
        try:
            #logger.info('db connected')
            yield db
        finally:
            db.close()
            #logger.info('db close')
    
    def get_db_write(self):
        db = self.writeSessionLocal()
        try:
            #logger.info('db connected')
            yield db
        finally:
            db.close()
            #logger.info('db close')
```


### Description

By default When an EndPoint receives a request with help of Dependency Injection I create a DB session for that route and use it inside that Service/Route/DAO Layer.
Consider an API/endpoint having multiple reads and writes. 
Now because pool_recycle is set to 20 and RDS connection in production environment is set to 1 minute after one minute connection would be lost.
If an Asynchronous task takes 30 Minutes to complete and on that completion I need to make some write. I wouldn’t  be able to make because i don’t have active db session.
### Also I need to make sure DB session has both read and write db's Instance

# Probable Way of direction of Solution:-
1. Context Managers for DB Sessions:
2. DBSessionMiddleWare
3. Routing Class to manage both read and write


### Operating System

macOS

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.75.0

### Python Version

3.8.9

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: jgould22
comments.body:
> Now because pool_recycle is set to 20 and RDS connection in production environment is set to 1 minute after one minute connection would be lost.

Have you tested that this is correct? I do not believe that it is.

Connections are only invalidated during checkout. So long as you have the connection checked out it will not be recycled.  When you return it to the the pool the it will be recycled.

https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/14/core/pooling.html#setting-pool-recycle

Operating System Details _No response_ ### FastAPI Version 0.78.0 ### Python Version 3.8.13 ### Additional Context This bug happens because whatever is converting bare dataclasses to pydantic dataclasses isn't caching the output of the conversion, which means that, in dataclass `B`, the dataclass `A` referenced in field `a` is different to the target of the mapping in field `b`. In a naïve converter, this is what happens: ``` Python 3.8.13 | packaged by conda-forge | (default, Mar 25 2022, 06:04:10) [GCC 10.3.0] on linux Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information. >>> from dataclasses import dataclass >>> from pydantic.dataclasses import dataclass as pydataclass >>> @dataclass ... class A: ... a: int ... >>> first = pydataclass(A) >>> second = pydataclass(A) >>> first == second False >>> id(first), id(second) (93867703664320, 93867703667648) >>> hash(first), hash(second) (5866731479020, 5866731479228) ``` I don't think this is a bug in pydantic, though, as FastAPI is the library that claims dataclasses are supported. I could try contributing a PR, if someone could point me to where the pydantic dataclass is generated. As an additional note, if I don't fill the `responses` dict, this bug doesn't happen.

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body:
Kudo's on the comprehensive report and research! (Y) 

I did some digging as well as to your last question (where is this is happening) and I am very confused. The response_fields are created upon app startup (when `APIRouter` is initiated). Here, a `ModelField()` object is made based on your `responses` parameter. This `ModelField()` still holds reference to `<class '__main__.B'>` in it's `type_` param.  

However, when the application is being called (for example, because you are going to /docs), it is running at some point `(fastapi/utils.py).get_flat_models_from_routes()`. Here, the route its responses are evaluated. Before it is added to a list called 'responses_from_routes' the evaluation of `.type` on the ModelField is still `<class '__main__.B'>`. But right there after, the evaluation changes to `<class 'pydantic.dataclasses._Pydantic_B_4839801328'>`. Is this some side effect of extending a list from dict_values()? (that is what is happening)

I am wrecking my brain on how that is possible...

comments.author: ghost
comments.body:
Thanks for the explanation.

After filing this issue, I took a look at FastAPI's code once more, and even though I claimed FastAPI wasn't caching the converted dataclasses, it seems to be caching them.

So, yeah, I'm confused as well.

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
#user schema
from typing import List, Optional
from app.schemas.RBAC.resource import ResourceInDBBase

from pydantic import BaseModel, EmailStr


# Shared properties
class UserBase(BaseModel):
    email: Optional[EmailStr] = None
    is_active: Optional[bool] = True
    is_superuser: bool = False
    full_name: Optional[str] = None
    first_name: Optional[str] = None
    last_name: Optional[str] = None
    phone: Optional[int] = None
    user_metadata: Optional[str] = None



# Properties to receive via API on creation
class UserCreate(UserBase):
    email: EmailStr
    password: str


# Properties to receive via API on update
class UserUpdate(UserBase):
    password: Optional[str] = None
    first_name: Optional[str] = None
    last_name: Optional[str] = None
    phone: Optional[int] = None
    user_metadata: Optional[str] = None
    is_active: Optional[bool] = True
    is_superuser: bool = None


class UserInDBBase(UserBase):
    id: int
    resources: List[ResourceInDBBase]

    class Config:
        orm_mode = True
        allow_population_by_field_name = True


# Additional properties to return via API
class User(UserInDBBase):
    pass


# Additional properties stored in DB
class UserInDB(UserInDBBase):
    hashed_password: str

#resource schema
from typing import List, Optional
from app.schemas.RBAC.role import RoleInDBBase

from pydantic import BaseModel

# Shared properties
class ResourceBase(BaseModel):
    resource_title: Optional[str] = None
    resource_slug: Optional[str] = None
    resource_description: Optional[str] = None

# Properties to receive via API on creation
class ResourceCreate(ResourceBase):
    resource_title: str
    resource_slug: str
    resource_description: str

# Properties to receive on Resource update
class ResourceUpdate(ResourceBase):
    resource_title: Optional[str] = None
    resource_slug: Optional[str] = None
    resource_description: Optional[str] = None

# Properties shared by models stored in DB
class ResourceInDBBase(ResourceBase):
    id: int
    roles: List[RoleInDBBase]

    class Config:
        orm_mode = True
        allow_population_by_field_name = True

# Properties to return to client
class Resource(ResourceInDBBase):
    pass

# Properties properties stored in DB

    pass

#role schema
from typing import List, Optional
from app.schemas.RBAC.permission import PermissionInDBBase

from pydantic import BaseModel

# Shared properties
class RoleBase(BaseModel):
    role_title: Optional[str] = None
    role_slug: Optional[str] = None
    role_description: Optional[str] = None

# Properties to receive via API on creation
class RoleCreate(RoleBase):
    role_title: str
    role_slug: str
    role_description: str

# Properties to receive on Role update
class RoleUpdate(RoleBase):
    role_title: Optional[str] = None
    role_slug: Optional[str] = None
    role_description: Optional[str] = None

# Properties shared by models stored in DB
class RoleInDBBase(RoleBase):
    id: int

    class Config:
        orm_mode = True
        allow_population_by_field_name = True

# Properties to return to client
class Role(RoleInDBBase):
    pass

# Properties properties stored in DB
class RoleInDB(RoleInDBBase):
    pass
```


### Description

I have a fastapi application with 3 entities in the database that have a many to many relationship in a cascading way. Users have many resources and vice versa, resources have many roles and vice versa, I am wondering if it is possible to get a user entry and then list resources attached to it and roles attached to the resource? comments.author: odiseo0
comments.body: Probably you're returning the wrong response, try returning it raw to see what are you sending, also I recommend you to update the version of FastAPI

comments.author: nzivo

comments.author: dinneoel
comments.body:
I reproduced the same error by this code:
```python
from typing import List

from pydantic import BaseModel


class Resource(BaseModel):
    roles: List[str]


class User(BaseModel):
    resources: List[Resource]


def main() -> None:
    User(
        resources=[
            {},
            {"roles": []},
            {},
        ]
    )


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```
And got the next error:
```
...
    raise validation_error
pydantic.error_wrappers.ValidationError: 2 validation errors for User
resources -> 0 -> roles
  field required (type=value_error.missing)
resources -> 2 -> roles
  field required (type=value_error.missing)
```
So, the reason of such error could be missing `roles` field, or it is equals to `None`.  

## Solution 1
You cloud make roles optional. Like this:
```python
class Resource(BaseModel):
    roles: Optional[List[str]]
```
It sets `roles` field to `None` even it is not provided at all.

## Solution 2 (Preferable)
I would recommend to use `default_factory` property like this:
```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

class Resource(BaseModel):
    roles: List[str] = Field(default_factory=list)
```
But notice that `default_factory` property is available only since `pydantic==1.5` ([fullstack mysql fastapi](https://github.com/vuongtlt13/full-stack-fastapi-mysql) has `pydantic==1.4`)

from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI, HTTPException, status
from sqlalchemy.orm import Session

from . import crud, models, schemas
from .database import SessionLocal, engine

from typing import List
from sqlalchemy.orm import joinedload

models.Base.metadata.create_all(bind=engine)



app = FastAPI()


# Dependency

    db = SessionLocal()
    try:
        yield db
    finally:
        db.close()


@app.post('/author/')
async def create_author(form: schemas.Author, db: Session = Depends(get_db)):
    create_author = models.Author(**form.dict())

    db.add(create_author)
    db.commit()
    db.refresh(create_author)
    return create_author
    
    
@app.post('/books')
async def create_book(form: schemas.Book, db: Session = Depends(get_db)):

    db.add(create_book)
    db.commit()
    db.refresh(create_book)
    return create_book

    return db.query(models.Book).options(joinedload(models.Book.authors)).\
         where(models.Book.id == id).one()

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body:
Alright, you haven't provided the full code so I am unable to make a working example. But I can give you some pointers. For starters:
```python
class Author(BaseModel):
    name:str
    #books:str EDIT: this is wrong replace by the following:
    books: List[Book] 

    class Config:
        orm_mode = True 
```
The property `books` already implies multiple (which is correct, one Author can have multiple Books). But you set it as a `str`, rather than a `List[Books]`.  Your request body should look like this:

```json
{
    "name": "author name",
    "books": [
        {
            "title": "fastapi book"
        }
    ]
}
```    
I don't know what you have classified as `models` and what are `schemas`. I would expect that the Pydantic models are `schemas` (such as `Author(BaseModel)`) and the table definitions are the `models`. You named them the same (both have an `Author` object) which is highly confusing. Also, and again this is now a recurring topic in this issue, you have provided an incomplete code base for us to really help you out.

```python
async def create_author(form: schemas.Author, db: Session = Depends(get_db)):
    create_author = models.Author(**form.dict())
```
What is the definition of `schemas.Author` and what is the definition of `models.Author`? 

Lastly; please don't quote full responses in your replies, it makes it incredibly unreadable. If you really need to quote, then be very precise and only quote that part that you want to reply on.

issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
func UserRoutes(incomingRoutes *gin.RouterGroup) {

	incomingRoutes.POST("/user/signin", controllers.SignIn())

	r := incomingRoutes.Group("/user")
	{
		r.Use(middleware.Authenticate())

		r.POST("/signup", controllers.SignUp())
		r.POST("/codeauthenticate", controllers.CodeAuthenticate())
		r.POST("/forgotpassword", controllers.ForgotPassword())
		r.POST("/newpassword", controllers.NewPassword())
		r.POST("/:email", controllers.GetUserByEmail())
		r.GET("/:id", controllers.GetUserById())
		r.PUT("/:id", controllers.UpdateUser())
		r.DELETE("/:id", controllers.DeleteUser())
		r.GET("/getallusers", controllers.GetAllUsers())
	}
}
```


### Description

Here in the code above, there are a function UserRoutes which handles all the routing for the user controller, the first route which is /user/signin can be excess without middleware (jwt token) ,  after signing I group all the routes to use middleware Authenticate() which is actually checking jwt, I did not write for every route that this route should use this and this route should use that.

### Wanted Solution

 Is there any way in FastAPI to use middleware like this? like to group the routes and use a specific middleware for a group of routes.

### Wanted Code

```python
-
```


### Alternatives

macOS

0.78.0

Python 3.9.10

_No response_

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body:
I think you could write middleware that is only doing some conditional logic based on the path of the scope object. 

Here is a working example, using native ASGI middleware. (Note that I am no expert in ASGI middleware, but this should work for your use case);

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException

class CustomMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app):
        self.app = app

    async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
        if scope["type"] != "http":
            return await self.app(scope, receive, send)

        if "/user/" in (scope["path"]).lower():
            print("user should be authenticated, path: ", scope["path"])
            user_is_authenticated = True #replace with your logic.
            if user_is_authenticated:
                await self.app(scope, receive, send)
            else:
                #Note that this doesn't work and will result in an internal server error 500. 
                raise HTTPException(401, "Not Authenthicated!")
        else:
            #unauthenticated endpoints:
            print("no need for authentication, path: ", scope["path"])
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)


app = FastAPI()

app.add_middleware(CustomMiddleware)


@app.get("/some_endpoint")
async def some_endpoint():
    return "hello"

@app.get("/some_other_endpoint")
async def some_other_endpoint():
    return "hello"

@app.get("/user/authenticated_endpoint")
async def user_authenticated_endpoint():
    return "hello"

@app.get("/user/other_authenticated_endpoint")
async def user_other_authenticated_endpoint():
    return "hello"


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import uvicorn
    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8000,  )
```

Results in:
```
INFO:     Application startup complete.
INFO:     Uvicorn running on http://0.0.0.0:8000 (Press CTRL+C to quit)
no need for authentication, path:  /some_endpoint
INFO:     127.0.0.1:65341 - "GET /some_endpoint HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
user should be authenticated, path:  /user/other_authenticated_endpoint
INFO:     127.0.0.1:65344 - "GET /user/other_authenticated_endpoint HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
```

comments.author: zoliknemet
comments.body:
I prefer to do it explicit more than implicit

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException, Depends, APIRouter

app = FastAPI()

# app.add_middleware(CustomMiddleware)


def authenticate_user():
    user_is_authenticated = False  # replace with your logic.
    if not user_is_authenticated:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=401)


@app.get("/some_endpoint")
async def some_endpoint():
    return "hello"


@app.get("/some_other_endpoint")
async def some_other_endpoint():
    return "hello"


user_router = APIRouter(dependencies=[Depends(authenticate_user)])


@user_router.get("/user/authenticated_endpoint")
async def user_authenticated_endpoint():
    return "hello"


@user_router.get("/user/other_authenticated_endpoint")
async def user_other_authenticated_endpoint():
    return "hello"


app.include_router(user_router)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import uvicorn

    uvicorn.run(app, host="localhost", port=8000)


```
In this case you can easily set authentication on routers or on whole app or only on some endpoints

issues.title: Question on fastapi on_event (shutdown) hooks for gunicorn-based set-up
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
@app.on_event('shutdown')
async def stop_sqs_clients():
    log.debug('*** Shutting down worker thread ***')
    asyncio.ensure_future(sqs_client.close())
```


### Description

Greetings.

1. We are running fastapi with gunicorn workers inside docker container.
2. During startup we need to create an AWS sqs client to listen to a queue
3. During shutdown we want to be able to close this sqs client before doing a graceful exit
4. We have used gunicorn workers with a gunicorn_conf.py file
5. When container starts up we able to see gunicorn worker threads spawning off and each thread hosting a fastapi app, and able to hit the 'startup' event. Ex. -

@app.on_event('startup')
async def handle_start_up_sequence():
    log.debug('Handling start up sequence')
    asyncio.ensure_future(sqs_client.open())

We even printed the PID to ensure that gunicorn indeed spawned off multiple threads with fastapi instance in each.

However, when gunicorn master/parent thread sends a SIGABORT to worler threads (due to reaching timeout), we are unable to hit the on_event('shutdown') hook, thereby leaving dangling reference of an active SQS client in the worker thread, causing it to result a core dump.

We are looking for possible ways to utilize the shutdown event for gunicorn worker threads. Any leads/references/pointers/documentation would be appreciated.

Thanks in advance.

### Operating System

Linux

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.58.1

### Python Version

Python 3.9.13

### Additional Context

gunicorn==20.0.4

comments.body: https://github.com/encode/uvicorn/issues/1116

comments.author: thegeekcentaur
comments.body: Thanks @Kludex for sharing this. So we still looking for a better and more elegant solution on this, right?

comments.author: Kludex
comments.body:
I wouldn't say that... 🤔 I think it's a `gunicorn` issue, but there hasn't been an explanation on `gunicorn`'s side about it.

In any case, if you feel blocked, you can inherit `UvicornWorker`, and use https://github.com/encode/uvicorn/pull/1424/.

In any case, not a FastAPI issue.

comments.author: thegeekcentaur
Kludex
comments.body: What do you mean late? If you use gunicorn, you are using that worker anyway...

comments.author: thegeekcentaur
comments.body: I am talking about over-all testing and regression risks, purely from internal process perspectives.

comments.author: sandys
Kludex
comments.body:
```python
import threading

from uvicorn.workers import UvicornWorker

class CustomWorker(UvicornWorker):
    def _install_sigquit_handler(self, server: Server) -> None:
        """Workaround to install a SIGQUIT handler on workers.
        Ref.:
        - https://github.com/encode/uvicorn/issues/1116
        - https://github.com/benoitc/gunicorn/issues/2604
        """
        if threading.current_thread() is not threading.main_thread():
            # Signals can only be listened to from the main thread.
            return

        loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
        loop.add_signal_handler(signal.SIGQUIT, self.handle_exit, signal.SIGQUIT, None)

    async def _serve(self) -> None:
        self.config.app = self.wsgi
        server = Server(config=self.config)
        self._install_sigquit_handler(server)
        await server.serve(sockets=self.sockets)
        if not server.started:
            sys.exit(Arbiter.WORKER_BOOT_ERROR)
```
Something like... I haven't tested.

issues.title: Update Middleware Documentation to use Starlette's way of instantiating middleware
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
app.add_middleware(Middleware1)
app.add_middleware(Middleware2)
```


### Description

I noticed that my middleware was running multiple times, and after some extensive Googleing and reading Starlette and FastAPI docs, I came across [this issue](https://github.com/encode/starlette/discussions/1161) in the Starlette repo.

The general issue is that when you call `add_middleware()` multiple times, it results in re-initializing the middleware each time. While it's not well documented in Starlette, the authors left a comment in the [source code](https://github.com/encode/starlette/blob/1225ae8404ed0ae0b9170fde1cf824ec3f9c0f2f/starlette/applications.py#L126) that `add_middleware()` is not preferred, and instead should be handled during configuration. Presently, FastAPI makes this possible, because you're able to do `FastAP(middleware=[MyCustomMiddleware])`.

However, in the FastAPI documentation, `app.add_middleware()` is what is shown. I think this can cause some issues with middleware usage in FastAPI, and it would help to update the docs and point folks towards the Starlette way of instantiating middleware (i.e. passing a list of middleware to your FastAPI/Starlette instance at initialization).

### Operating System

macOS

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.72.0

### Python Version

3.9.12

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body: I would agree, we should at least make sure the documentation uses the recommended approach.

comments.author: gareth-leake
comments.body:
I know FastAPI also has a decorator approach `app.add_middleware("http")` -- I didn't see this approach in the Starlette docs either, so I'm not sure if this is a custom FastAPI approach. But that method _also_ causes multiple instantiations of middleware.

Perhaps this isn't the case if you only call it one time and have one app. But if (1) you have multiple sub-apps mounted onto a base app, and you want middleware in all of them, or (2) you have more than one piece of middleware, both the decorator approach and `app.add_middleware()` approach cause multiple instantiations.

My own solution that worked well was to create a `middleware.py` file, and write all my middleware as Class instances, following the patterns found in Starlette's code.

Example:
```python
# middleware.py

import uuid
import time
import logging
from typing import Callable
from fastapi import Request
from starlette.middleware import Middleware
from starlette.middleware.base import BaseHTTPMiddleware


class RequestIDMiddleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware):
    async def dispatch(self, request: Request, call_next: Callable):
        request.state.uuid = uuid.uuid4().hex
        start_time = time.time()
        response = await call_next(request)
        process_time = time.time() - start_time
        response.headers["X-Process-Time"] = str(process_time)
        logger.debug(f"LATENCY {process_time}s {request.method} {request.url}")
        return response

custom_middleware = [Middleware(RequestIDMiddleware)]
```

```python
#main.py
from .middleware import custom_middleware

app = FastAPI(middleware=custom_middleware)
```

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body:
The `app.middleware("http")` decorator is indeed syntax sugar from Starlette (not FastAPI) where the decorated function is treated as the `dispatch` method of a new `BaseHTTPMiddleware` class. It is also discouraged to use in their source code:

See here:
https://github.com/encode/starlette/blob/1225ae8404ed0ae0b9170fde1cf824ec3f9c0f2f/starlette/applications.py#L262-L283

Also, @gareth-leake, be aware that there are discussions going on on deprecating BaseHTTPMiddleware. It's usage is discouraged as well, because it is fundamentally broken. It will not play nicely with contextVars. Recommendation is to rewrite as "pure ASGI" middleware.

comments.author: gareth-leake
comments.body: Ah, yeah that totally makes sense. It's still just calling `add_middleware`. Thanks for pointing that out!

comments.author: Kludex
comments.body:
The ~`add_middleware`~ `middleware("http")` decorator is discouraged on Starlette, but not on FastAPI. If removed from Starlette, FastAPI will probably implement the decorators anyway...

The reason for being discouraged is not the "issue" about being initialized multiple times. It's because of the decorator style that Starlette doesn't want to maintain.

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body:
> The `add_middleware` is discouraged on Starlette, not on FastAPI.

AFAICT there is no `add_middleware()` method in FastAPI, it inherits it from Starlette directly. I also didn't know that the `Starlette.add_middleware()` itself was discouraged, the source code only discourages the use of the decorator style.

comments.author: Kludex
comments.body: Sorry, I meant `middleware("http")` was discouraged... I've fixed the above. 😅

comments.author: gareth-leake
comments.body: The decorator style ultimately implements the `add_middleware` function, which is discouraged by Starlette [here](https://github.com/encode/starlette/blob/1225ae8404ed0ae0b9170fde1cf824ec3f9c0f2f/starlette/applications.py#L126). Unfortunately the issues around middleware are not well documented. Perhaps this should be something I bring up with Starlette instead. The difficulty is that `add_middleware` and the decorator method both work, _but_ they end up creating issues if called multiple times. However, there's no clear indication in the documentation that these things should only be called once.

from typing import Union
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, root_validator, parse_obj_as, ValidationError
router = APIRouter(tags=["http"])
from ..test.data import CLIENTS, TARGETS
class Client(BaseModel):

### Description

 Why if I delete arg in clients func item: list[Client] and pydantic model invalid it got500 error
 If i added item: list[Client] then I got pretty json error in browser?
 I think args dont related with output response.
I think best way use like 
@router.get("/api/clients/")
def clients() -> list[Client]:
...
instead
@router.get("/api/clients/", response_model=list[Client])
def clients(item: list[Client]):

0.68.2

3.9.1

_No response_

comments.author: shurshilov
zoliknemet
comments.body:
@shurshilov 
Few comments on your code:
1. HTTP GET is not suppose to have request body - your method should be like this: 
```python
@router.get("/api/clients/", response_model=list[Client])
def clients():
    return CLIENTS
```

2. when `response_model` is defined it always tries to instantiate it - in this case it will take CLIENTS and make object of type List[Client] even if returned CLIENTS is dict it will work

3. You get 500 because your Client takes ID as INTEGER - your data says
```
CLIENTS = [{"id": "1a", "short_name_loc": "Demo", "long_name_loc": "DEMO"}]
```
here in dict, ID is String and can not map it to Client - ValidationError is raised

4. In second comment you returned DICTIONARY and response_model is STRING - you have to have good types declared and returned

If I understood correctly you have issue with 500 error instead you expect 400 to be returned.
I would say that 500 is valid in this case, it is internal error, code has bugs.
400 is used if user called your API with wrong parameters/data - 400 Bad Request

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from bson import ObjectId
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field


class PyObjectId(ObjectId):
    @classmethod
    def __get_validators__(cls):
        yield cls.validate

    @classmethod
    def validate(cls, v):
        if not ObjectId.is_valid(v):
            raise ValueError("Invalid ObjectId")
        return ObjectId(v)
    @classmethod
    def __modify_schema__(cls, field_schema):
        field_schema.update(type="string")


class MyBaseModel(BaseModel):
    pass

    class Config:
        json_encoders = {
            ObjectId: lambda oid: str(oid)
        }


class Model2(MyBaseModel):
    a: PyObjectId


class Model1(MyBaseModel):
    a: str = Field(example="Foo")
    b: Model2 = Field(example=Model2(a="62960507a3f31888572152e2"))


app = FastAPI()


@app.get("/", response_model=Model1)
def read_root():
    model2 = Model2(
        a="62960507a3f31888572152e2"
    )
    model1 = Model1(
        a="Fii",
        b=model2
    )
    return model1
```

  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/fastapi/encoders.py", line 101, in jsonable_encoder
    encoded_value = jsonable_encoder(
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/fastapi/encoders.py", line 101, in jsonable_encoder
    encoded_value = jsonable_encoder(
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/fastapi/encoders.py", line 101, in jsonable_encoder
    encoded_value = jsonable_encoder(
  [Previous line repeated 4 more times]
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/fastapi/encoders.py", line 144, in jsonable_encoder
    raise ValueError(errors)
ValueError: [TypeError("'ObjectId' object is not iterable"), TypeError('vars() argument must have __dict__ attribute')]
```

To fix this issue, simply remove the `Field(example=Model2(a="62960507a3f31888572152e2"))` text in the example code.

### Operating System

macOS

### Operating System Details

macOS 12.2.1

### FastAPI Version

0.75.2

### Python Version

3.9.12

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body: The `example` parameter only takes simple values or dicts (or something that can be construed as a dict). That is because, the `openapi.json` is basically just one going JSON document that describes your API and does not do 'in-depth' decoding. The `json_encoders` you defined are only used when serialising a response. So, it doesn't magically create an example based on your `BaseModel`, it just parses it as JSON for the purpose of putting it into the `openapi.json`. This is not a bug. Also, you can't fix this by adding a `__dict__` method to your `PyObjectId` class. That is because you use that object in a model (model2), that is nested in another model (model1). The example that you are giving is the request example of `Model2`, and it doesn't make sense to define that in `PyObjectId`. If you were to use `Model1` in a request parameter, you also needed to demonstrate that the input is like `"a": "62960507a3f31888572152e2"` and not bother the user with the type of object (as they cannot post objects, only json). So, for your use case above, the only thing to make sense is to add an example as follows: ```python class Model1(MyBaseModel): a: str = Field(example="Foo") b: Model2 = Field(example={"a":"62960507a3f31888572152e2"}) ``` which results in: When putting such a model in a request param, it then would look like this: ```python @app.get("/path") def read_path(input: Model1): return input ``` results in (Note: it looks the same but it defines the request **body**): Hope this clarifies for you :)

I followed your advice and it works well with the example in dict type. Thank you!

issues.title: MP4 Files cause mixed content - website not secure
issues.body:
Hi all,

I need some advice for a web service I deployed using MS Azure. Firstly, I have deployed this with a SSL certificate and https has been configured in Azure. In fact it all works fine if I remove the video from the webpage.

When I remove the MP4 file from my app, the website is secure, and there is no 'this website is not secure' warning. Bare in mind that I also have images stored in the same folder as the video, and they load no problem without any security warning.

However when I include the mp4, I get  website is not secure and the following information from the developer (f12) information:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/44236859/172398397-a609c83f-6296-4d44-afe9-d7a6950ca21d.png)

What I find really strange is that the way I link to the files in my app is the same for both images and videos as seen below:
```
<meta http-equiv="Content-Security-Policy" content="upgrade-insecure-requests"> # this is inside the <head> tag

#images srv vs video src:
## image (http secure)
src="{{ url_for('static', path= '\home_page\\assets\\img\\profile_pic.jpg')}}"

## video (causes https not secure)
src="{{ url_for('static', path= '\home_page\\assets\\img\\cube_race.mp4')}}" 

```
I already tried changing the path to https://website-name.co.uk/path-to-video, but it ended up doing this when deployed instead
http://website-name.co.uk/https://path-I-entered-here, it seems whatever path I provide will be attached to the end of http://website-name regardless.


Please can someone advise how to secure the video so I can have a fully secure website without warnings for my users.

_Originally posted by @VirajVaitha123 in https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/discussions/4995_

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body: What kind of service are you using in Azure? The problem most likely lies within there, but cannot be sure. I see a lot of hits looking for “azure web app mp4”, all pertaining to ISS config for certain mime types..

comments.author: VirajVaitha123
comments.body:
MS Azure is just what i've used to deploy the website, I don't think the problem is related to Azure.

As mentioned in the post, if I remove the MP4 files, the HTTPS works fine.

It's something to do with how to reference static files using http(s) with FastAPI.

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body: Or, it has something to do with how your Azure service is running your application. But if you are sure it has nothing to do with anything else than FastAPI, the I don’t know what could cause this.

comments.author: VirajVaitha123
comments.body:
Hi,

Just for future people facing the same issue, I resolved the issue by uploading the videos to a Azure Blob Storage, and viewing the mp4 content with the link to the image in a container of that Azure Blob Storage. I believe this extra step helped understand somewhere that the content is SAFE, enabling HTTPS. 

Many Thanks,

Viraj
