issues.title: Registering a common error model for Swagger
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI()


@app.get("/mangos")
async def root():
    raise Exception("We have no mangos")

@app.get("/bananas")
async def root():
    raise Exception("We have no bananas")
```


### Description

My situations:
 - I have a standard error response model (let's say ErrorResponseV1) for all error status code responses in RestAPI application
 - Being more details, having custom middleware I'm able to map all exceptions including built-in validation and not found exceptions on the previously mentioned ErrorResponseV1 format
 - Now I would like to use swagger UI. therefore it would be great to have a mechanism to let FastApi know that in case of 404, 409, 400 responses from server we use ErrorResponseV1 model
 
However by default:
- FastApi has no idea that my middleware handling all exceptions and return ErrorResponseV1 
- FastApi still annotates some endpoints with ibuilt-in error models formats which is misleading. E.g. sometimes FastApi says that an endpoint is able to return 422 response with ValidationError model
- Plus at the bottom of Swagger UI page there are some built-in error response models like HTTPValidationError and previously mentioned ValidationError 

I don't want to manually craft Swagger schema from 0, and I even like when FastApi tries to predict possible response statuses based on endpoint parameters.
I tried to use the "responses" parameter in FastApi app constructor or when I'm adding a router, but firstly it does not work with a Type parameter making things complicated.
I tried to pass a schema getting it via Pydantic schema() method since my error model inherits BaseModel, but it looks like it doesn't work and requires manual crafting and adjusting.

Even when it's done with manual schema writing and passing it via "responses", FastApi applies all registered responses on all endpoints.
E.g. In case of usage:
`FastAPI(responses = {422 : {...schema...}})`
FastApi will add this type of response to all endpoints in the app.

Therefore I just want to know whether I can let FastApi know which model to expect as a default one in case of errors. It could one for all errors or per Http Error code type. And expected output is straightforward:
- FastApi still tries to predict possible http responses but using my model
- There are no built-in error response models in swagger (bth, this one I can deal with, can just delete these models from generated schema knowing that no endpoint documentation in swagger will refer to it anymore)

Many thanks :)

### Operating System

Linux

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.75.2

### Python Version

3.9.5

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: zoliknemet

issues.title: Better way to handle multiple custom middleware?
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
class TimingMiddleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware):
    async def dispatch(self, request: Request, call_next: RequestResponseEndpoint) -> Response:
        begin = time.time()
        response = await call_next(request)
        response_time = time.time() - begin
        response.headers["X-Response-Time"] = str(response_time)
        return response


class LoggingMiddleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware):
    async def dispatch(self, request: Request, call_next: RequestResponseEndpoint) -> Response:
        logger.info({
            'message': 'request log',
            'request': request,
            'method': request.method,
            'url': request.url
        })

        response = await call_next(request)

        logger.info({
            'message': 'response log',
            'response': response,
            'status': response.status_code,
            'response_time': response.headers.get('X-Response-Time')
        })

        return response


class RequestContextMiddleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware):
    async def dispatch(self, request: Request, call_next: RequestResponseEndpoint) -> Response:
        correlation_id_token = _correlation_id_ctx_var.set(request.headers.get('X-Correlation-ID', str(uuid4())))

        try:
            response = await call_next(request)
            response.headers['X-Correlation-ID'] = get_correlation_id()
        except Exception as err:
            raise err
        finally:
            _correlation_id_ctx_var.reset(correlation_id_token)

        return response



app.add_middleware(middleware.TimingMiddleware)
app.add_middleware(middleware.LoggingMiddleware)
app.add_middleware(middleware.RequestContextMiddleware)
```


### Description

I have a FastAPI application that uses multiple custom middleware classes.  Currently, I have created each middleware as its own class, and then add the middleware to the app with "add_middleware" method.

However, because of the nature of the middleware, the order in which the middleware get added is important.  Is there a better or cleaner way to be creating and registering these middleware?  Should I just be creating one main middleware class, that contains all the functionality, or is better to split them up like I have them?  I dont like the fact that the order in which each one is added to the app matters. 


### Operating System

macOS

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.75.2

### Python Version

Python 3.9.12

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body:
I understand that the ordering of Middleware classes is not really something that is desired. However, from a maintainability and reusability point of view I think splitting them up is the right approach. 

When you would combine them in one big blob of logic, it would be very hard to (unit) test.

An exception to the above would be where the ordering of code prior `call_next()` should be different than the code after `call_next()`. For example, your code now does this:
```
request -> 
    (1)start timer ->
        (2)log request ->
            (3)set correlation id ->
                    respons ->
            (3)add correlation id to response header ->
        (2)log response ->
    (1)add time header ->
response ->
```
However, if you want to log the response with the timer header already set, you would need to change that around:
```
request -> 
    (1)start timer ->
        (2)log request ->
            (3)set correlation id ->
                    respons ->
            (3)add correlation id to response header ->
        (1)Ladd time header ->
    (2)log response->
response ->
```

In such a case, I can imagine that you combine where it makes sense. However, I would still recommend to make your code as maintainable and reusable as possible. The way you have structured it now would allow your colleagues to reuse bits and pieces rather than to untangle complicated ordering logic. 

Hope this helps!

comments.author: adriangb
comments.body:
First off, I **highly recommend you do not use `BaseHTTPMiddleware`**: https://www.starlette.io/middleware/#basehttpmiddleware

Second, what I would do in your case is pre-compose this middlewares.
First off, I'm assuming this is some sort of shared library.
If this is a single app, I would suggest you add a comment explaining that the order matters please don't touch it, a test _if you really want to_ and otherwise go have a 🍺 , this is not something that would concern me in a code review.

Assuming this is some sort of library or you really want to enforce order, there's plenty of ways to do that without creating a single huge middleware.
Since none of your middleware have constructor parameters, this should be pretty straightforward.

## Option 1: export a list of middlewares

middleware.py
```python
from starlette.middleware import Middleware

my_pre_ordered_middlewares = [Middleware(TimingMiddleware), Middleware(LoggingMiddleware)]`

...

# in the application code
app = FastAPI(middleware=my_pre_ordered_middleware)
```

## Option 2: a hook

```python
def add_my_middleware(app: FastAPI) -> None:
    app.add_middleware(...)
    app.add_middleware(...)
```

## Option 3: a meta-middleware

```python
class MetaMiddleware:

    def __init__(self, app: FastAPI) -> None:
        self._app = TimingMiddleware(LoggingMiddleware(app))

   async def __call__(self, scope, receive, send):
        await self._app(scope, receive, send)
```

You could make this some sort of fancy factory, but I'd keep it simple.

issues.title: Model with a root validator in `Depends` leads to 500 error instead of 422
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from typing import Optional

from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.param_functions import Depends
from pydantic import BaseModel
from pydantic.class_validators import root_validator

app = FastAPI()


class Options(BaseModel):
    a: Optional[int]
    b: Optional[int]

    @root_validator()
    def at_least_one_option(cls, values):
        assert any(
            opt is not None for opt in values.values()
        ), "At least one option should be enabled."
        return values


@app.get("/")
def read_root(options: Options = Depends(Options)):
    return options
```


### Description

If the assertion in the `root_validator` fails, the validation error that is produced is not caught correctly. The result is a 500 error to the client, and a traceback showing the validation error on the server-side. The expected result would be a 422 response, similar to how any other validation error is handled.

Reproduce as follows:

* Save the above snippet to a file, say `main.py`
* Run `uvicorn main:app` or some other way of running the app.
* In a separate terminal, run `curl -X 'GET' '127.0.0.1:8000/?a=str' -H 'accept: application/json'`
  * This produces a 422 response as expected.
* Now run `curl -X 'GET' '127.0.0.1:8000/' -H 'accept: application/json'`
  * This produces a 500 error
  * Meanwhile, the other terminal will show an error traceback ending in a validation error.

I believe this is caused by the error being triggered during dependency resolution, which appears to be outside the scope of the error handler that catches validation errors.


### Operating System

Linux

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.78.0

### Python Version

3.9.1

### Additional Context

I'm doing this because it allows defining query parameters with a pydantic model, which allows me to reuse this model as part of other models. Based on https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/dependencies/classes-as-dependencies/

comments.author: unights
comments.body:
Try this.
```python
from typing import Optional

from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from fastapi.param_functions import Depends
from pydantic import BaseModel
from pydantic.class_validators import root_validator

app = FastAPI()


class Options(BaseModel):
    a: Optional[int]
    b: Optional[int]

    @root_validator()
    def at_least_one_option(cls, values):
        if all(opt is None for opt in values.values()):
            raise HTTPException(422, "At least one option should be enabled.")
        # assert any(
        #     opt is not None for opt in values.values()
        # ), "At least one option should be enabled."
        return values


@app.get("/")
def read_root(options: Options = Depends(Options)):
    return options
```

comments.author: unights
comments.body:
Or, if you want to catch all `AssertionError` and return a 422 response, it might help you.
```python
from typing import Optional

from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI, Request
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()


@app.exception_handler(AssertionError)
async def assertion_exception_handler(request: Request, exc: AssertionError):
    return JSONResponse(status_code=422, content={'detail': str(exc)})


class Options(BaseModel):
    a: Optional[int]
    b: Optional[int]


@app.get("/")
def read_root(options: Options = Depends(Options)):
    assert any(opt is not None for opt in options.dict().values()), "At least one option should be enabled."
    return options
```

comments.author: bsamseth
comments.body:
@Lin-Your Thank's for the suggestions. To work around this I instead added a normal pydantic validator to the last field in the model, and did the assertion there. That way I can still rely on the built-in error handling and formatting of validation errors.

I didn't make this issue because I need a workaround, however, and rather because this looks like a straight-up bug. While one can certainly do other things to achieve similar results, I still think this should be fixed.

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body:
I agree, it is a straight up bug. I played around a bit and it seems that somehow Pydantic is throwing an `ValidationError` that isn't handled by Fastapi. Normally, any `ValidationError` that is thrown by Pydantic is caught and raised as an `RequestValidationError` by Fastapi, which is then by default handled by `request_validation_exception_handler` (returning a 422 response). I don't see how to solve this though. 

To satisfy my curiosity; you said:

> I instead added a normal pydantic validator to the last field in the model, and did the assertion there. 

Could you show me how? I tried to reproduce this but I got the same behaviour with `@validator`. Only when I did something like `a : Optional[int] = Field(..., gt=10)` I was able to get a 'real' 422 (when providing an a<10).

comments.author: JarroVGIT
comments.body: Just wanted to let you know that this is a known issue, and a long standing feature request. See this issue for workarounds and details: #1474

FROM "user"
WHERE "user".user_id = **%(user_id_1)s**

I am printing my query in the put method so that I can check whether the parameters are being matched or not but in the where clause why is the "%" coming before the user_id and why _1 is appended to it ? 



### Operating System

Windows

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.78.0

### Python Version

3.10.2

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: slackner
comments.body:
The `%(user_id_1)s` part of your SQL query looks correct to me. This is a just a placeholder, when executing the query SQLAlchemy will insert the corresponding values or pass them separately to the database.

When looking at the code, first thing I noticed is that `id` does not have any type in some API endpoints. This means you will get the id as a string (e.g. in `destroy` and `get_user`), and your database will probably not know how to deal with the string - integer comparison.

In addition, the `update` method passes a pydantic object to SQLAlchemy in `usr.update(request)`. I suspect it should be something like `usr.update(request.dict())`, since the underlying [update](https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/14/orm/query.html#sqlalchemy.orm.Query.update) method expects a dictionary.

Do these hints solve your issue? If not, please provide a full Python backtrace of the exception you are encountering.

issues.title: jsonable_encoder alters json_encoders of Pydantic objects
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, SecretStr
from fastapi.encoders import jsonable_encoder


class Credentials(BaseModel):
    password: SecretStr


ENCODERS = {SecretStr: lambda v: v.get_secret_value() if v is not None else None}

creds = Credentials(password="helloworld")

a = jsonable_encoder(creds)
print(a)  # {'password': '**********'}, as expected
b = jsonable_encoder(creds, custom_encoder=ENCODERS)
print(b)  # {'password': 'helloworld'}, as expected
c = jsonable_encoder(creds)
print(c)  # gives {'password': 'helloworld'}, but should be {'password': '**********'}?


creds = Credentials(password="123456789")

d = jsonable_encoder(creds)
print(d)  # gives {'password': '123456789'}, but should be {'password': '**********'}?
```


### Description

* I'm using `jsonable_encoder` at multiple locations in the code, sometimes with `custom_encoder` and sometimes without.
* My understanding is that passing `custom_encoder` should only affect the current call and not modify any global state.
* What actually happens is that it modifies `__config__.json_encoders`, and as a result, public API endpoints might suddenly start returning secret values that should be filtered.


### Operating System

Linux

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.78.0

### Python Version

Python 3.9.2

### Additional Context

Using Pydantic version 1.9.1.

comments.author: xingdongzhe
comments.body: I see that it use `__config__.json_encoders` and also pass to next call with `encoder`, so could we use `encoder = encoder.copy()` to avoid  modifies `__config__.json_encoders`?

comments.author: slackner
comments.body:
Yes, copying `encoder` before modifying it fixes the issue. Since FastAPI requires at least Python 3.6 merging the dictionaries could also be done with `{**a, **b}`, so an alternative fix would look like this:

```diff
diff --git a/fastapi/encoders.py b/fastapi/encoders.py
index 4b7ffe31..39cc2925 100644
--- a/fastapi/encoders.py
+++ b/fastapi/encoders.py
@@ -52,7 +52,7 @@ def jsonable_encoder(
     if isinstance(obj, BaseModel):
         encoder = getattr(obj.__config__, "json_encoders", {})
         if custom_encoder:
-            encoder.update(custom_encoder)
+            encoder = {**encoder, **custom_encoder}
         obj_dict = obj.dict(
             include=include,  # type: ignore # in Pydantic
             exclude=exclude,  # type: ignore # in Pydantic
```

Note that I'm not sure yet if the change has any negative side effects. Would be good to know to know if there was a reason why `.update()` was used in the first place. :thinking:

comments.author: slackner
comments.body: For reference, the code in question was initially added in https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/715. There is no explanation why the pydantic config itself is updated, so it probably was meant to be a copy in the first place.

issues.title: Uploading file with security dependency waits for full file to be loaded prior to authentication?
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [x] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
# main.py (start up application with this)
from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI, HTTPException, status, File, UploadFile
from fastapi.security import HTTPBasic, HTTPBasicCredentials
import secrets

app = FastAPI()
security = HTTPBasic()

def get_current_username(credentials: HTTPBasicCredentials = Depends(security)):
    correct_username = secrets.compare_digest(credentials.username, "stanleyjobson")
    correct_password = secrets.compare_digest(credentials.password, "swordfish")
    if not (correct_username and correct_password):
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,
            detail="Incorrect email or password",
            headers={"WWW-Authenticate": "Basic"},
        )
    return credentials.username

@app.post("/uploadfile/")
async def create_upload_file(file: UploadFile = File(...), username: str = Depends(get_current_username)):
    return {"filename": file.filename}


# uploadFile.py (to be put into separate file and run separately)
import requests
from requests.auth import HTTPBasicAuth
import time
import os

badAuth = HTTPBasicAuth(username="badstanley", password="badfish")
goodAuth = HTTPBasicAuth(username="stanleyjobson", password="swordfish")

smallFile = "smallFile.txt"
bigFile = "bigFile.txt"

if not os.path.exists(smallFile):
    with open(smallFile, "wb") as ofh:
        ofh.write(os.urandom(262144))  # generate 256 kb file

if not os.path.exists(bigFile):
    with open(bigFile, "wb") as ofh:
        ofh.write(os.urandom(268435456))  # generate 256 mb file

for file in [smallFile, bigFile]:
    print(file)
    for auth in [badAuth, goodAuth]:
        files = {"file": open(file, 'rb')}
        startTime = time.time()
        res = requests.post("http://0.0.0.0:8000/uploadfile", files=files, auth=auth)
        print(res.text, f"took {time.time() - startTime:.4f} seconds")
```


### Description
***Note:*** Basically the same as https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/3165, but here I provide a full example and am curious if this is intended behavior or an opportunity for code improvement.

If I upload a file using an upload endpoint that depends on authentication, it seems that the full file is uploaded prior to checking whether the provided credentials are valid. E.g., if you start up the application below (`main.py`) and run the provided `uploadFile.py` script, you'll see that the time it takes to deny the large file upload request is significantly longer than the time to deny the small file upload request. To me, this indicates that the entire file is attempted to be read-in by the application prior to checking for authentication.

So I guess my question is three-fold:
1. Is this expected behavior?
2. Wouldn't it be more efficient to check authentication before spending the time reading in the entire upload file request
3. How would I implement a way to perform the upload in such a way? Using a middleware endpoint?

Thank you!

### Operating System

macOS

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.78.0

### Python Version

4.9.7

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: adriangb
comments.body:
I wouldn't say this is expected or unexpected: it's an implementation detail.

I think not always but generally it would be best do do auth first. You can't change this behavior. If you are receiving a single file you could just stream the bytes instead of using an UploadFile.

comments.author: piehld
comments.body: Thanks, I ended up using a middleware instead. Appreciate the response and advice.

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Thanks for the help @adriangb! 🙇 

And thanks for coming back to close the issue after finding a solution for your use case @piehld. 🍰 

> Sorry for the long delay! 🙈  I wanted to personally address each issue and they piled up through time, but now I'm checking each one in order.

comments.author: anubhav-sl
comments.body:
> Thanks, I ended up using a middleware instead. Appreciate the response and advice.

What are you using ?

issues.title: How to do GET request query parameter database validation which depends on multiple other query params.
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.

- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
import datetime

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/clinic/available/doctors/list/")
def get_supplier_available_experts_list_on_clinic(clinic_id: str, supplier_id: str, date: datetime.date):
    """Return the list of the experts for a given supplier to a given clinic on a specific date."""
    return {"experts": []}
```


### Description

On my above code you can see the function **get_supplier_available_experts_list_on_clinic()** takes three URL parameters. One of them is **clinic_id**, one is **supplier_id** and other is a valid **date**. The function does some internal calculations to generated the list of available experts for a specific supplier to a specific clinic on a given date.

The problem is I want to validate the three parameters as follows:

- There must be a valid clinic on the Database for the inputted Clinic ID
- There must be a valid supplier on the Database for the inputted Supplier ID
- There must be a relation between the Clinic and the Supplier on the Database
- There must be a valid schedule for the Clinic and for the Supplier on the Database for the given Date

Currently, I can do these validations checkups on my internal scripts but I want to do it on the parameter level like using some **Dependencies** or other mechanisms.  Is it currently possible with the FastAPI? 

### Operating System

macOS

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.77.1

### Python Version

3.10.2

### Additional Context

Current Database: PostgreSQL 14

comments.author: acidjunk
comments.body:
You could probably use a pydantic validator: to do some heavy lifting.
https://pydantic-docs.helpmanual.io/usage/validators/

But considering that 2 of them need a DB (and a valid record) -> it would probably be good to raise 404 error's when the ID don't exist. And 422 when the relation doesn't exist?

So IMO: it also depends on how you want to design your API.

comments.author: aboubacs
saanpritom
comments.body: Thanks @aboubacs for the solution. I think this will work. I want to do it on the parameter level because i have multiple URL functions which required the same parameters. So, I have to validate the parameter each time. That's why I want a re usable clean solution.

comments.author: saanpritom
comments.body: Thanks @acidjunk for the reply. I have checked the documentation you sent. But I think the solution which @aboubacs provided is more suited to my problem.

Thanks for the help here everyone! 🍰 

I agree that using a dependency should do the trick. 🤓 

Thanks @saanpritom for coming back to close the issue once you found a solution, that's highly appreciated.

> Sorry for the long delay! 🙈  I wanted to personally address each issue and they piled up through time, but now I'm checking each one in order.

issues.title: Overwrite/extend FastAPI - APIRouter route
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
import uvicorn
from fastapi import APIRouter, FastAPI

router = APIRouter()


@router.get("/")
def index():
    return "hello world!"


@router.get("/")
def new_index():
    return "hello world 2!"


app = FastAPI()
app.include_router(router)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    uvicorn.run("server:app")
```


### Description

I copy here my thread in [StackOverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/72219039/how-to-overwrite-extend-fastapi-apirouter-route?noredirect=1#comment127596595_72219039)

So... I'm trying to create a simple pluggable FastAPI application where plugins can add, or not, API endpoints. Now is working fine so every plugin adds its own API endpoints. But what if a plugin can extend other plugins, to add or modify its endpoints...

For that, I build a plugin graph to handle the plugin dependencies and now a plugin can import the router from its dependencies and add some new endpoints. But I can´t overwrite the existing endpoints with the new routes.

I have also found an interesting thing in the generated API `/docs` that maybe can be a bug. The sample code generates this doc, which seems to be OK to me:

[![enter image description here][1]][1]

But... the endpoint returns the wrong response: 
At this point, I spect to have a `"hello world 2!"` response from `new_index` not a `"hello world!"` from `index`


Following a suggestion in the StackOverflow comment, I found this solution which I'm not happy with

```python
class Router(APIRouter):
    def add_api_route(...) -> None:
        ...

        route_index = self._get_route_index_by_path(route.path)
        if route_index >= 0:
            self.routes[route_index] = route
        else:
            self.routes.append(route)

    def _get_route_index_by_path(self, path: str) -> int:
        for index, route in enumerate(self.routes):
            if route.path == path:
                return index
        return -1


app = FastAPI()
router = Router()
....

```

I mean I'm not happy because I can´t find a good way to replace the route, since the `__eq__` to check if the route is in the list is on `starlette.routing.Route` so this was a simplest solution that I Found


  [1]: https://i.stack.imgur.com/033Fm.png

### Operating System

Linux

### Operating System Details

Fedora 35

### FastAPI Version

issues.title: How to use FastAPI Dependency Injection features in places other than path operation functions? (In logging config to be precise)
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
class CustomLoggerAdapter(logging.LoggerAdapter):
    """
        This overridden "process" method prepends the current
        user_id with all the log messages.
    """

    def process(self, msg, kwargs):
        from logic.auth.token_management import get_user_from_token
        from fastapi import Depends
        from data.models.users import Users
        def get_user(user: Users = Depends(get_user_from_token)):

            print(type(user))
            return user

        try:
            user = get_user()
        except:
            user = None

        return f'[user_id: {user.id if user else "null"}] {msg}', kwargs
```


### Description

* I am trying to add the currently logged-in `user_id` in all log messages. 

* I am using Python's `logging` library.

* I have created a `CustomLoggerAdapter` where the overridden `process` method would prepend the `user_id` in the log message. 

* I have to use FastAPI's DI system to get the current user.

* However, it seems FastAPI doesn't like its DI system to be used anywhere else other than the path-operation functions.

* So, is there any way I can use the DI system from the logging config-related codes?  If not then is there any you can think of to achieve this? 
 

 

 

### Operating System

Linux

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.70.0

### Python Version

3.9.10

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.body: That's not possible, `Depends` only works in FastAPI Endpoints

comments.body: I would give a chance to some DI libraries for such things. Dependency-injector works great with FatApi, but I guess there are other libraries

issues.title: How to pass a list of regex in allow_origin_regex in FastAPI CORS Middleware?
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
-
```


### Description

So, I have a list of very long and complicated regex which are all for my possible _origins_ for my FastAPI APP. I am using the CORS Middleware as given here: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/cors/#use-corsmiddleware (usage given below)

However,  **allow_origin_regex** argument takes only a _string_ as input
and *allow_origins* argument takes a list of strings as input which I would ideally want for **allow_origin_regex** as well.

So while I can do this:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware

app = FastAPI()

origins = [
    "http://localhost",
    "http://localhost:8080",
]

app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=origins,
    allow_credentials=True,
    allow_methods=["*"],
    allow_headers=["*"],
)

```

I can not do this:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware

app = FastAPI()

origins_regex = [
    "regex1",
    "regex2",
]

app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origin_regex=origins_regex,
    allow_credentials=True,
    allow_methods=["*"],
    allow_headers=["*"],
)

```

How can I achieve this functionality?


I inspected CORS code too in Starlette rep and it confirms about the arguments here: https://github.com/encode/starlette/blob/master/starlette/middleware/cors.py

Code snippet:
```python
class CORSMiddleware:
    def __init__(
        self,
        app: ASGIApp,
        allow_origins: typing.Sequence[str] = (),
        allow_methods: typing.Sequence[str] = ("GET",),
        allow_headers: typing.Sequence[str] = (),
        allow_credentials: bool = False,
        allow_origin_regex: typing.Optional[str] = None,
        expose_headers: typing.Sequence[str] = (),
        max_age: int = 600,
    ) -> None:
```


### Operating System

Linux

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.75.0

### Python Version

Python 3.6.8

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: aboubacs
comments.body: In your case, what's wrong with writing a regex that would match any of your origins ? (so in your example something like `"(regex1)|(regex2)"`)

comments.author: raghavsikaria
comments.body:
There's nothing wrong, infact, that is what I am doing right now. It's just that, that feature will be much more cleaner. That option **exists in Django framework** where I have sent all regex as list. I find combining all long complicated regex in one string as unreadable.

Is this even a valid painpoint/usecase ? If not, I'll close/delete the issue.

comments.author: aboubacs
comments.body: I don't know, but to find out, it would probably be better to submit the issue in starlette. The `CORSMiddleware` from `fastapi.middleware.cors` is just the class from starlette but packaged in another import path (https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/master/fastapi/middleware/cors.py), so the change to add the option to send a list of regex would only need to be done in starlette, not fastapi.

comments.author: sector119
comments.body:
Not a bug, but Item.schema() should be called with ref_template=REF_PREFIX + “{model}” when submodel types are used.

REF_PREFIX could be imported from fastapi.openapi.constants and it defaults to "#/components/schemas/"

That have to be mentioned in docs IMO

issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, Depends, APIRouter
from starlette_graphene3 import GraphQLApp, make_playground_handler
from shortest_path.schema import schema
from shortest_path.utils.security import get_api_key

graphql_app = GraphQLApp(
    schema=schema,
    on_get=make_playground_handler()
)


async def wrapper(req: Request) -> None:
    await graphql_app(req.scope, req.receive, req._send)

app = FastAPI()
router = APIRouter(tags=["graphql"])

router.add_api_route(
    "/graphql", wrapper, dependencies=[Depends(get_api_key)], include_in_schema=True, methods=["POST"]
)

router.add_api_route(
    "/graphql", wrapper, include_in_schema=True, methods=["GET"]
)

router.add_api_websocket_route("/graphql", graphql_app)
app.include_router(router)
```


### Description

Dependencies are as follows:
```
fastapi = "==0.75.2"
uvicorn = {version = "==0.17.6", extras = ["standard"]}
gunicorn = "==20.1.0"
```

Executing in Dockerfile with following entry point: 
```
CMD exec gunicorn --bind :8080 --workers 1 --worker-class uvicorn.workers.UvicornWorker --timeout 0 app.main:app
```

Calling my graphql endpoint doesn't result in any erroneous behaviour on the client side, but I end up with these logs.
```
[2022-05-03 05:49:08 +0000] [9] [ERROR] Exception in ASGI application
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/uvicorn/protocols/http/httptools_impl.py", line 372, in run_asgi
    result = await app(self.scope, self.receive, self.send)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/uvicorn/middleware/proxy_headers.py", line 75, in __call__
    return await self.app(scope, receive, send)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/fastapi/applications.py", line 261, in __call__
    await super().__call__(scope, receive, send)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/starlette/applications.py", line 112, in __call__
    await self.middleware_stack(scope, receive, send)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/starlette/middleware/errors.py", line 181, in __call__

  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/starlette/middleware/errors.py", line 159, in __call__

  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/starlette/exceptions.py", line 82, in __call__
    raise exc
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/starlette/exceptions.py", line 71, in __call__
    await self.app(scope, receive, sender)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/fastapi/middleware/asyncexitstack.py", line 21, in __call__
    raise e
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/fastapi/middleware/asyncexitstack.py", line 18, in __call__
    await self.app(scope, receive, send)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/starlette/routing.py", line 656, in __call__
    await route.handle(scope, receive, send)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/starlette/routing.py", line 259, in handle
    await self.app(scope, receive, send)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/starlette/routing.py", line 64, in app
    await response(scope, receive, send)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/starlette/responses.py", line 149, in __call__
    await send(
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/starlette/exceptions.py", line 68, in sender
    await send(message)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/starlette/middleware/errors.py", line 156, in _send

  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.8/site-packages/uvicorn/protocols/http/httptools_impl.py", line 519, in send
    raise RuntimeError(msg % message_type)
RuntimeError: Unexpected ASGI message 'http.response.start' sent, after response already completed.
```

I can't pinpoint the exact cause of these logs or why exactly the ASGI server is emitting a message after the response has been sent.

Linux

### Additional Context

- I have some I/O tasks in as part of my processing, e.g. where I do: 
```
    results = await asyncio.gather(*[run_query(query) for query in queries], return_exceptions=True)
```
- I also use starlette background tasks to perform after-response logging to other sinks, e.g.

```
    background.add_task(log_route_metrics, df, aisle_bay_pairs, optimal_route, totes)
```

comments.author: Kludex

comments.author: bitnahian
comments.body:
I've solved this issue with the following:

```
from fastapi import Request, Depends, APIRouter
from starlette.websockets import WebSocket
from starlette_graphene3 import GraphQLApp, make_playground_handler
from your_app.api.api_v1.endpoints.schema import schema
from your_app.core.security import get_api_key

graphql_app = GraphQLApp(
    schema=schema,
    on_get=make_playground_handler(playground_options={"subscriptionEndpoint": "/graphql-ws"})
)


async def wrapper(gql_req: Request):
    # return "Hello World"
    method  = gql_req.method
    if method == "GET":
        return await graphql_app._get_on_get(gql_req)
    elif method == "POST":
        return await graphql_app._handle_http_request(gql_req)

async def ws_wrapper(gql_ws_req: WebSocket) -> None:
    return await graphql_app(gql_ws_req.scope, gql_ws_req.receive, gql_ws_req._send)

router = APIRouter(tags=["graphql"])

router.add_api_route(
    "/graphql", 
    wrapper, 
    dependencies=[Depends(get_api_key)], 
    include_in_schema=True, 
    methods=["POST"],
    name='gql_req'
)

router.add_api_route(
    "/graphql", 
    wrapper, 
    include_in_schema=True, 
    methods=["GET"], 
    name='gql_req'
)

router.add_api_websocket_route(
    "/graphql-ws", 
    ws_wrapper, 
    name='gql_ws_req'
)

```

In starlette-graphene3, if you directly call the app like so `graphql_app(req.scope, req.receive, req._send)`, it'll hit this part of the code in the starlette-graphene3 library: 

```
 await response(scope, receive, send) # line 111 in starlette_graphene3.py
```

This calls the response object, and I believe fastapi tries to call the response object again in upstream call, which causes the error.

If you simply return the JSONResponseObject with ._handle_http_request() or the HTMLResponse with .get_on_get(), it won't await on response(scope, receive, send).

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Thanks for the help here @Kludex! And thanks for closing the issue after finding a solution @bitnahian. 🍰 

> Sorry for the long delay! 🙈  I wanted to personally address each issue and they piled up through time, but now I'm checking each one in order.

### Commit to Help

### Example Code

```python
from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI, HTTPException, status

...

client = TestClient(app)

def test_foo(token: str = Depends(oauth2_scheme)):
    response = client.get("/foo")
    assert response.status_code == 200
    assert response.json() == {"Available Endpoints": ENDPOINT_LIST}
```


### Description

I followed the instructions for [oath2 with password](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/security/oauth2-jwt/) setup.

My API calls all work and are structured like so:

```python
@app.get("/foo")
async def read_foo(
        token: str = Depends(oauth2_scheme),
):
    return foo()
```

when I try to run the tests **in a different folder and file**, the authentication fails, ie i get a 401 response.


```python
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient

client = TestClient(app)

def test_foo(token: str = Depends(oauth2_scheme)):
    response = client.get("/foo")
    assert response.status_code == 200
    assert response.json() == {"Available Endpoints": ENDPOINT_LIST}
```

Python 3.10.4

_No response_

comments.author: jgould22
comments.body:
Is your intention to run `test_foo` with pytest?

You cannot just toss a Depends in there and expect it to work. When you use `Depends` FastAPI knows what to do with it but pytest does not. To pytest you are just setting a default parameter. 

You have to authenticate with the test client as an api consumer would (sorta like [here](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/testing/?h=testing#extended-testing-file) ) or override the dependency like [here](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/testing-dependencies/?h=over)

comments.author: Razikus
comments.body:
Isn't it just beause requests.get is not jsonable?
You should return .json()

so
```
import requests
from fastapi.encoders import jsonable_encoder

r = requests.get('https://api.github.com/thisodesntexist')
jsoned = r.json()
jsonable_encoder(jsoned)
```

I don't know if this is an issue to encoder

comments.author: dg-ow
comments.body:
My reading of the `jsonable_encoder` documentation is that it should return something that can be encoded in JSON, and doesn't say anything about limitations.

https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/encoder/

comments.author: Razikus
comments.body:
https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/master/fastapi/encoders.py#L29

The limitations here are simple - Fastapi couldn't know about all objects and classes in the world
The idea about jsonable_encoder is that you should provide jsonable object

Response from Request isn't jsonable before you decide what type of response you want to have
https://docs.python-requests.org/en/latest/user/quickstart/#response-content

How Fastapi should interract with this?
Try to execute .json() command?

What if we will have another library that have toJson() command
Should it also try to execute toJson() method?

comments.author: dg-ow
comments.body:
I see nothing in the code you linked me to documenting limitations.  I attempts to encode objects, with what looks like a last ditch failsafe at the bottom of the method.  The tutorial I linked to says the jsonable_encoder can return an JSON encodable object.  It even references a non-JSON encodable object, `datetime`.

So yes, I expect jsonable_encoder to give me back something that can be JSON encoded.  Maybe your idea of a toJson method is the way to go.

comments.author: tomy0000000
comments.body:
If you looked into the [source code of `jsonable_encoder`](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/1876ebc77949a9a254909ec61ea0c09365169ec2/fastapi/encoders.py#L29), you'll find that it is basically an enhanced version of `json.dumps` with handling of `datetime`, `pydantic` models, `dataclass`, and some data structure that developer would "typically" return in the router function, no more and no less.

The error you saw, particularly
```
TypeError: 'property' object is not iterable
```
```
TypeError: vars() argument must have __dict__ attribute
```
these two, is actually `jsonable_encoder` try to guess how this object can be turned into json object

But the point is, it is not meant to support every type of object existed in python. Which is why you are the person responsible to call `r.json()` before returning it, since you know the object better than `jsonable_encoder`.

comments.author: grahamalama
comments.body:
> My reading of the `jsonable_encoder` documentation is that it should return something that can be encoded in JSON, and doesn't say anything about limitations.
> 
> https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/encoder/

> But the point is, it is not meant to support every type of object existed in python.

I wanted to chime in and say that I agree that the documentation here is a bit misleading. The documentation only says:

> [`jsonable_encoder`] receives an object, like a Pydantic model, and returns a JSON compatible version. 

It doesn't say:
> `jsonable_encoder` supports these object types `<...>`. If what you're trying to serialize isn't one of those types, it should have a `dict` method. Otherwise, `jsonable_encoder` will throw an error.

Or something along those lines. 

I _do_ get that it would be impossible to account for every type of Python object, but having those caveats in the documentation and maybe a more helpful error message would be useful.

issues.title: Exception handlers bypass middlewares
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
import logging
import sys
import time

import uvicorn
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
from fastapi.responses import Response

logger = logging.getLogger()
logger.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)

handler = logging.StreamHandler(sys.stdout)
handler.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
formatter = logging.Formatter("%(asctime)s - %(name)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s")
handler.setFormatter(formatter)
logger.addHandler(handler)

app = FastAPI()


@app.get("/")
def index():
    raise ValueError("Crash.")
    return "Test."


async def log_request(request: Request, call_next):
    try:
        response = await call_next(request)
    except Exception:
        logger.error("Middleware observed exception.")

    logger.info("Returning response.")
    return response


async def log_exception(request: Request, exc: Exception):
    logger.error(f"Encountered exception {exc}.")
    return Response(status_code=503, content="test")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.exception_handler(Exception)(log_exception)
    app.middleware("http")(log_request)
    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8000)
```


### Description

I have this use case where I want to log the duration of all requests, this includes requests which crash the route handler function. For this I introduced a request logging middleware. From my observations once a exception handler is triggered, all middlewares are bypassed.  So even if the exception handler returns an Response object there is no way to react to it in the middleware.

Is my understanding of this correct? And is this a bug or a feature?

(Based on the answer to do this I will submit an MR which adds this to the Middleware documentation)

### Operating System

Linux

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.70.1

### Python Version

Python 3.10.0

### Additional Context

_No response_

```INFO:     127.0.0.1:35798 - "GET / HTTP/1.1" 503 Service Unavailable
  File "/home/user/.local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/uvicorn/middleware/proxy_headers.py", line 75, in __call__

    return response

    raise exc

    await self.app(scope, receive, _send)
  File "/home/user/.local/lib/python3.10/site-packages/starlette/middleware/base.py", line 63, in __call__
    response = await self.dispatch_func(request, call_next)
  File "/home/user/code/test/gh_help/middleware_except/main.py", line 34, in log_request
    return response
UnboundLocalError: local variable 'response' referenced before assignment
2022-04-21 19:58:12,286 - root - INFO - Returning response.
```
Which you would expect since `call_next` is throwing the exception response is never set.

If I correct for the error by refactoring your function to 

```python
async def log_request(request: Request, call_next):
    response = None
    try:
        response = await call_next(request)
    except Exception:
        logger.error("Middleware observed exception.")
        response =  Response(status_code=200, content="Caught in the middleware")
    logger.info("Returning response.")
    return response
```
I get the expected 200 with the logged 
```
2022-04-21 20:05:12,086 - uvicorn.error - INFO - Uvicorn running on http://0.0.0.0:8000 (Press CTRL+C to quit)
2022-04-21 20:05:14,417 - root - ERROR - Middleware observed exception.
2022-04-21 20:05:14,418 - root - INFO - Returning response.
INFO:     127.0.0.1:35808 - "GET / HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
```

This is with `FastAPI 0.75.1` so my suggestion to you is to update FastAPI since ` 0.70.1`  uses `starlette 16` and there have been a number of fixes related to exception handling between 16 and 17

issues.body:

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from pathlib import Path
from typing import List

from fastapi import Body, Depends, FastAPI, HTTPException, Response
from fastapi.responses import FileResponse, HTMLResponse, JSONResponse, RedirectResponse
from sqlalchemy.orm import Session

from . import config, crud, models, schemas
from .database import SessionLocal, engine

models.Base.metadata.create_all(bind=engine)

app = FastAPI(title="Foo", description="Bar")

# Dependency
def get_db():
    db = SessionLocal()
    try:
        yield db
    finally:
        db.close()

@app.get("/favicon.ico", include_in_schema=False)
async def favicon():
    return FileResponse(config.FAVICON_PATH)

@app.get("/items", response_model=List[schemas.MyModel], tags=["items"])
def get_all_items(skip: int = 0, limit: int = 100, db: Session = Depends(get_db)):
    items = crud.get_all_items(db, skip=skip, limit=limit)
    return items
```


### Description

I am following the excellent documentation of FastAPI itself to create this app (https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/sql-databases/)
I have a simple FastAPI which is deployed in my own server.
The app connects to a SQLite database which resides in the server itself.
After I start the application, in the app log, I continuously get the below message and I do not know how to stop it.

```
INFO:     Uvicorn running on http://0.0.0.0:8001 (Press CTRL+C to quit)
INFO:     Started reloader process [12097] using watchgod
INFO:     Started server process [12099]
INFO:     Waiting for application startup.
INFO:     Application startup complete.
INFO:     <ip>:0 - "GET /docs HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
INFO:     <ip>:0 - "GET /openapi.json HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
INFO:     <ip>:0 - "GET /api/v1/items?skip=0&limit=100 HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
INFO:     <ip>:0 - "GET /queue/worker HTTP/1.1" 404 Not Found
INFO:     <ip>:0 - "GET /queue/worker HTTP/1.1" 404 Not Found
INFO:     <ip>:0 - "GET /queue/worker HTTP/1.1" 404 Not Found
```

I suspect this is because I am connecting to SQLite DB which does not support async operations natively and the methods in my main.py start with **def foo** instead of **async def foo**

How do I get rid of this message? **"GET /queue/worker HTTP/1.1" 404 Not Found**
It is polluting my logs currently. Can I fix it or have a way to suppress it?

### Operating System

Linux

### Operating System Details

SLES12 SP5

### FastAPI Version

0.75.0

### Python Version

Python 3.7.4

### Additional Context

I am following the excellent documentation of FastAPI itself to create this app (https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/sql-databases/)
And I am connecting to a SQLite DB.

issues.title: How to update response schema in swagger for all endpoints after modifying response at middleware level in a FastAPI Application?
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Receive, Scope, Send, Message
from starlette.requests import Request
import json
from starlette.datastructures import MutableHeaders
from fastapi import FastAPI


class MetaDataAdderMiddleware:
    application_generic_urls = ['/openapi.json', '/docs', '/docs/oauth2-redirect', '/redoc']
    
    def __init__(
            self, 
            app: ASGIApp
    ) -> None:
        self.app = app

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        if scope["type"] == "http" and not any([scope["path"].startswith(endpoint) for endpoint in MetaDataAdderMiddleware.application_generic_urls]):
            responder = MetaDataAdderMiddlewareResponder(self.app, self.standard_meta_data, self.additional_custom_information)
            await responder(scope, receive, send)
            return
        await self.app(scope, receive, send)


class MetaDataAdderMiddlewareResponder:

    def __init__(
            self,
            app: ASGIApp,
    ) -> None:
        """
        """
        self.app = app
        self.initial_message: Message = {}

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        self.send = send
        await self.app(scope, receive, self.send_with_meta_response)

    async def send_with_meta_response(self, message: Message):

        message_type = message["type"]
        if message_type == "http.response.start":
            # Don't send the initial message until we've determined how to
            # modify the outgoing headers correctly.
            self.initial_message = message

        elif message_type == "http.response.body":
            response_body = json.loads(message["body"].decode())

            data = {}
            data["data"] = response_body
            data['metadata'] = {
                'field_1': 'value_1',
                'field_2': 'value_2'
            }

            data_to_be_sent_to_user = json.dumps(data, default=str).encode("utf-8")

            headers = MutableHeaders(raw=self.initial_message["headers"])
            headers["Content-Length"] = str(len(data_to_be_sent_to_user))
            message["body"] = data_to_be_sent_to_user

            await self.send(self.initial_message)
            await self.send(message)


app = FastAPI(
    title="MY DUMMY APP",
)

app.add_middleware(MetaDataAdderMiddleware)

@app.get("/")
async def root():
    return {"message": "Hello World"}
```


### Description

So, I have created a Custom Middleware for my big FastAPI Application, which alters responses from all of my endpoints this way:

Response model is different for all APIs. However, my MDW adds meta data to all of these responses, in an uniform manner. This is what the **final response object** looks like:

```json
{
    "data": <ANY RESPONSE MODEL THAT ALL THOSE ENDPOINTS ARE SENDING>,
    "meta_data": 
    {
        "meta_data_1": "meta_value_1",
        "meta_data_2": "meta_value_2",
        "meta_data_3": "meta_value_3",
    }
}
```

So essentially, all original responses, are wrapped inside a `data` field, a new field of `meta_data` is added with all meta_data. This `meta_data` model is uniform, it will always be of this type:
```json
    "meta_data": 
    {
        "meta_data_1": "meta_value_1",
        "meta_data_2": "meta_value_2",
        "meta_data_3": "meta_value_3",
    }
```
Now the problem is, when the swagger loads up, it shows the original response model in schema and not the final response model which has been prepared. How to alter swagger to reflect this correctly?
I have tried this:

```python
# This model is common to all endpoints!
# Since we are going to add this for all responses 
class MetaDataModel(BaseModel):
    meta_data_1: str
    meta_data_2: str
    meta_data_3: str

class FinalResponseForEndPoint1(BaseModel):
    data: OriginalResponseForEndpoint1
    meta_data: MetaDataModel

class FinalResponseForEndPoint2(BaseModel):
    data: OriginalResponseForEndpoint2
    meta_data: MetaDataModel

and so on ...
```

This approach does render the Swagger perfectly, but there are 2 major problems associated with it:

+ All my FastAPI endpoints break and give me an error when they are returning response. For example: my endpoint1 is still returning the original response but the endpoint1 expects it to send response adhering to `FinalResponseForEndPoint1` model
+ Doing this approach for all models for all my endpoints, does not seem like the right way



### Operating System

Linux

### Operating System Details

_No response_

### FastAPI Version

0.75.0

### Python Version

3.6.8

### Additional Context

_No response_

comments.author: jonatasoli
comments.body: Wouldn't this be the case to customize the output directly in the documentation instead of using the automatically generated documentation?

comments.author: rafsaf
comments.body:
Maybe you could just extend it yourself? I doubt FastAPI will be ever so smart to detect such things in middlewares.

Read: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/extending-openapi/

At the end of the day, you can think of generated openapi spec as a `dict`, you can generate the default output (the wrong one, actually), and process it in a way you want. This should be at least doable, but the question is an effort. But worth a try.

issues.title: How to modify Response coming from all endpoints and also adjust the content-length post modification of response in a middleware?
issues.body:
### First Check

- [X] I added a very descriptive title to this issue.
- [X] I used the GitHub search to find a similar issue and didn't find it.
- [X] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search.
- [X] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information.
- [X] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer.
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Pydantic](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [Swagger UI](https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui).
- [X] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to [ReDoc](https://github.com/Redocly/redoc).

### Commit to Help

- [X] I commit to help with one of those options 👆

### Example Code

```python
# Here is a minimal reproducible example

import json
from starlette.datastructures import MutableHeaders
from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Receive, Scope, Send, Message
import datetime
import socket
from fastapi import FastAPI

class MetaDataAdderMiddleware:
    application_generic_urls = ['/openapi.json', '/docs', '/docs/oauth2-redirect', '/redoc']
    
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp) -> None:
        self.app = app

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:

        start_time = datetime.datetime.utcnow()

        async def send_wrapper(message: Message) -> None:   
         
            if message["type"] == "http.response.body" and len(message["body"]) and not any([scope["path"].startswith(endpoint) for endpoint in MetaDataAdderMiddleware.application_generic_urls]):

                response_body = json.loads(message["body"].decode())

                end_time = datetime.datetime.utcnow()
                response_processing_time_seconds = end_time - start_time
                data = {}
                data["data"] = response_body
                data['metadata'] = {
                    'request_timestamp_utc': start_time,
                    'response_timestamp_utc': end_time,
                    'processing_time_seconds': response_processing_time_seconds,
                    'service_host': socket.gethostname()
                }

                data_to_be_sent_to_user = json.dumps(data, default=str).encode("utf-8")
                message["body"] = data_to_be_sent_to_user

            await send(message)
        await self.app(scope, receive, send_wrapper)


app = FastAPI(
    title="MY DUMMY APP",
)

app.add_middleware(MetaDataAdderMiddleware)

@app.get("/")
async def root():
    return {"message": "Hello World"}
```


### Description

So here is my usecase: All of my endpoints in FastAPI APP, whatever response they are sending, I need  to wrap that response, with some metadata. Let's say, some endpoint is sending me this: `{"data_key": "data_value"}`. But, the users should see, this as the final output:
```json
{
    "data": {"data_key": "data_value"}
     "metadata": {
          "request_timestamp_utc": "somevalue",
          ...and so on
     }
}
```

I have a big application, and numerous routers. We have achieved the functionality of adding Request ID, Authentication and Authorization, so far by writing middlewares.


However, when I hit APIs of my app, after adding the abovementioned MetaDataAdderMiddleware, I am greeted with this following error:

```txt
ERROR:    Exception in ASGI application
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<MY PYTHON PATH>/lib/python3.6/site-packages/uvicorn/protocols/http/httptools_impl.py", line 521, in send
    raise RuntimeError("Response content longer than Content-Length")
RuntimeError: Response content longer than Content-Length
```

This error is logical enough, since I have modified the Response body but not changed the content-length headers.

Here is snapshot of message and scope bodies in the send_wrapper function level, and as well as header values I have printed from the httptools_impl.py level:
(I have edited out some fields, to mask org-specific things)

```txt
send_wrapper called
message: {'type': 'http.response.start', 'status': 200, 'headers': [(b'content-length', b'58'), (b'content-type', b'application/json')]}
scope: {'type': 'http', 'asgi': {'version': '3.0', 'spec_version': '2.1'}, 'http_version': '1.1', 'scheme': 'http', 'method': 'POST', 'root_path': '', 'query_string': b'', 'headers': [(b'content-type', b'application/json'), (b'accept', b'*/*'), (b'cache-control', b'no-cache'), (b'accept-encoding', b'gzip, deflate'), (b'content-length', b'238'), (b'connection', b'keep-alive')], 'app': <fastapi.applications.FastAPI object at >, 'fastapi_astack': <contextlib2.AsyncExitStack object at >, 'router': <fastapi.routing.APIRouter object at >, 'endpoint': <function initiate_playbook_execution at >, 'path_params': {}, 'route': <fastapi.routing.APIRoute object at >}
INFO:     - "POST /MYAPI" 200 OK

INSIDE httptools_impl
name: b'content-length' | value: b'58'
self.expected_content_length: 58

send_wrapper called
message: {'type': 'http.response.body', 'body': b'{"status":true,"stdout":null,"stderr":null,"message":null}'}
scope: {'type': 'http', 'asgi': {'version': '3.0', 'spec_version': '2.1'}, 'http_version': '1.1', 'scheme': 'http', 'method': 'POST', 'root_path': '', 'query_string': b'', 'headers': [(b'content-type', b'application/json'), (b'accept', b'*/*'), (b'cache-control', b'no-cache'), (b'accept-encoding', b'gzip, deflate'), (b'content-length', b'238'), (b'connection', b'keep-alive')], 'app': <fastapi.applications.FastAPI object at >, 'fastapi_astack': <contextlib2.AsyncExitStack object at >, 'router': <fastapi.routing.APIRouter object at >, 'endpoint': <function initiate_playbook_execution at >, 'path_params': {}, 'route': <fastapi.routing.APIRoute object at >}

INSIDE httptools_impl
body: b'{"data": {"status": true, "stdout": null, "stderr": null, "message": null}, "metadata": {"request_timestamp_utc": "BLAH", "response_timestamp_utc": "BLAH", "processing_time_seconds": "0:00:00.469472", "some_field": "some_value"}}'
num_bytes: 286
```

Here are the attempts that I have made to update the content-length:

+ In the send wrapper function just after I update the response body, I have tried doing the following:
    ```python
    data_to_be_sent_to_user = json.dumps(data, default=str).encode("utf-8")
    message["body"] = data_to_be_sent_to_user
    headers = MutableHeaders(scope=scope)
    headers["content-length"] = str(len(data_to_be_sent_to_user))

    # But this hasn't worked, no change in situation!
    ```

How can I proceed forward?

Linux

_No response_

0.75.0

Python 3.6.8

_No response_

comments.author: vanishingESCkey
comments.body:
Don't know how to properly set the content-length but I can share a slightly different approach of including additional meta data to response. It works on router level, not middleware. I have seen similar questions few times before, would be good to have  some built-in feature in fastapi for that

https://gist.github.com/vanishingESCkey/fadce8ef487d936c972e4557bdd36297

comments.author: raghavsikaria
comments.body:
hey @vanishingESCkey, thanks for the quick help! This solution will work, but it seems like a cumbersome task to add this one-by-one for all API routers. I however got some help from StackOverflow, here in this question that I had raised: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/71764142/how-to-modify-the-content-length-post-modification-of-response-in-a-middleware-i 
and the user there, suggested me to have a look at Starlette's GZipMiddleware codebase here: https://github.com/encode/starlette/blob/fcc4c705ff69182ebd663bc686cb55c242d32683/starlette/middleware/gzip.py#L60

So the idea is, the problematic `content-length` value is in header present in `http.response.start` message. So, how GZipMiddleware has been written is, they have simply not sent this first `http.response.start` message instantly. Instead, they also capture `http.response.body`, then modify the response, then find its length, then update the length in `http.response.start` message, and then send both these messages in the correct order.

The working implementation that I was able to write, borrowing heavily from GZipMiddleware is here:
```python
from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Receive, Scope, Send, Message
from starlette.requests import Request
import json
from starlette.datastructures import MutableHeaders


class MetaDataAdderMiddleware:
    application_generic_urls = ['/openapi.json', '/docs', '/docs/oauth2-redirect', '/redoc']
    
    def __init__(
            self, 
            app: ASGIApp
    ) -> None:
        self.app = app

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        if scope["type"] == "http" and not any([scope["path"].startswith(endpoint) for endpoint in MetaDataAdderMiddleware.application_generic_urls]):
            responder = MetaDataAdderMiddlewareResponder(self.app, self.standard_meta_data, self.additional_custom_information)
            await responder(scope, receive, send)
            return
        await self.app(scope, receive, send)


class MetaDataAdderMiddlewareResponder:

    def __init__(
            self,
            app: ASGIApp,
    ) -> None:
        """
        """
        self.app = app
        self.initial_message: Message = {}

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        self.send = send
        await self.app(scope, receive, self.send_with_meta_response)

    async def send_with_meta_response(self, message: Message):

        message_type = message["type"]
        if message_type == "http.response.start":
            # Don't send the initial message until we've determined how to
            # modify the outgoing headers correctly.
            self.initial_message = message

        elif message_type == "http.response.body":
            response_body = json.loads(message["body"].decode())

            data = {}
            data["data"] = response_body
            data['metadata'] = {
                'field_1': 'value_1',
                'field_2': 'value_2'
            }

            data_to_be_sent_to_user = json.dumps(data, default=str).encode("utf-8")

            headers = MutableHeaders(raw=self.initial_message["headers"])
            headers["Content-Length"] = str(len(data_to_be_sent_to_user))
            message["body"] = data_to_be_sent_to_user

            await self.send(self.initial_message)
            await self.send(message)
```

### Example Code

```python
from dataclasses import dataclass
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI()

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Mod:
    foo: str


@app.get("/", response_model=Mod)
async def get_mod():
    return {"foo": "bar"}
```

### Workarounds:

1. remove `frozen=True`, or
2. `from pyndatic.dataclasses impotr dataclass` -- the pydantic version works.

It could be a bug in pydantic for all I know, or even a limitation, in which case `fastapi` docs need to be updated.

comments.author: jonatasoli
comments.body: In this case I think the response_model statement expect a pydantic object and not a dataclass.

comments.author: dimaqq
comments.body:
Note that `dataclasses.dataclass` can be used if they are not frozen.
My gut tells me that pydantic attempts to convert my dataclass into their kind in order to generate the schema and/or to validators, in which case this is not a FastAPI problem per se.

If this is indeed a limitation, let's note this in FastAPI docs 🙏🏿

comments.author: jonatasoli
comments.body: Why in your case, use a dataclass instead of a pydantic object?

comments.author: dimaqq
comments.body:
A couple of reasons, though perhaps neither is super important:
1. simplicity, if I can use stdlib dataclass, why not?
2. exporting data in a shape that's defined in a 3p package

comments.author: dimaqq
comments.body:
I wonder if https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/issues/3469 is related.
Conceivably, generating the OpenAPI schema for the entire API could use a BaseModel somewhere in pydantic 🤔

comments.author: dimaqq
comments.body: Or maybe this: https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/issues/2555

comments.author: dimaqq
comments.body: Indeed it is. All I'm proposing is for FastAPI to document this limitation for the time being.
